8|mathematics|1_Rational_Numbers.overview: Rational numbers include all integers and fractions of the form p/q where q ≠ 0. Class 8 extends number understanding to include negative fractions, their properties, and operations on them.

key_concepts.definition: A number expressible as p/q where p, q are integers and q ≠ 0. Examples: 3/4, -5/2, 7 (=7/1), 0 (=0/1).
Rational numbers are equivalent if p₁/q₁ = p₂/q₂, i.e., p₁ × q₂ = p₂ × q₁.
p/q where q > 0 and HCF(|p|, q) = 1. Example: -6/10 → -3/5 in standard form.
Rational numbers can be plotted on a number line; between any two rational numbers there are infinitely many rational numbers (dense).

laws_and_formulas.name: Additive inverse
laws_and_formulas.formula: Additive inverse of p/q is -p/q; their sum = 0

laws_and_formulas.name: Multiplicative inverse
laws_and_formulas.formula: Multiplicative inverse of p/q is q/p; their product = 1

key_points: Properties: Closure, Commutativity, Associativity hold for + and ×. Distributivity: a(b+c) = ab + ac.
Additive identity = 0; Multiplicative identity = 1.
To compare: convert to same denominator (or cross-multiply).
Rational numbers are dense: between 1/3 and 1/2 lie infinitely many rationals.
Every integer is rational but not every rational is an integer.

8|mathematics|2_Exponents_and_Powers.overview: Exponents represent repeated multiplication. Laws of exponents simplify complex calculations. Negative and zero exponents extend the concept and are essential for scientific notation.

key_concepts.definition: In aⁿ, a is the base and n is the exponent/power; aⁿ = a × a × ... × a (n times).
a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ; Example: 2⁻³ = 1/8.
Any non-zero base raised to the power 0 equals 1: a⁰ = 1 (a ≠ 0).
A number written as m × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ m < 10. Example: 602,000,000 = 6.02 × 10⁸.

key_points: Scientific notation for small numbers: 0.00035 = 3.5 × 10⁻⁴.
(-1)ⁿ = 1 if n is even; (-1)ⁿ = -1 if n is odd.
0⁰ is undefined; 0ⁿ = 0 for n > 0.
Application: distance between Earth and Sun = 1.5 × 10¹¹ m.
Comparing numbers in scientific notation: larger power of 10 = larger number.

8|mathematics|3_Squares_and_Square_Roots.overview: A perfect square is the square of an integer. Square roots reverse this process. Class 8 covers methods to find square roots (prime factorisation, long division) and properties of square numbers.

key_concepts.definition: A natural number that is the square of another natural number: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36...
√n is the number that, when squared, gives n. √25 = 5 because 5² = 25.
Three natural numbers (a, b, c) where a² + b² = c². Common: (3,4,5), (5,12,13), (8,15,17).

laws_and_formulas.name: Square root by prime factorisation
laws_and_formulas.formula: Express as product of prime pairs; take one from each pair. √324 = √(2²×3⁴) = 2×3² = 18

key_points: A perfect square ends in: 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, or 9 — never 2, 3, 7, or 8.
Square of odd number is odd; square of even number is even.
Sum of first n odd numbers = n². (1+3+5+...+2n-1 = n²)
Long division method for square roots works for large numbers and decimals.
If n is not a perfect square, √n is irrational (e.g., √2, √3, √5).

8|mathematics|4_Cubes_and_Cube_Roots.overview: Cubing a number means multiplying it by itself three times. Class 8 explores perfect cubes, their properties, and how to find cube roots using prime factorisation.

key_concepts.definition: A natural number that is the cube of another natural number: 1, 8, 27, 64, 125...
∛n is the number that, when cubed, gives n. ∛27 = 3 because 3³ = 27.
Cube of negative number is negative: (-2)³ = -8. So cube roots of negatives exist: ∛(-8) = -2.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cube root by prime factorisation
laws_and_formulas.formula: Express as product of prime triplets; take one from each. ∛216 = ∛(2³×3³) = 2×3 = 6

key_points: Cube of an even number is even; cube of an odd number is odd.
Sum of first n odd numbers' cubes: 1³+2³+...+n³ = [n(n+1)/2]².
There's no direct long division method for cube roots; use prime factorisation.
∛(-27) = -3; ∛(-125) = -5; cube roots of negatives are negative.

8|mathematics|5_Playing_with_Numbers.overview: Number puzzles and divisibility rules help develop number sense. This chapter covers divisibility tests, properties of numbers in different bases, and letter-substitution puzzles (cryptarithmetic).

key_concepts.definition: Quick tests: by 2 (last digit even); by 3 (digit sum divisible by 3); by 4 (last two digits divisible by 4); by 5 (ends in 0 or 5); by 9 (digit sum divisible by 9); by 11 (alternating digit sum divisible by 11).
A two-digit number ab̄ = 10a + b; three-digit abc̄ = 100a + 10b + c.
If ab̄ = 10a+b and reversed ba̅ = 10b+a, then ab̄ - ba̅ = 9(a-b); this is always divisible by 9.

key_points: ab̄ + ba̅ = 11(a+b) — sum of a 2-digit number and its reverse is always divisible by 11.
Any 3-digit number minus any permutation of its digits is divisible by 9.
A number is divisible by 6 if it's divisible by both 2 and 3.
Divisibility by 7: no simple rule — divide and check.
Cryptarithmetic: each letter represents a unique digit; use logical deduction (carrying, constraints) to solve.

8|mathematics|6_Sets.overview: A set is a well-defined collection of objects. Set theory provides the language for all of mathematics. Class 8 covers set notation, types of sets, and basic set operations.

key_concepts.definition: A well-defined collection of distinct objects (elements). Written with curly braces: {1, 2, 3}.
Empty set (∅ or {}), Singleton ({5}), Finite ({a,b,c}), Infinite (N = {1,2,3,...}), Universal set (U — contains all sets under consideration).
A ⊆ B if every element of A is in B. Every set is a subset of itself; ∅ is a subset of every set.
Union (A∪B): elements in A or B or both. Intersection (A∩B): elements in both A and B. Difference (A-B): in A but not B. Complement (A'): in U but not A.

key_points: Sets are unordered and contain no duplicates: {1,2,3} = {3,1,2}.
Power set of A: P(A) = set of all subsets; if n(A) = n, then n(P(A)) = 2ⁿ.
Venn diagrams: circles represent sets; overlapping region = intersection.
Disjoint sets: A∩B = ∅ (no common elements).
Equal sets: A = B if A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A.

8|mathematics|7_Percent_and_Percentage.overview: Percent means 'per hundred'. Percentages are used universally in real life — discounts, taxes, scores, statistics. Class 8 builds fluency in converting between fractions, decimals, and percentages.

key_concepts.definition: A ratio with denominator 100; x% = x/100. Example: 25% = 25/100 = 1/4.
% change = (change/original) × 100.

laws_and_formulas.name: Finding new value
laws_and_formulas.formula: New value = Original × (1 + r/100) [for r% increase]

key_points: To convert fraction to %: multiply by 100. 3/4 = 75%.
To convert % to fraction: divide by 100. 40% = 2/5.
If price rises by 20% then falls by 20%, net result is NOT 0% — it's a decrease of 4%.
Percentage error = (absolute error/true value) × 100.
Common percentages to memorise: 1/3 ≈ 33.3%, 1/6 ≈ 16.7%, 1/8 = 12.5%.

8|mathematics|8_Profit_and_Loss.overview: Profit and loss calculations are essential business mathematics. Class 8 covers cost price, selling price, profit/loss percentage, and applications like discount, marked price, and VAT/GST.

key_concepts.definition: Price at which an article is purchased (including expenses like transport, repair).
Price at which an article is sold.
The listed/labelled price before any discount; also called Tag price.
Reduction on Marked Price. Discount = MP - SP; Discount% = (Discount/MP) × 100.

laws_and_formulas.name: Profit
laws_and_formulas.formula: Profit = SP - CP (when SP > CP); Profit% = (Profit/CP) × 100

laws_and_formulas.name: Loss
laws_and_formulas.formula: Loss = CP - SP (when CP > SP); Loss% = (Loss/CP) × 100

key_points: Profit% and Loss% are always calculated on CP (not SP) — unless stated otherwise.
Successive discounts: discount of 10% then 20% ≠ 30%. Effective discount = 10 + 20 - (10×20)/100 = 28%.
VAT/GST is added on the selling price: final price = SP × (1 + tax rate/100).
Breakeven: when SP = CP (no profit, no loss).
If SP of 10 articles = CP of 8: loss = 2/10 = 20%.

8|mathematics|9_Simple_and_Compound_Inte.overview: Interest is the cost of borrowing money. Simple interest grows linearly; compound interest grows exponentially (interest on interest). Class 8 introduces compound interest and its applications.

key_concepts.definition: Interest calculated only on the principal. SI = PRT/100 where P = principal, R = rate%, T = time in years.
Interest calculated on principal + accumulated interest. Amount after n years = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ. CI = Amount - Principal.
How often interest is calculated: annually, half-yearly (R/2, 2T), quarterly (R/4, 4T).

laws_and_formulas.name: Simple Interest
laws_and_formulas.formula: SI = (P × R × T)/100; Amount = P + SI

laws_and_formulas.name: Compound Interest (annual)
laws_and_formulas.formula: A = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ; CI = A - P

laws_and_formulas.name: CI vs SI difference for 2 years

key_points: CI > SI for the same principal, rate, and time (beyond 1 year).
Population/depreciation also use CI formula: Population after n years = P(1 ± r/100)ⁿ.
When interest is compounded half-yearly: rate becomes R/2 and time becomes 2T.
Rule of 72: to estimate doubling time at r% CI, years ≈ 72/r.
Real-life CI: bank savings, EMI, depreciation of machinery, population growth.

8|mathematics|10_Direct_and_Inverse_Vari.overview: Direct and inverse variation describe how two quantities relate. Direct variation: both increase together; inverse variation: one increases as the other decreases. These are foundational for physics and everyday problem-solving.

key_concepts.definition: y ∝ x means y/x = constant (k). If x doubles, y doubles. Example: cost ∝ quantity bought.
y ∝ 1/x means xy = constant (k). If x doubles, y halves. Example: speed and time for fixed distance.
Find the value for 1 unit, then scale to required number of units.

key_points: Graph of direct variation: straight line through origin (y = kx).
Graph of inverse variation: hyperbola (xy = k).
Direct variation examples: more workers → more work done in same time. Distance ∝ speed (at constant time).
Inverse variation examples: more workers → less time to finish same work. Pressure and volume (Boyle's Law).
Compound variation: multiple variables — check each pair individually.

8|mathematics|11_Algebraic_Expressions.overview: Algebraic expressions use variables, constants, and operations. Class 8 covers addition, subtraction, and multiplication of polynomials, and introduces division of polynomial by a monomial.

key_concepts.definition: Parts of an expression separated by + or -. In 3x² - 4xy + 7, terms are 3x², -4xy, and 7.
Terms with the same variable(s) and same exponent(s): 3x²y and -7x²y are like terms; can be combined.
An expression with one or more terms with non-negative integer exponents: monomial (1 term), binomial (2 terms), trinomial (3 terms).

key_points: Addition/subtraction: only like terms can be combined.
Multiplication: use distributive property; multiply each term by each term.
Degree of a polynomial: highest power of the variable(s). 3x² + 4x - 1 has degree 2 (quadratic).
When multiplying expressions, apply laws of exponents: xᵃ × xᵇ = xᵃ⁺ᵇ.
Division of polynomial by monomial: divide each term separately.

8|mathematics|12_Algebraic_Identities.overview: Algebraic identities are equations true for all values of variables. They allow quick expansion and factorisation without lengthy multiplication, and are the backbone of simplification in algebra.

key_concepts.definition: An equation that is true for all values of the variable(s). Unlike an equation (specific solutions), identities hold universally.

key_points: Use (a+b)² to expand (103)² = (100+3)² = 10000 + 600 + 9 = 10609 — quick mental math.
Always verify by substituting specific values (e.g., a=2, b=3).
(a+b)² = (a-b)² + 4ab — useful identity derived from the two.
These identities are used in factorisation, simplification, and mensuration.

8|mathematics|13_Factorisation.overview: Factorisation is the reverse of expansion — writing an expression as a product of its factors. It is essential for simplifying, solving equations, and algebraic manipulation.

key_concepts.definition: The highest common factor extracted from all terms: 6x² + 9x = 3x(2x + 3).
Group terms into pairs, factor each pair, then extract common binomial: ax + ay + bx + by = (x+y)(a+b).
Recognise patterns: x² - 16 = (x+4)(x-4); x² + 6x + 9 = (x+3)².

key_points: Always take out the HCF first before trying other methods.
x² + bx + c: find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b → (x + p)(x + q).
Difference of squares: a² - b² = (a+b)(a-b). Use for expressions like 4x² - 9 = (2x+3)(2x-3).
Factorisation of cubics using a³ ± b³ identities.
Verify factorisation by re-expanding the product.

8|mathematics|14_Linear_Equations_in_One.overview: A linear equation in one variable has the form ax + b = c with a unique solution. Class 8 covers solving equations involving fractions and applying them to word problems.

key_concepts.definition: An equation where the variable appears only to the first power: 2x + 5 = 13.
The value of the variable that makes the equation true. For 2x + 5 = 13: x = 4.
Moving a term from one side of the equation to the other, changing its sign.

laws_and_formulas.name: Solving principle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Whatever you do to one side, do to the other. Add/subtract/multiply/divide both sides by the same quantity.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cross multiplication
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a/b = c/d, then ad = bc

key_points: Simplify: collect like terms, then isolate the variable.
Equations with fractions: multiply both sides by the LCM of denominators.
Word problems: define variable, form equation, solve, and verify.
Applications: age problems, number problems, geometry problems.
A linear equation in one variable has exactly one solution (unless contradiction or identity).

8|mathematics|15_Linear_Inequations.overview: An inequation compares two expressions using inequality signs (>, <, ≥, ≤). Linear inequations have a range of solutions, represented on a number line. Key rule: multiplying/dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality.

key_concepts.definition: A mathematical statement showing that two expressions are not equal: 2x + 3 > 7.
All values of the variable satisfying the inequation; represented on a number line or in set notation.

laws_and_formulas.name: Addition/subtraction property
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a > b, then a+c > b+c for any c.

laws_and_formulas.name: Multiplication property
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a > b and c > 0, then ac > bc. BUT if c < 0, then ac < bc (inequality reverses).

laws_and_formulas.name: Solving
laws_and_formulas.formula: Same steps as equations, but reverse inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by negative.

key_points: Open circle (○) on number line: strict inequality (> or <).
Closed circle (●): inclusive inequality (≥ or ≤).
Example: -2x + 1 ≥ 5 → -2x ≥ 4 → x ≤ -2 (inequality reverses when dividing by -2).
Replacement set: the set from which values of the variable are taken.
The solution set can be finite (if replacement set is finite) or infinite.

8|mathematics|16_Quadrilaterals.overview: A quadrilateral is a polygon with four sides. Class 8 covers the properties of general quadrilaterals, the angle sum property, and introduces various special quadrilaterals.

key_concepts.definition: A closed polygon with 4 sides, 4 vertices, and 4 angles. Sum of interior angles = 360°.
A line segment joining two non-adjacent vertices. A quadrilateral has 2 diagonals.
All diagonals lie inside the quadrilateral; all interior angles < 180°.

laws_and_formulas.name: Angle sum property
laws_and_formulas.formula: Sum of interior angles of a quadrilateral = 360°

laws_and_formulas.name: Exterior angle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Exterior angle = 180° - interior angle; sum of all exterior angles = 360°

key_points: Angle sum of a polygon with n sides = (n-2) × 180°. For quadrilateral: (4-2)×180 = 360°.
Kinds of quadrilaterals: trapezium (one pair parallel), parallelogram (two pairs parallel), kite (two pairs equal adjacent sides), rectangle, rhombus, square.
A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles.
Midpoint Theorem for quadrilateral: line segment joining midpoints of two sides is parallel to the third side.
Sum of exterior angles of ANY convex polygon = 360°.

8|mathematics|17_Special_Types_of_Quadri.overview: Special quadrilaterals — parallelogram, rectangle, rhombus, square, trapezium — have specific properties relating sides, angles, and diagonals. Class 8 builds a systematic understanding of these shapes.

key_concepts.definition: Opposite sides parallel and equal; opposite angles equal; diagonals bisect each other.
Parallelogram with all angles 90°; diagonals equal and bisect each other.
Parallelogram with all sides equal; diagonals bisect each other at right angles; diagonals bisect the angles.
Rectangle + Rhombus: all sides equal, all angles 90°; diagonals equal, perpendicular, bisect each other, bisect angles.
One pair of parallel sides (parallel sides = bases); no parallel sides = scalene trapezium; equal non-parallel sides = isosceles trapezium.

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of trapezium
laws_and_formulas.formula: Area = ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × height

laws_and_formulas.name: Area of rhombus
laws_and_formulas.formula: Area = ½ × d₁ × d₂ (where d₁, d₂ are diagonals)

key_points: Hierarchy: Square ⊂ Rectangle ⊂ Parallelogram; Square ⊂ Rhombus ⊂ Parallelogram.
A square is a special case of both rectangle and rhombus.
Kite: two pairs of equal adjacent sides; one diagonal (symmetry axis) bisects the other at 90°.
Isosceles trapezium: non-parallel sides equal; base angles equal; diagonals equal.
Diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at 90° but are NOT equal (unlike square).

8|mathematics|18_Construction.overview: Geometric construction uses a ruler and compass to create accurate figures. Class 8 focuses on constructing special quadrilaterals given sufficient conditions (sides, angles, diagonals).

key_concepts.definition: A quadrilateral is uniquely determined by 5 independent measurements (5 constraints from sides, angles, and diagonals).
Given: two adjacent sides + included angle (or diagonal). Use parallel lines through opposite vertices.
Given: two adjacent sides (since all angles = 90°). Or: length + diagonal.

key_points: A quadrilateral has 5 independent measurements (from 4 sides + 1 diagonal, or sides + angles).
Constructing rhombus: given one side + one diagonal (since all sides equal).
Constructing square: given one side OR one diagonal.
Always check constructed figure with a ruler and protractor for accuracy.
Standard constructions to know: perpendicular bisector, angle bisector — used as tools in quadrilateral construction.

8|mathematics|19_Representing_3-D_in_2-D.overview: Three-dimensional objects are represented on two-dimensional surfaces through various techniques — nets, isometric drawings, and oblique projections. This develops spatial visualisation skills essential for geometry and design.

key_concepts.definition: A flat (2D) pattern that can be folded to make a 3D solid. A cube has 11 distinct nets.
3D figure drawn on isometric dot/grid paper; all three dimensions are shown; uses 30° and 60° angles.
Shows front face true shape; receding edges drawn at 45° and shortened.

key_points: Faces, Edges, Vertices — Euler's Formula: F + V - E = 2 (for any convex polyhedron).
Cube: 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices; 6 + 8 - 12 = 2 ✓.
Tetrahedron: 4 faces, 6 edges, 4 vertices; 4 + 4 - 6 = 2 ✓.
Cross-sections of 3D objects (cylinder, cone, cube) produce 2D shapes — important for visualisation.
Shadow projections: a cube can cast a square, rectangle, or hexagon shadow depending on orientation.

8|mathematics|20_Symmetry_Reflection_and.overview: Symmetry — line symmetry, rotational symmetry — is a fundamental concept in geometry, art, and nature. Reflection produces mirror images; rotation turns a figure about a fixed point.

key_concepts.definition: A figure has line symmetry if one half is a mirror image of the other about a line (line of symmetry/axis).
Number of times a figure looks identical during one full rotation (360°). A square has rotational symmetry of order 4.
Each point maps to a point equidistant from and perpendicular to the mirror line, on the opposite side.

key_points: Equilateral triangle: 3 lines of symmetry; rotational symmetry order 3 (every 120°).
Square: 4 lines of symmetry (2 sides, 2 diagonals); rotational symmetry order 4.
Circle: infinite lines of symmetry; infinite rotational symmetry.
A figure with only 1-fold rotational symmetry (i.e., only at 360°) has no rotational symmetry.
Reflection in x-axis: (x, y) → (x, -y); in y-axis: (x, y) → (-x, y).

8|mathematics|21_Area_of_a_Trapezium_and.overview: Class 8 extends area calculations to trapeziums, rhombuses, and general polygons by subdividing them into triangles. These skills are applied in real-world problems involving land and design.

key_concepts.definition: ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × height. The two parallel sides are called bases.
½ × d₁ × d₂ where d₁, d₂ are the diagonals.
Divide into triangles or trapeziums; sum their areas.

laws_and_formulas.name: Trapezium area
laws_and_formulas.formula: A = ½(a + b)h, where a, b = parallel sides, h = perpendicular height

laws_and_formulas.name: Triangle area
laws_and_formulas.formula: A = ½ × base × height; also Heron's formula: A = √[s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)]

key_points: The height of a trapezium is the perpendicular distance between the two parallel sides.
Rhombus: all 4 sides equal; diagonals bisect each other at 90°; Area = ½ d₁d₂.
Complex polygons: split into known shapes (triangles, rectangles, trapeziums), compute each area, add.
Land area problems: use surveying — divide field into triangles using a baseline and offsets.
Heron's formula is used when all three sides of a triangle are known (no height given).

8|mathematics|22_Surface_Area_Volume_and.overview: Surface area measures the outer covering of a solid; volume measures the space inside. Class 8 covers cuboid, cube, and introduces cylinder and cone formulas.

key_concepts.definition: A box shape with 6 rectangular faces. LSA (lateral surface area) and TSA (total surface area) formulas derived from the net.
Special cuboid with all sides equal (l = b = h = a).
Circular cross-section solid with two parallel circular bases.
Amount of space inside a 3D solid. Measured in cubic units (cm³, m³). Volume = length × breadth × height (cuboid).

key_points: Capacity = Volume (in liquid context); 1 litre = 1000 cm³ = 1 dm³.
LSA/CSA: lateral/curved part only (excludes bases); TSA: includes bases.
Hollow cylinder: volume = π(R² - r²)h where R = outer radius, r = inner radius.
When a cuboid is melted and recast, volume stays the same.

8|mathematics|23_Data_Handling.overview: Data handling involves collecting, organising, representing, and interpreting data. Class 8 covers bar graphs, pie charts, histograms, mean, median, and mode — the three averages.

key_concepts.definition: Arithmetic average; Mean = Sum of all observations / Number of observations.
Middle value when data is arranged in order. If n is even: median = average of n/2 and n/2+1 values.
The value that appears most frequently. A dataset can have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes (bimodal, multimodal).
Data organised into class intervals with frequencies; used when data range is large.
Bar chart for grouped continuous data; no gaps between bars; bars represent class intervals.

key_points: Mean is affected by extreme values (outliers); median is resistant to outliers.
Pie chart: angle for each sector = (frequency/total) × 360°.
Class width = upper boundary - lower boundary; all classes should have equal width in a histogram.
Frequency polygon: connect midpoints of histogram bars; shows shape of distribution.
Stem and leaf plot: shows individual data values while grouping them; useful for small datasets.

8|mathematics|24_Probability.overview: Probability measures the likelihood of an event. It ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Class 8 introduces experimental and theoretical probability and their relationship.

key_concepts.definition: A process with a set of possible outcomes (e.g., tossing a coin, rolling a die).
The set of all possible outcomes: S = {H, T} for a coin; S = {1,2,3,4,5,6} for a die.
A subset of the sample space: 'getting an even number' = {2, 4, 6} from a die.
P(E) = Number of favourable outcomes / Total number of equally likely outcomes.
Based on actual trials: P(E) = Number of times event occurred / Total number of trials.

key_points: P(impossible event) = 0; P(certain event) = 1.
P(getting H on a fair coin) = 1/2; P(getting 6 on a fair die) = 1/6.
Complementary event: 'getting a 6' and 'not getting a 6' are complementary; probabilities sum to 1.
Law of Large Numbers: as trials increase, experimental probability → theoretical probability.
Cards: a standard deck has 52 cards (4 suits × 13 values); P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13.

8|physics|1_Matter_Kinetic_Theory_of_Mat.overview: Matter is made up of tiny particles in constant random motion. The Kinetic Theory explains the three states of matter (solid, liquid, gas), changes of state, and properties like diffusion and Brownian motion.

key_concepts.definition: Matter is made of tiny particles (atoms/molecules) in continuous, random motion. The kinetic energy of particles increases with temperature.
Solid (fixed shape/volume, particles vibrate), Liquid (fixed volume, flows, particles slide), Gas (no fixed shape/volume, particles move freely).
Spontaneous mixing of particles of two substances due to random particle motion. Rate: gases > liquids > solids.
Random zigzag motion of small particles (e.g., smoke particles, pollen in water) caused by unequal bombardment by surrounding fluid particles.
Melting (solid→liquid), Freezing (liquid→solid), Evaporation (liquid→gas at surface), Boiling (liquid→gas throughout at BP), Condensation (gas→liquid), Sublimation (solid→gas directly).

key_points: Evidence for Kinetic Theory: Brownian motion (Robert Brown, 1827), diffusion, pressure of gas.
Latent heat: heat absorbed/released during state change without temperature change.
Latent heat of fusion (melting): more kinetic energy breaks bonds; temperature stays at MP.
Gas pressure: caused by molecular bombardment on container walls; higher T → faster molecules → more pressure.
Plasma: 4th state of matter; ionised gas at very high temperatures (stars, lightning).

8|physics|2_Physical_Quantities_and_Meas.overview: Measurement is fundamental to physics. This chapter covers SI units, precision of instruments, and error analysis. Accurate measurement is the foundation of scientific understanding.

key_concepts.definition: Any property that can be measured. Fundamental (mass, length, time, current, temperature, amount, luminosity) and Derived (speed, force, pressure).
International System of Units: metre (m), kilogram (kg), second (s), ampere (A), kelvin (K), mole (mol), candela (cd).
Smallest measurement an instrument can make. Ruler: 1mm; vernier calliper: 0.1mm; screw gauge: 0.01mm.
Difference between measured value and true value. Systematic errors (calibration faults) and random errors (human variation).

key_points: Significant figures indicate the precision of a measurement.
Vernier Calliper: measures internal/external dimensions and depth to 0.1mm accuracy.
Screw Gauge (micrometer): measures wire/sheet thickness to 0.01mm; uses a screw mechanism.
Conversion: 1 km = 1000 m; 1 kg = 1000 g; 1 hour = 3600 s; 1 litre = 10⁻³ m³.
Accuracy vs Precision: accurate = close to true value; precise = consistent (reproducible) measurements.

8|physics|3_Force_and_Pressure.overview: Force causes or changes motion, deformation, or direction. Pressure is force per unit area. This chapter covers types of forces, Newton's laws concepts, and pressure in fluids (Archimedes' principle, Pascal's law).

key_concepts.definition: A push or pull that can change an object's state of rest or motion, shape, or direction. SI unit: Newton (N).
Force per unit area: P = F/A. SI unit: Pascal (Pa = N/m²). Smaller area → more pressure for the same force.
Pressure in a fluid (liquid/gas) = ρgh (ρ = density, g = gravity, h = depth). Increases with depth.
An object submerged in a fluid experiences an upthrust (buoyant force) equal to the weight of fluid displaced.
Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. Application: hydraulic press, brakes.

laws_and_formulas.name: Archimedes' Principle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Upthrust = weight of fluid displaced = ρ × V × g

key_points: Knife edge, needle tip: small area → high pressure → can cut/pierce easily.
Snowshoes, wide tyres: large area → low pressure on soft ground.
Objects float when upthrust ≥ weight; sink when weight > upthrust.
Law of flotation: floating object displaces its own weight of fluid.
Relative density (specific gravity) = density of substance / density of water; if RD > 1, sinks in water.

8|physics|4_Energy.overview: Energy is the capacity to do work. It exists in many forms (kinetic, potential, heat, light, sound, electrical, chemical, nuclear). The Law of Conservation of Energy states energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted.

key_concepts.definition: Energy due to motion: KE = ½mv².
Energy due to position or configuration. Gravitational PE = mgh; Elastic PE stored in stretched spring.
Work done on an object = change in its kinetic energy: W = ΔKE.
Rate of doing work: P = W/t. SI unit: Watt (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Commercial unit: kilowatt-hour (kWh).

laws_and_formulas.name: Work done
laws_and_formulas.formula: W = F × d × cos θ (θ = angle between force and displacement)

laws_and_formulas.name: Efficiency
laws_and_formulas.formula: Efficiency = (Useful output energy / Total input energy) × 100%

key_points: Work is done only when force causes displacement in the direction of force.
1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J (used for electrical energy billing).
Total mechanical energy = KE + PE = constant (in absence of friction).
Renewable energy: solar, wind, hydro, geothermal; non-renewable: fossil fuels, nuclear.
At maximum height (projectile): KE = 0, PE = maximum; at lowest point: KE = maximum, PE = 0.

8|physics|5_Light_Energy.overview: Light is a form of electromagnetic radiation that travels in straight lines at 3 × 10⁸ m/s. This chapter covers reflection (mirrors) and refraction (lenses), and their applications.

key_concepts.definition: 1. Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection. 2. Incident ray, normal, and reflected ray are in the same plane.
Converging mirror (reflecting surface curves inward). Used in torches, shaving mirrors, headlights. Real inverted images (except when object is within focal length — virtual, erect, magnified).
Diverging mirror (reflecting surface curves outward). Used in rear-view mirrors and security mirrors. Always forms virtual, erect, diminished images.
Bending of light as it passes from one medium to another due to change in speed.
Converging lens; thicker in the middle. Applications: magnifying glass, camera, projector, eye lens.

laws_and_formulas.name: Snell's Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; also n = sin i / sin r

key_points: Dispersion: white light splits into VIBGYOR (violet to red) spectrum by a prism.
Total Internal Reflection: when angle of incidence exceeds critical angle in denser medium; basis of optical fibres.
Concave lens (diverging): always forms virtual, erect, diminished image — used for short-sightedness correction.
Refractive index of glass ≈ 1.5; diamond ≈ 2.4 (very high → sparkling brilliance).
Real image: can be projected on a screen; formed by actual intersection of rays. Virtual: cannot be projected.

8|physics|6_Heat_Transfer.overview: Heat flows from hot to cold regions by three mechanisms: conduction (solids), convection (fluids), and radiation (no medium needed). Understanding heat transfer explains thermal insulation, climate, and everyday phenomena.

key_concepts.definition: Transfer of heat through direct contact without mass movement of the medium. Best in metals (free electrons); poor in non-metals, liquids, gases.
Transfer of heat by movement of heated fluid (liquid/gas). Hot fluid rises (less dense), cold sinks — convection currents. Examples: sea breeze, boiling water, room heating.
Transfer of heat by electromagnetic waves — no medium required. Sun heats Earth by radiation. All objects above 0 K radiate; hotter objects radiate more.
Dull black surfaces: excellent absorber and emitter of radiation. Shiny white surfaces: poor absorber (good reflector), poor emitter.

key_points: Land and sea breeze: convection due to differential heating — daytime sea breeze (sea → land); night land breeze.
Thermos (Dewar) flask: prevents all three modes — vacuum stops conduction/convection; silvered walls reflect radiation.
Greenhouse effect: Earth's atmosphere absorbs outgoing radiation — keeps Earth warm; enhanced by CO₂.
Good conductors: copper, silver, iron. Poor conductors (insulators): wood, glass, rubber, still air.
Radiation is fastest mode of heat transfer (speed of light); conduction is slowest.

8|physics|7_Sound.overview: Sound is a mechanical longitudinal wave produced by vibrating objects. It requires a medium to travel (cannot travel through vacuum) and has properties like frequency, amplitude, and speed.

key_concepts.definition: A longitudinal mechanical wave — particle vibration is parallel to direction of propagation; produces compressions and rarefactions.
Number of vibrations per second; SI unit: Hertz (Hz). Determines pitch: high frequency = high pitch.
Maximum displacement of a particle from mean position. Determines loudness: larger amplitude = louder sound.
In air: ~340 m/s at room temperature; faster in liquids (~1500 m/s in water) and solids (~5000 m/s in steel).
Reflection of sound from a hard surface. Minimum distance for echo = 17 m (sound travels 34m in 0.1s minimum for ear to distinguish).

laws_and_formulas.name: Echo distance
laws_and_formulas.formula: Distance = (v × t) / 2 (sound travels to wall and back)

key_points: Audible range: 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz. Infrasound: < 20 Hz. Ultrasound: > 20,000 Hz.
Ultrasound uses: SONAR (depth of ocean), medical imaging, cleaning, crack detection.
Noise pollution: > 85 dB causes hearing damage; decibel (dB) is the unit of sound intensity.
Musical note qualities: pitch (frequency), loudness (amplitude), quality/timbre (waveform shape).
Soundproofing: soft materials (carpet, curtains) absorb sound; hard surfaces reflect it.

8|physics|8_Electricity_and_Magnetism.overview: Electricity involves the flow of electric charge. Magnetism is a force associated with moving charges and magnetic materials. Electromagnetism links the two: a changing magnetic field induces electricity and current creates a magnetic field.

key_concepts.definition: Flow of electric charge: I = Q/t. SI unit: Ampere (A). Conventional current flows from + to -; electrons flow from - to +.
Electrical potential difference that drives current: V = IR. SI unit: Volt (V).
Opposition to current flow: R = V/I. SI unit: Ohm (Ω). Depends on material, length, cross-sectional area, temperature.
Temporary magnet created by passing current through a coil wound around a soft iron core; strength increases with more turns/current.
A changing magnetic flux through a coil induces an EMF (Faraday's Law). Basis of generators and transformers.

key_points: Series circuit: same current everywhere; voltage divides; one component fails → all fail.
Parallel circuit: same voltage; current divides; one component fails → others work. (House wiring = parallel).
Fleming's Left Hand Rule: for motor (force direction); Right Hand Rule: for generator (induced current direction).
Magnetic field pattern around a bar magnet: from N pole to S pole outside the magnet.
Earth as a magnet: geographic North = magnetic South; magnetic field protects from solar wind.

8|chemistry|1_Matter.overview: Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space, composed of tiny particles (atoms and molecules). This chapter covers the particle model, states of matter, and properties that distinguish the three states.

key_concepts.definition: Anything with mass and volume made of particles (atoms/molecules).
Fixed shape and volume; particles closely packed, vibrate in fixed positions.
Fixed volume but takes the shape of its container; particles flow past each other.
No fixed shape or volume; particles move rapidly and randomly.
Spreading of one substance through another due to random particle motion.

key_points: In solids, particles are held by strong intermolecular forces; in gases, forces are negligible.
Sublimation: direct solid-to-gas change (dry ice, iodine, camphor, naphthalene).
Evaporation occurs at all temperatures; boiling only at the boiling point.
Plasma is the fourth state of matter at very high temperatures.
Brownian motion (random movement of particles in fluid) proves the kinetic theory.

8|chemistry|2_Physical_and_Chemical_Chan.overview: Physical changes alter the form or appearance of matter without changing its chemical composition. Chemical changes produce new substances with different properties.

key_concepts.definition: No new substance formed; original substance can be recovered. E.g., melting ice, dissolving sugar.
New substance(s) formed with different properties; usually irreversible. E.g., burning wood, rusting iron.
Change in colour, production of gas, change in odour, formation of precipitate, heat/light energy change.
Releases heat energy. E.g., burning, neutralisation.
Absorbs heat energy. E.g., photosynthesis, dissolution of ammonium nitrate in water.

key_points: Melting, boiling, condensation, freezing, and dissolving are physical changes.
Burning, rusting, fermentation, souring of milk, and digestion are chemical changes.
A new substance is the key indicator of a chemical change.
Some physical changes are irreversible (breaking glass), though no new substance forms.
Chemical changes involve rearrangement of atoms to form new compounds.

8|chemistry|3_Elements_Compounds_and_Mix.overview: All matter is classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures. Elements consist of one type of atom; compounds of two or more chemically combined elements; mixtures are physical combinations of substances.

key_concepts.definition: Pure substance made of one kind of atom; cannot be broken down chemically. E.g., iron (Fe), oxygen (O₂), gold (Au).
Two or more elements chemically combined in a fixed ratio. E.g., water (H₂O), salt (NaCl).
Two or more substances physically combined in any proportion. Properties of components retained.
Uniform composition; components indistinguishable. E.g., salt solution, alloys, air.
Non-uniform; components visually distinguishable. E.g., sand and salt, granite.

laws_and_formulas.name: Law of Definite Proportions
laws_and_formulas.statement: A compound always contains elements in a fixed mass ratio. E.g., H₂O always has H:O = 1:8 by mass.

key_points: Separation methods: filtration, evaporation, distillation, chromatography, magnetic separation.
An alloy is a homogeneous mixture of metals (e.g., brass = Cu + Zn; steel = Fe + C).
In a compound, elements are in fixed mass ratio (Law of Definite Proportions).
Air is a mixture; water is a compound — a common distinction to remember.
Compounds have entirely new properties different from their constituent elements.

8|chemistry|4_Atomic_Structure.overview: The atom consists of a central nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons orbiting in shells. Atomic number, mass number, and electronic configuration define an element's identity and chemical behaviour.

key_concepts.definition: Positively charged particle in the nucleus. Charge: +1, mass: 1 amu.
Neutral particle in the nucleus. Charge: 0, mass: 1 amu.
Negatively charged particle in shells around nucleus. Charge: –1, mass: ~1/1836 amu.
Number of protons; defines the element. E.g., Z=1 is Hydrogen.
Total protons + neutrons. A = Z + N.
Same element, same Z, different A (different number of neutrons). E.g., ¹²C and ¹⁴C.
Number of electrons gained, lost, or shared during bonding; determines combining capacity.

laws_and_formulas.name: Shell Electron Capacity
laws_and_formulas.formula: Max electrons in shell n = 2n²  (K: 2, L: 8, M: 18, N: 32)

key_points: An atom is electrically neutral: number of protons = number of electrons.
Valence electrons (in outermost shell) determine chemical properties.
Noble gas configuration (8 electrons in outer shell) is stable — the basis of chemical bonding.
Isotopes have the same chemical properties but different physical properties.
Bohr's model: electrons revolve in fixed energy levels/shells K, L, M, N...

8|chemistry|5_Language_of_Chemistry.overview: Chemistry uses symbols, formulae, and equations as its language. Symbols represent elements; formulae represent compounds; balanced equations represent chemical reactions. This chapter covers writing and balancing chemical equations using valency.

key_concepts.definition: 1–2 letter abbreviation for an element. E.g., H (Hydrogen), Fe (Iron/Ferrum), Na (Sodium/Natrium).
Represents compound composition using symbols and subscripts. E.g., H₂O, NaCl, CaCO₃.
The combining capacity of an element or radical.
A group of atoms behaving as a unit with a fixed valency. E.g., SO₄²⁻, OH⁻, NH₄⁺.
Equal atoms of each element on both sides, satisfying Law of Conservation of Mass.

key_points: Formula writing: write symbols, write valencies, criss-cross (swap) the valencies as subscripts.
State symbols: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous.
Always balance equations by changing coefficients, never by changing subscripts in the formula.
Some symbols come from Latin names: Fe (Ferrum), Na (Natrium), K (Kalium), Pb (Plumbum).
A molecular formula gives the actual number of atoms; an empirical formula gives the simplest ratio.

8|chemistry|6_Chemical_Reactions.overview: Chemical reactions rearrange atoms to form new substances. This chapter classifies reactions into combination, decomposition, displacement, double displacement, and redox reactions, with examples from everyday chemistry.

key_concepts.definition: Two or more reactants form one product: A + B → AB. E.g., 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
One compound breaks into simpler substances: AB → A + B. E.g., 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂
A more reactive element displaces a less reactive one from its compound.
Two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds. E.g., AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl↓ + NaNO₃
Loss of electrons / gain of oxygen / loss of hydrogen.
Gain of electrons / loss of oxygen / gain of hydrogen.

key_points: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (electrons), Reduction Is Gain (electrons).
Exothermic reactions release heat (combustion); endothermic reactions absorb heat (photosynthesis, decomposition).
The reactivity series determines which metals can displace others.
Precipitation occurs when two solutions react to form an insoluble product.
Redox reactions involve simultaneous oxidation and reduction.

8|chemistry|7_Carbon_and_its_Compounds.overview: Carbon forms millions of compounds due to its unique ability to form chains (catenation) and four bonds (tetravalency). This chapter introduces hydrocarbons (alkanes, alkenes, alkynes), functional groups, and important carbon compounds.

key_concepts.definition: Carbon's unique ability to bond with other carbon atoms forming chains, branches, or rings.
Carbon has valency 4 — can form four covalent bonds simultaneously.
Saturated hydrocarbons with only single C–C bonds. Formula: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. E.g., Methane (CH₄), Ethane (C₂H₆).
Unsaturated with one C=C double bond. Formula: CₙH₂ₙ. E.g., Ethene (C₂H₄).
Unsaturated with one C≡C triple bond. Formula: CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. E.g., Ethyne (C₂H₂).
An atom or group of atoms in an organic molecule that determines its chemical properties. E.g., –OH (alcohol), –COOH (carboxylic acid).

key_points: Carbon forms over 10 million known compounds — more than any other element.
Saturated compounds (alkanes) undergo substitution reactions; unsaturated (alkenes, alkynes) undergo addition reactions.
Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is used as fuel, solvent, and in beverages; methanol (CH₃OH) is toxic.
Biogas (mainly methane) is produced from organic waste — a clean renewable fuel.
Petroleum refining by fractional distillation yields LPG, petrol, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oil, and bitumen.

8|chemistry|8_Water.overview: Water is the most abundant liquid and essential for life. This chapter covers water as a universal solvent, hardness of water, the water cycle, and methods of water purification and treatment.

key_concepts.definition: Water dissolves more substances than any other liquid due to its polarity.
Contains dissolved Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts; forms scum with soap instead of lather.
Caused by Ca(HCO₃)₂ and Mg(HCO₃)₂; removed by boiling.
Caused by CaSO₄, MgSO₄, CaCl₂; not removed by boiling; removed by washing soda or distillation.
Water safe for drinking, treated by sedimentation, filtration, and chlorination.

key_points: Water has anomalous expansion: maximum density at 4°C; expands when frozen — why ice floats.
The water cycle: evaporation → condensation (cloud formation) → precipitation → collection.
Chlorination kills pathogens in public water supply; fluoridation prevents tooth decay.
Hardness of water increases scale formation in boilers — an energy and safety concern.
Sewage treatment (primary, secondary, tertiary) prevents water pollution.

8|chemistry|9_Hydrogen.overview: Hydrogen is the lightest element and most abundant in the universe. This chapter covers its preparation from metals and acids, physical and chemical properties, and its importance as a future clean fuel.

key_concepts.definition: Diatomic, colourless, odourless, highly flammable gas; lightest element (atomic mass = 1).
Zinc + dilute sulphuric acid → zinc sulphate + hydrogen: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑
Burns with a pale blue flame: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. Explosive in 2:1 (H₂:O₂) ratio.
Reduces metal oxides: CuO + H₂ → Cu + H₂O. Used in metallurgy.
Addition of hydrogen to unsaturated compounds; used to make vegetable ghee from vegetable oils.

key_points: Hydrogen is collected by downward displacement of air (it is lighter than air).
The 'pop' test: hydrogen burns with a squeaky pop when a burning splint is applied.
Electrolysis of water: hydrogen at cathode, oxygen at anode (2:1 volume ratio).
Hydrogen as fuel produces only water vapour — environmentally clean.
Hydrogen is used in the Haber process (N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) to produce ammonia for fertilisers.

8|biology|1_Transport_of_Food_and_Minera.overview: Plants have two vascular systems: xylem transports water and minerals from roots to leaves; phloem transports food (sucrose) from leaves to other parts. This chapter explains the mechanisms behind these two transport systems.

key_concepts.definition: Vascular tissue that conducts water and dissolved minerals upward from roots; consists of dead cells (tracheids, vessels, fibres, parenchyma).
Vascular tissue that transports organic food (sucrose, amino acids) in both directions; consists of living cells (sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem parenchyma, fibres).
Evaporation of water from leaves (stomata) creates negative pressure that pulls water up the xylem — the main force for water movement.
Movement of water from a region of higher water potential (dilute solution/pure water) to lower water potential (concentrated solution) through a semipermeable membrane.
Positive pressure generated in roots by active uptake of minerals, pushing water into xylem — contributes to water movement at night and in short plants.

key_points: Translocation: movement of food through phloem; both upward (to growing shoot tips, flowers) and downward (to roots, storage organs).
Wilting: loss of turgor pressure in cells due to water loss exceeding uptake.
Guttation: water droplets on leaf tips in the morning — caused by root pressure when transpiration is low.
Ringing experiment (removal of phloem ring): food accumulates above the cut, proving food moves in phloem.
Capillarity and cohesion-tension: water molecules stick to each other and to xylem walls — key physical forces.

8|biology|2_Reproduction_in_Plants.overview: Plants reproduce sexually (by seeds) and asexually (vegetative propagation). Class 8 covers the structure of flowers, pollination, fertilisation, and fruit and seed formation in angiosperms.

key_concepts.definition: Involves fusion of gametes (egg + sperm from pollen); produces genetically varied offspring; results in seeds.
Transfer of pollen grains from anther to stigma. Self-pollination (same flower/plant); cross-pollination (different plant of same species).
Pollen grain lands on stigma → pollen tube grows down style → enters ovule → sperm fuses with egg (fertilisation) → zygote.
Unique to angiosperms: one sperm + egg → zygote (embryo); second sperm + two polar nuclei → 3n endosperm (food).
After fertilisation: ovary wall → pericarp (fruit); ovule → seed; embryo develops from zygote; endosperm nourishes embryo.

key_points: Insect-pollinated: bright, scented, sticky pollen, nectar; wind-pollinated: small, dull, light pollen, feathery stigma.
Dispersal of seeds: wind (light, winged — drumstick, dandelion), water (fibrous — coconut), animals (hooked — xanthium, fleshy fruits eaten and seeds excreted), explosion (Impatiens).
Germination requires: water (activates enzymes), oxygen (aerobic respiration for energy), suitable temperature.
Epigeal germination (cotyledons above soil — bean); Hypogeal germination (cotyledons below — pea).
Ovule → seed; ovary → fruit; petals etc. dry up and fall off.

8|biology|3_Reproduction_in_Animals.overview: Animals reproduce sexually (fusion of gametes) or asexually (budding, binary fission). This chapter covers types of reproduction, the human reproductive system, and embryonic development.

key_concepts.definition: Binary fission (Amoeba, bacteria — one organism splits in two); Budding (Hydra, yeast — bud grows and separates); Regeneration (Planaria — cut pieces grow into whole organisms).
Fusion of male gamete (sperm) and female gamete (ovum) → zygote; produces genetic variation.
External fertilisation: outside the body in water (fish, frog); Internal fertilisation: inside female body (reptiles, birds, mammals).
Oviparous: lay eggs (fish, frog, birds, reptiles); Viviparous: give birth to live young (most mammals including humans).
Male: testes produce sperm; Female: ovaries produce eggs (ovulation monthly); fertilisation in fallopian tube; implantation in uterus; gestation ~9 months.

key_points: Amoeba: binary fission — nucleus divides → cytoplasm divides → two identical daughter cells.
Hydra: budding — outgrowth from parent body grows, develops mouth and tentacles, then separates.
Frog: external fertilisation; male and female shed gametes into water; tadpole (larva) undergoes metamorphosis.
Placenta: structure in human uterus through which nutrients, oxygen, and wastes are exchanged between mother and foetus.
Foetal development: zygote → morula → blastocyst → embryo → foetus; full term = 40 weeks.

8|biology|4_Ecosystem.overview: An ecosystem is a community of organisms interacting with their physical environment. Energy flows through food chains; materials cycle through biogeochemical cycles. Ecosystems can be aquatic or terrestrial.

key_concepts.definition: Biotic: producers (plants), consumers (animals), decomposers (bacteria, fungi). Abiotic: sunlight, water, soil, temperature, air.
Linear sequence showing transfer of energy from producers to consumers: Grass → Rabbit → Fox → Lion.
Interconnected food chains in an ecosystem; more stable and realistic.
Only ~10% of energy at one trophic level passes to the next; 90% lost as heat. So food chains rarely have more than 4–5 links.
Cycling of carbon, nitrogen, and water through biotic and abiotic components of ecosystems.

key_points: Ecological pyramid: always narrows upward — energy/biomass decrease at higher trophic levels.
Carbon cycle: photosynthesis (CO₂ → organic carbon); respiration/decomposition/combustion (organic carbon → CO₂).
Nitrogen cycle: fixation → nitrification → assimilation → ammonification → denitrification.
Decomposers are essential — they return nutrients to the soil for use by producers.
Deforestation disrupts ecosystems: loss of habitat, soil erosion, altered water cycle, loss of biodiversity.

8|biology|5_Endocrine_System.overview: The endocrine system uses chemical messengers (hormones) secreted by glands directly into the blood to regulate body functions. It works slowly but with long-lasting effects, complementing the faster nervous system.

key_concepts.definition: A chemical messenger produced by an endocrine gland and transported in blood to a target organ. Specific — each hormone affects specific target cells.
'Master gland' at base of brain; controls other endocrine glands; secretes GH (growth hormone), TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, ADH, oxytocin.
In neck; secretes thyroxine — regulates metabolism, growth, development. Iodine is required. Deficiency: goitre (underactive); excess: hyperthyroidism.
Both exocrine (digestive enzymes) and endocrine (hormones). Islets of Langerhans: beta cells → insulin (lowers blood glucose); alpha cells → glucagon (raises blood glucose).
On top of kidneys. Adrenal medulla: adrenaline — 'fight or flight' hormone. Adrenal cortex: cortisol (stress response), aldosterone (Na⁺/K⁺ balance).

key_points: Diabetes mellitus Type 1: insufficient insulin production; treated with insulin injections.
Diabetes mellitus Type 2: cells become resistant to insulin; diet and medication manage it.
Feedback control: high blood glucose → insulin released → glucose taken up by cells → blood glucose falls → insulin secretion stops.
Ovaries: oestrogen and progesterone (female sex hormones — puberty, menstrual cycle, pregnancy).
Testes: testosterone (male sex hormone — puberty, sperm production, secondary sexual characteristics).

8|biology|6_Adolescence.overview: Adolescence is the period of transition from childhood to adulthood (typically 10–19 years). It is characterised by rapid physical, hormonal, and psychological changes — puberty. Understanding these changes helps young people navigate them confidently.

key_concepts.definition: Period of sexual maturation; controlled by hormones (testosterone in males, oestrogen in females); occurs around 10–14 years in girls, 11–16 in boys.
Changes at puberty that distinguish males and females but are not directly involved in reproduction: voice change (males), breast development (females), growth of body hair (both).
Monthly cycle (~28 days) in females: menstruation (days 1–5) → follicular phase → ovulation (~day 14) → luteal phase → menstruation again if no fertilisation.
First menstrual period; marks beginning of female reproductive capability, usually 11–14 years.

key_points: Changes in boys: testes enlarge, voice breaks (larynx grows), facial/body hair, increased muscle mass, sperm production.
Changes in girls: ovaries mature, menarche, breast development, widening of hips.
Common changes in both: height spurt, pubic and underarm hair, acne (sebaceous glands more active).
Role of pituitary gland: at puberty, begins producing FSH and LH → stimulate gonads (ovaries/testes) to produce sex hormones.
Emotional changes: increased self-awareness, mood swings, desire for independence — normal part of development.

8|biology|7_Circulatory_System.overview: The circulatory system is a closed, double circulation system (pulmonary + systemic). The heart pumps blood; arteries carry blood away from the heart; veins return it. Blood transports oxygen, nutrients, and waste products.

key_concepts.definition: 4-chambered muscular pump (2 atria + 2 ventricles); left side handles oxygenated blood, right side handles deoxygenated blood; walls of ventricles thicker than atria.
Pulmonary circulation: right heart → lungs → left heart (oxygenation). Systemic circulation: left heart → body → right heart (oxygen delivery).
Arteries: thick walls, carry blood away from heart, high pressure. Veins: thinner walls, carry blood to heart, have valves. Capillaries: one cell thick — site of gas/nutrient/waste exchange.
Plasma (55%): water + proteins + nutrients + waste. Red Blood Cells (RBC): haemoglobin carries O₂. White Blood Cells (WBC): immune defence. Platelets: clotting.
Pressure exerted by blood on artery walls: systolic (heart contracting)/diastolic (heart relaxing). Normal: ~120/80 mmHg.

key_points: Pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood (only artery to carry deoxygenated blood); pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood (only vein to carry oxygenated blood).
Cardiac cycle: Systole (contraction) → Diastole (relaxation). Heart rate at rest: 60–80 beats/min.
Valves: tricuspid (right AV), bicuspid/mitral (left AV), semilunar (aortic and pulmonary) — prevent backflow.
Lymphatic system: collects tissue fluid, returns it to blood; lymph nodes filter pathogens; lymphocytes produced.
Haemoglobin: iron-containing protein in RBCs; binds O₂ in lungs, releases in tissues; CO binds permanently (poisoning).

8|biology|8_Nervous_System.overview: The nervous system controls and coordinates body functions using electrical signals. It has the central nervous system (brain + spinal cord) and peripheral nervous system (nerves). Neurons are the functional units.

key_concepts.definition: Structural and functional unit of the nervous system. Parts: dendrites (receive signals), cell body (contains nucleus), axon (conducts impulse), myelin sheath (insulation, speeds conduction), synaptic terminals.
Junction between two neurons. Electrical impulse → neurotransmitter release → crosses synaptic cleft → binds to next neuron's receptors.
Cerebrum (thought, memory, voluntary actions — left/right hemispheres), Cerebellum (balance, coordination), Medulla Oblongata (breathing, heart rate, blood pressure — vital reflexes).
Rapid, automatic, unconscious response to a stimulus. Reflex arc: receptor → sensory neuron → spinal cord (relay neuron) → motor neuron → effector.
Runs through vertebral column; conducts impulses between brain and body; controls reflex actions.

key_points: Nervous system divisions: Central (brain + spinal cord) + Peripheral (cranial nerves + spinal nerves).
Peripheral: Somatic (voluntary, skeletal muscles) + Autonomic (involuntary — sympathetic 'fight/flight', parasympathetic 'rest/digest').
Types of neurons: sensory (sense organs → CNS), motor (CNS → effectors), relay/interneurons (within CNS).
Withdrawal reflex (touching hot object): receptor → sensory neuron → spinal cord → motor neuron → muscle withdraws — no brain involvement.
Myelin sheath: insulates axon; speeds up nerve impulse conduction (saltatory conduction); multiple sclerosis attacks myelin.

8|biology|9_Diseases_and_First_Aid.overview: Diseases are abnormal conditions affecting health. They can be communicable (infectious — spread by pathogens) or non-communicable (lifestyle-related, genetic). First aid is the immediate care given to an ill or injured person before professional medical help arrives.

key_concepts.definition: Disease caused by pathogens (bacteria, virus, fungi, protozoa, parasites) that can spread person to person: cholera, TB, malaria, dengue, COVID-19.
Not infectious; lifestyle, genetic, or environmental: diabetes, hypertension, cancer, cardiovascular disease, asthma.
Body's ability to resist disease. Innate (natural barriers) and Acquired (specific — antibodies from exposure or vaccination).
Contains weakened/killed pathogens or antigens; stimulates immune system to produce memory cells → protects against future infection.
DRABC: Danger, Response, Airway, Breathing, Circulation. Recovery position for unconscious breathing patients.

key_points: Golden rules of first aid: preserve life, prevent deterioration, promote recovery. Do not remove embedded objects.
Burns: cool under running water (20 min); do NOT use ice, butter, or toothpaste.
Fracture: immobilise the limb; do not attempt to reset; take to hospital.
Dengue: spread by Aedes aegypti mosquito; no vaccine; prevention = eliminating stagnant water breeding sites.
Antibiotics treat bacterial infections only — not viral (cold, flu, COVID-19).

8|biology|10_Food_Production_and_Managem.overview: With a growing global population, food production must increase sustainably. This chapter covers crop improvement, food management, soil conservation, and the challenges of food security for India and the world.

key_concepts.definition: Using plant breeding, hybrid seeds, and genetic engineering to develop higher-yielding, disease-resistant, drought-tolerant crop varieties.
1960s–70s: introduction of high-yielding variety (HYV) seeds of wheat and rice in India (Norman Borlaug — wheat; MS Swaminathan — Indian father of Green Revolution).
Agricultural method using natural fertilisers (compost, manure), crop rotation, and biological pest control instead of synthetic chemicals.
Preventing soil erosion: contour ploughing, terracing, windbreaks (shelter belts), afforestation, strip cropping.
Scientific management of livestock for milk, meat, eggs: cattle (dairy), poultry, fish (aquaculture), pig farming.

key_points: Food security: availability (enough food), accessibility (people can get it), and utilisation (nutritious, safe food).
India: Public Distribution System (PDS) — ration shops distribute subsidised food grains to BPL families.
Biofortification: developing crops with enhanced nutritional value — Golden Rice (Vitamin A), iron-rich beans.
Pest management: chemical (pesticides), biological (natural predators), integrated pest management (IPM).
Nitrogen fixation by Rhizobium in legume root nodules reduces need for chemical nitrogen fertilisers.

8|history_civics|history_1_A_Period_of_T.overview: The 18th–19th centuries saw a transition from medieval to modern history: the decline of traditional empires, the rise of nationalism, and the Industrial Revolution transforming Europe and its colonies.

key_concepts.definition: Shift from agrarian to industrial economy (mid-18th century, England); steam engine, mechanised factories, urbanisation.
European nations establishing political and economic control over Asia, Africa, and the Americas.
18th century European philosophical movement stressing reason, rights, and scientific thought; influenced revolutions in America and France.

key_points: James Watt's steam engine (1769): revolutionised industry and transport.
Enclosure movement: common lands taken over → peasants moved to cities → supplied industrial labour.
Colonialism: Britain, France, Spain, Portugal established empires for raw materials and markets.
Key transitions: feudalism → capitalism; agrarian → industrial; monarchy → democracy.
This period set the stage for the American, French, and Industrial revolutions and their global impact.

8|history_civics|history_2_The_Growth_of.overview: Nationalism — the idea that people sharing a common culture, language, or history should form their own nation — grew through the 19th century, leading to revolutions, wars of unification, and the collapse of empires.

key_concepts.definition: The belief that people of a shared culture/language/history should have their own self-governing nation-state.
Overthrew the French monarchy; proclaimed Liberty, Equality, Fraternity; spread nationalist and democratic ideas across Europe.
1871: Otto von Bismarck unified German states under Prussian leadership through 'blood and iron' — wars against Austria and France.
Cavour (diplomacy), Garibaldi (military — 'Red Shirts'), Mazzini (ideology) unified Italy by 1861.

key_points: Napoleon: spread French revolutionary ideas across Europe (nationalism, legal reform); Code Napoleon.
1848 Revolutions ('Spring of Nations'): nationalist uprisings across Europe — largely failed but showed strength of nationalist sentiment.
Ottoman Empire: declined in 19th century; Greece, Serbia, Bulgaria gained independence (nationalism breaking apart empires).
Romantic Nationalism: use of language, folk culture, and history to build national identity (Grimm Brothers, composers like Chopin).
Nationalism's dark side: led to World War I (ethnic tensions in Austria-Hungary, competition among nations).

8|history_civics|history_3_The_American_.overview: The American Civil War (1861–1865) was fought between the Northern States (Union) and Southern States (Confederacy) over the issue of slavery and states' rights. The Union's victory preserved the United States and led to the abolition of slavery.

key_concepts.definition: Economic: North (industrial) vs South (agrarian, slave-based cotton economy). Political: states' rights vs federal authority. Moral: slavery — Northern abolitionists vs Southern slaveholders.
16th US President (1861–1865); issued Emancipation Proclamation (1863) freeing slaves in Confederate states; united the Union; assassinated in 1865.
Executive order by Lincoln declaring slaves in Confederate states to be free; moral turning point of the war.
Post-war rebuilding of Southern states; 13th Amendment abolished slavery; 14th Amendment granted citizenship to African Americans.

key_points: Confederate States of America: 11 Southern states that seceded from the Union in 1861.
Battle of Gettysburg (1863): deadliest battle; Union victory — turning point of the war.
General Robert E. Lee (Confederate) surrendered to General Ulysses S. Grant (Union) at Appomattox, April 1865.
13th Amendment (1865): abolished slavery throughout the USA.
Lincoln's Gettysburg Address: defined the war as a struggle for democracy — 'government of the people, by the people, for the people'.

8|history_civics|history_4_India_in_the_.overview: The 18th century saw the disintegration of the Mughal Empire and the rise of regional powers (Marathas, Sikhs, Mysore, Hyderabad). European trading companies exploited this political fragmentation to establish their power.

key_concepts.definition: After Aurangzeb's death (1707): weak successors, Maratha invasions, Nadir Shah's invasion (1739 — took Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor), Ahmad Shah Durrani's invasions.
Former Mughal provinces that became independent: Hyderabad (Nizam), Awadh (Nawab), Bengal (Murshid Quli Khan), Mysore (Hyder Ali → Tipu Sultan).
Most powerful force after Mughal decline; Peshwas led military expansion; Battle of Panipat 1761 (Ahmad Shah Durrani vs Marathas) weakened them.
British (East India Company) vs French (Compagnie des Indes) for control of trade and influence; Carnatic Wars decided in Britain's favour.

key_points: Nadir Shah (1739): Persian invader sacked Delhi, took Koh-i-Noor diamond and Peacock Throne.
Third Battle of Panipat (1761): Ahmad Shah Durrani defeated Marathas — ended Maratha bid for pan-Indian power.
The British used 'divide and rule' to exploit conflicts between Indian rulers.
Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan: strongest resistance to British from Mysore — four Anglo-Mysore Wars.
Battle of Plassey (1757) and Buxar (1764): established British supremacy in Bengal.

8|history_civics|history_5_Traders_to_Ru.overview: The British East India Company transformed from a trading body to the ruling power of India through wars, diplomacy, and annexation policies. By 1858, Britain directly controlled the subcontinent.

key_concepts.definition: Robert Clive defeated Nawab Siraj-ud-Daullah of Bengal (with traitor Mir Jafar's help); gave British economic and political control of Bengal — foothold for empire.
British defeated combined army of Nawab of Bengal, Nawab of Awadh, and the Mughal Emperor — confirmed British as the supreme power in India.
Dalhousie's policy: if a ruler died without a natural heir, the state was 'lapsed' to the British. Annexed: Satara, Nagpur, Jhansi, Awadh.
Wellesley's policy: Indian rulers accepted British troops and control of foreign policy in exchange for protection; gave up sovereignty gradually.

key_points: Anglo-Mysore Wars (1767–1799): 4 wars; Tipu Sultan killed at Seringapatam (1799) in the 4th war.
Anglo-Maratha Wars (1775–1818): 3 wars; 3rd ended Maratha power — British controlled most of India.
Anglo-Sikh Wars (1845–1849): 2 wars; Punjab annexed after death of Ranjit Singh.
Dual Government of Bengal (Clive): British controlled revenue; Nawab nominal administration — exploitative.
1858: Company rule ended; Crown (Queen Victoria) took direct control of India.

8|history_civics|history_6_British_Polic.overview: British economic and administrative policies fundamentally transformed Indian society. Land revenue systems exploited farmers; de-industrialisation destroyed Indian crafts; economic drain impoverished India.

key_concepts.definition: Cornwallis: zamindars made permanent landowners; fixed revenue; if not paid → land auctioned; exploitative for peasants.
Thomas Munro (Madras/Bombay): direct settlement with individual farmers (ryots); flexible but burdensome revenue demands.
Dadabhai Naoroji's concept: India's economic resources flowing to Britain as profits, salaries, and payments without return benefit — impoverished India.
British machine-made goods ruined Indian textile and craft industries — handloom weavers lost their livelihoods; India became a raw material supplier and market for British goods.

key_points: Mahalwari Settlement: northern India; settlement with village community (mahal); revenue paid collectively.
Railways built (from 1853): primarily served British interests (moving raw materials to ports, troops quickly); but also connected India.
Lord Macaulay's Minute (1835): introduced English education — created an educated Indian class to serve the British administration.
Forest Acts: restricted Indian access to forests; disrupted tribal livelihoods.
Economic drain estimated to be 12-15% of India's GDP annually — severely retarded Indian economic development.

8|history_civics|history_7_The_Revolt_of.overview: The Revolt of 1857 (also called the First War of Indian Independence or the Sepoy Mutiny) was a major uprising against British rule, beginning with sepoy grievances but becoming a broader resistance involving rulers, farmers, and common people.

key_concepts.definition: The Enfield rifle's greased cartridges (rumoured to be coated with cow and pig fat) — offended Hindu and Muslim sepoys who had to bite them off.
Indian soldier (infantry) in the British East India Company's army; were the primary instigators of the revolt.
Bahadur Shah Zafar (Delhi — titular leader), Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi, Nana Sahib (Kanpur), Begum Hazrat Mahal (Lucknow), Tantya Tope.

Political (Doctrine of Lapse, annexation), Economic (drain, land revenue), Social/Cultural (missionary activity, social reforms), Military (discrimination against Indian officers, overseas deployment).

key_points: Mangal Pandey: sepoy who shot a British officer on March 29, 1857 at Barrackpore — early spark.
10 May 1857: sepoys at Meerut refused to use cartridges, freed imprisoned colleagues, marched to Delhi.
Revolt was localised: Bengal, NW provinces, Central India; Punjab, Bengal Presidency, Madras largely unaffected.
Why it failed: no unified leadership or national plan; many rulers stayed loyal to British; superior British arms.
Aftermath: East India Company rule ended; Crown took direct control (1858); Mughal Empire ended; Queen Victoria's Proclamation promised no more annexations.

8|history_civics|history_8_Socio-Religio.overview: 19th century India saw powerful reform movements challenging caste discrimination, sati, child marriage, and superstition. Reformers used both Hindu scriptures and Enlightenment ideas to advocate for a rational, egalitarian society.

key_concepts.definition: Founded Brahmo Samaj (1828); opposed sati, idol worship, child marriage, caste discrimination; supported widow remarriage, English education; called 'Father of Modern India'.
Founded 1828 by Ram Mohan Roy; monotheistic, rationalist; later led by Debendranath Tagore and Keshab Chandra Sen.
Founded 1875 by Dayananda Saraswati; 'Back to the Vedas'; opposed idol worship, caste, untouchability; promoted Hindi, women's education.
Founded by Swami Vivekananda (disciple of Ramakrishna Paramahansa); promoted Vedanta, service as worship; presented Hinduism globally at Chicago Parliament of Religions (1893).
Abolition of sati (1829 — Lord William Bentinck); Widow Remarriage Act (1856); Age of Consent Act (1891, raised to 12 years); Child Marriage Restraint Act.

key_points: Sati Pratha: widow burning on husband's pyre — declared illegal in 1829 largely due to Ram Mohan Roy's campaign.
Jyotiba Phule and Savitribai Phule: fought for education of women and lower castes in Maharashtra; Satya Shodhak Samaj.
Sir Syed Ahmad Khan: founded Aligarh Muslim University (1875); promoted modern education for Muslims.
Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi: led Wahhabi movement for purification of Islam; anti-British jihad.
Theosophical Society (Annie Besant, Madame Blavatsky): promoted Indian spirituality; supported Indian self-rule.

8|history_civics|history_9_Indias_Freedo.overview: The Indian National Congress (founded 1885) represented the early phase of nationalism — moderate and constitutional. Early leaders believed in petition and representation within the British system to gradually achieve self-rule.

key_concepts.definition: Founded by A.O. Hume (British civil servant) with Dadabhai Naoroji, Pherozeshah Mehta; first session in Bombay; advocated constitutional reforms.
Early Congress leaders: Dadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Pherozeshah Mehta. Method: petitions, memorials, resolutions; faith in British sense of justice.
'Grand Old Man of India'; conceptualised 'Drain of Wealth'; first Indian elected to British Parliament (1892); thrice Congress President.
Viceroy Curzon divided Bengal (Hindu west + Muslim east) — official reason: administrative efficiency; real motive: divide nationalist movement. Led to Swadeshi movement.

key_points: Demands of Moderates: Indianisation of civil services, reduction in military expenditure, free press, judicial reform.
Ilbert Bill controversy (1883): proposed Indian judges could try European defendants → racist backlash → showed limits of British 'fair play'.
Indian Councils Acts 1861 and 1892: some Indian representation in legislative councils — Moderates' limited gains.
Swadeshi movement (post-1905): boycott of British goods; use of Indian-made goods; mass protest against partition of Bengal.
Indian National Congress split (1907 Surat): Moderates vs Assertives (Bal Gangadhar Tilak's faction).

8|history_civics|history_10_Indias_Freed.overview: The Assertive (Extremist) phase represented a more confrontational nationalism. Leaders like Tilak called for Swaraj (self-rule) immediately. The Swadeshi movement, revolutionary activities, and the emergence of the Home Rule movement marked this period.

key_concepts.definition: Led by Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, Bipin Chandra Pal (Lal-Bal-Pal trio); demanded complete Swaraj; used mass mobilisation (not just petitions).
Boycott of British goods; promotion of Indian-made goods (Swadeshi); burning of foreign cloth; spread to rest of India.
'Lokmanya' (respected by the people); 'Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it'; used Ganesh Chaturthi and Shivaji festival for mass mobilisation; Home Rule League (1916).
Tilak's Home Rule League + Annie Besant's Home Rule League demanded self-government within the British Empire.
Indian Councils Act: increased Indian members in councils but introduced separate electorates for Muslims — divide and rule.

key_points: Revolutionary activities: Aurobindo Ghosh, Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Bhagat Singh's predecessors.
Partition of Bengal annulled (1911) — victory for Swadeshi movement.
World War I (1914–1918): India contributed soldiers and resources; expected political reforms as reward.
Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919): dyarchy in provinces; disappointingly limited self-government.
Lucknow Pact (1916): Congress-Muslim League agreement; showed Hindu-Muslim unity.

8|history_civics|history_11_Indias_Freed.overview: Mahatma Gandhi transformed the freedom struggle into a mass movement using non-violent civil disobedience (Satyagraha). Three major movements — Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, Quit India — built inexorable pressure on British rule, leading to independence in 1947.

key_concepts.definition: Gandhi's philosophy of non-violent resistance; 'truth force' — using moral authority and suffering to change the oppressor's heart.
Boycott of British titles, courts, schools, legislative councils; return of Indian goods; led by Gandhi after Jallianwala Bagh massacre. Withdrawn after Chauri Chaura (Feb 1922).
Gandhi marched 241 miles from Sabarmati to Dandi (April 6, 1930) to make salt illegally — defying the Salt Tax; massive civil disobedience followed.
Gandhi's call ('Do or Die') to immediately end British rule; 'Quit India!'; massive arrests including Gandhi, Nehru; underground resistance; most violent mass movement.
General Dyer ordered firing on unarmed crowd gathered at Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar — 379 official dead (actual much higher); turning point in Indian opinion against the British.

key_points: Rowlatt Act (1919): allowed detention without trial — triggered nationwide protests and Jallianwala Bagh.
Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931): civil disobedience suspended; Gandhi attended Second Round Table Conference in London.
Subhas Chandra Bose: formed Indian National Army (Azad Hind Fauj) in South-East Asia with Japanese support; 'Give me blood and I will give you freedom'.
Cabinet Mission Plan (1946): proposed united India with autonomous provinces — failed; partition became inevitable.
Independence: August 15, 1947; India and Pakistan became separate nations; Partition caused massive violence and displacement (15 million displaced, ~1 million died).

8|history_civics|history_12_The_United_N.overview: The United Nations (UN) was established in 1945 after World War II to maintain international peace, promote cooperation, and protect human rights. It replaced the failed League of Nations.

key_concepts.definition: Founding document of the UN; signed June 26, 1945 in San Francisco; 51 original member nations; came into force October 24, 1945 (UN Day).
All member states (193 today); each has one vote; passes resolutions (non-binding); discusses all global issues.
15 members: 5 permanent (USA, UK, Russia, China, France — P5) + 10 elected for 2 years. Primary responsibility for international peace and security; P5 have veto power.
UNESCO (education/culture), WHO (health), UNICEF (children), FAO (food/agriculture), UNDP (development), UNHCR (refugees), ILO (labour), IMF, World Bank.

key_points: UN Headquarters: New York, USA; Secretary-General is the chief administrative officer.
Veto power: any P5 member can block any substantive Security Council resolution.
MDGs (Millennium Development Goals): 2000–2015; SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals): 2015–2030 — 17 goals including no poverty, zero hunger, quality education.
UN peacekeeping: 'Blue Helmets' — UN soldiers deployed to conflict zones; India is one of the largest contributors.
India and the UN: founding member; permanent seat sought in expanded Security Council; significant role in peacekeeping.

8|history_civics|civics_1_The_Union_Legi.overview: The Parliament is India's supreme legislative body. It is bicameral — Lok Sabha (Lower House) and Rajya Sabha (Upper House). Parliament makes laws, approves the budget, and holds the executive accountable.
