Haemoglobin in red blood cells binds O₂ (in lungs) and releases it at tissues.
CO₂ is transported mainly as bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻) in plasma.
Tidal volume: ~500 mL per breath at rest; vital capacity: ~4.5 L (maximum breath in + breath out).
Breathing rate: 15–20 breaths/min at rest; increases with exercise (more CO₂ → stimulates medulla → faster breathing).

9|biology|Health_and_Hygiene_Cause_of_di.overview: Diseases can be caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and parasitic worms. Understanding the causative agents helps in prevention (vaccines, hygiene) and treatment (antibiotics, antivirals).

key_concepts.definition: Disease caused by a pathogen (bacteria, virus, fungus, protozoan, parasite) that can spread from person to person.
Tuberculosis (Mycobacterium), Cholera (Vibrio cholerae), Typhoid (Salmonella typhi), Tetanus (Clostridium tetani), Pneumonia (Streptococcus).
Influenza (flu), COVID-19 (SARS-CoV-2), Polio (poliovirus), Measles, Chickenpox, Hepatitis B, HIV/AIDS, Rabies.
Malaria (Plasmodium — transmitted by Anopheles mosquito), Amoebic dysentery (Entamoeba histolytica).
Tapeworm (Taenia), Roundworm (Ascaris), Filaria (Wuchereria bancrofti — causes elephantiasis).

key_points: Antibiotics kill bacteria but NOT viruses — cannot use for flu or COVID-19.
Vaccines: contain weakened/killed pathogen or antigens; stimulate immune memory without causing disease.
Malaria lifecycle: Plasmodium → mosquito → human bloodstream → liver → red blood cells → fever.
Vector: organism that transmits disease. Mosquito (malaria, dengue, filaria), sandfly (kala-azar), tsetse fly (sleeping sickness).
DOTS (Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course): WHO strategy to cure tuberculosis.

9|biology|Health_and_Hygiene_Hygiene.overview: Personal and community hygiene practices prevent the spread of diseases and maintain individual and public health. This chapter covers principles of cleanliness, food hygiene, and prevention of common diseases.

key_concepts.definition: Regular bathing, handwashing (especially before eating and after toilet), dental hygiene, clean clothing, safe sexual practices.
Proper cooking, refrigeration, covering food, avoiding contaminated water — prevents foodborne illness.
Boiling, chlorination, filtration, UV treatment — makes water safe for drinking.
Vaccination prevents infectious diseases by stimulating immune system to produce antibodies.

key_points: Handwashing with soap for 20 seconds removes 99% of pathogens.
Pasteurisation (63°C for 30 min or 72°C for 15 sec): kills harmful bacteria in milk.
National Immunisation Programme (India): BCG (TB), OPV (Polio), DPT (Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus), Hepatitis B, MMR.
Vector control: draining stagnant water (mosquito breeding sites), using bed nets, insecticides.
ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution): water + salt + sugar — prevents dehydration in diarrhoea.

9|biology|Health_and_Hygiene_Waste_gener.overview: Waste generation is a consequence of human activity. Improper waste disposal causes pollution and disease. This chapter covers types of waste, their impact, and sustainable management strategies.

key_concepts.definition: Organic waste broken down by microorganisms: food scraps, paper, garden waste. Can be composted.
Waste that does not decompose or decomposes very slowly: plastic, glass, metals, e-waste.
The 3R approach: Reduce (use less), Reuse (use again), Recycle (convert into new material).
Discarded electronic equipment; contains toxic metals (Pb, Hg, Cd); requires special handling.

key_points: Landfill: buried waste; leachate can contaminate groundwater; methane (GHG) produced.
Incineration: burning waste reduces volume but produces toxic gases if not filtered.
Composting: aerobic decomposition of organic waste → nutrient-rich compost for agriculture.
Biogas: anaerobic decomposition of organic waste → methane for cooking/electricity.
India's Swachh Bharat Mission: focuses on open defecation free (ODF) status and solid waste management.

9|biology|Health_and_Hygiene_Sources_of_.overview: Domestic (household) waste is the primary source of municipal solid waste. Understanding what we throw away and how to classify it is the first step toward responsible waste management.

key_concepts.definition: Waste generated in households: kitchen waste, paper, plastic, glass, metal, e-waste, medical waste.
Separating waste at source into wet (biodegradable) and dry (non-biodegradable) — makes recycling and composting efficient.
Potentially infectious waste from hospitals: sharps (needles), body fluids, used bandages; must be treated separately.

key_points: Kitchen waste (vegetable peels, food scraps): biodegradable → compost pit or biogas plant.
Paper, cardboard: recyclable; reduces deforestation when recycled.
Plastic: non-biodegradable; takes 400+ years to decompose; enters food chains as microplastics.
Open burning of waste releases dioxins, furans — highly toxic carcinogens.
Informal waste pickers in India recover large amounts of recyclable material — need recognition and safety.

9|biology|Health_and_Hygiene_Methods_of_.overview: Safe disposal of waste prevents environmental pollution and disease transmission. Methods include sanitary landfills, incineration, composting, biogas, and recycling — each appropriate for different waste types.

key_concepts.definition: Engineered landfill with liner to prevent leachate contamination; daily soil cover controls odour and pests.
Biodegradable waste + microorganisms + oxygen → humus-rich compost. Aerobic (open pile) or vermicomposting (earthworms).
Organic waste decomposes anaerobically → biogas (60% methane + CO₂) for cooking/electricity; slurry used as fertiliser.

key_concepts.formula: Converting waste into new products: paper, glass, metals can be recycled multiple times; reduces resource extraction.

key_concepts.definition: Burning waste at high temperatures; reduces volume by 90%; requires advanced filters (scrubbers) to prevent air pollution.

key_points: Vermicomposting: earthworms (Eisenia fetida) break down organic waste — produces high-quality compost faster than ordinary composting.
India generates ~62 million tonnes of municipal solid waste per year; only ~25% is processed.
Secure landfill: for hazardous industrial waste — impermeable liner, leachate collection, groundwater monitoring.
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR): manufacturers responsible for end-of-life collection and recycling of their products.
Zero Waste goal: redesigning systems so waste is eliminated or recovered — the circular economy approach.

9|history_civics|history_1_The_Harappan_.overview: The Harappan (Indus Valley) Civilisation (c. 2600–1900 BCE) was one of the world's earliest urban civilisations, flourishing in the Indus basin. Its cities — Harappa and Mohenjo-daro — showed remarkable town planning, drainage, and standardisation.

key_concepts.definition: Bronze Age urban civilisation along the Indus River (modern Pakistan and northwest India); also called Harappan after the first site excavated.
Cities had a grid pattern — streets at right angles; a raised Citadel (rulers/religious) and a lower town (houses/granaries); burnt-brick construction.
Large public bath suggesting ritual cleansing; evidence of a sophisticated water management and drainage system.
Square steatite (soapstone) seals with animal figures (unicorn, bull) and undeciphered script; used in trade and administration.
Possible causes: climate change, drying of the Saraswati River, floods, Aryan migration; around 1900 BCE.

key_points: Major sites: Harappa (Punjab), Mohenjo-daro (Sindh), Lothal (Gujarat — has dockyard), Kalibangan (Rajasthan), Dholavira (Gujarat).
Economy: agriculture (wheat, barley, cotton — first cotton growers), trade (with Mesopotamia — seals found there).
No evidence of temples or large religious structures; possible priest-king rulers.
Standardised weights and measures — evidence of central administration.
The script remains undeciphered — written right to left (boustrophedon sometimes).

9|history_civics|history_2_The_Vedic_Per.overview: The Vedic Period (c. 1500–600 BCE) covers the period of Aryan settlement in India and the composition of the Vedas. The Early Vedic period saw pastoral, semi-nomadic societies; the Later Vedic period saw settled agriculture, iron use, and a more rigid caste system.

key_concepts.definition: Indo-European speaking people who entered India c. 1500 BCE; pastoral, tribal society; composed the Rigveda.
Sacred texts: Rigveda (hymns to gods — oldest), Samaveda (melodies), Yajurveda (rituals), Atharvaveda (charms/spells).
Four social divisions: Brahmin (priests/scholars), Kshatriya (warriors/rulers), Vaishya (merchants/farmers), Shudra (servants). Became hereditary in later Vedic period.
Early tribal assemblies — Sabha (elder council) and Samiti (general assembly) checked royal power.
Settlement in Ganga plains; iron use (black-red ware pottery); rise of janapadas (territorial kingdoms); Upanishads (philosophical texts).

key_points: Rigveda: composed c. 1500–1200 BCE; 1028 hymns; oldest literary text of Indian subcontinent.
Panini composed Ashtadhyayi — the first scientific grammar of Sanskrit — in the later Vedic period.
Upanishads introduced concepts of Brahman (universal spirit) and Atman (individual soul) — basis of Hindu philosophy.
Economy shifted from cattle-rearing to settled agriculture; barley (yava) was the main crop.
Ashrama system: Brahmacharya (student), Grihastha (householder), Vanaprastha (forest dweller), Sannyasa (renunciant).

9|history_civics|history_3_Jainism_and_B.overview: Jainism and Buddhism arose in the 6th century BCE as reform movements against Vedic ritualism and the caste system. Both emphasised non-violence, ethical living, and personal liberation through one's own efforts, not divine grace.

key_concepts.definition: 24th Tirthankara (ford-maker); born c. 599 BCE in Vaishali; attained Kevalajnana (enlightenment); preached Five Vows (Ahimsa, Satya, Asteya, Brahmacharya, Aparigraha).
Born c. 563 BCE in Lumbini (Nepal); attained enlightenment at Bodh Gaya; first sermon at Sarnath (Dhamma Chakra Pravartan); died at Kushinagar.
1. Life is suffering (Dukkha); 2. Suffering has a cause (desire/attachment); 3. Suffering can end; 4. The Eight-Fold Path ends suffering.
Right View, Intention, Speech, Action, Livelihood, Effort, Mindfulness, Concentration — the Buddhist way to Nirvana.
Right Faith, Right Knowledge, Right Conduct — the path to liberation (Moksha) in Jainism.

key_points: Both rejected Vedic rituals and caste system; preached in Prakrit (common people's language), not Sanskrit.
Jainism: extreme Ahimsa (non-violence) — monks strain water before drinking, sweep path to avoid stepping on insects.
Buddhism: Theravada (older, individual liberation) vs Mahayana (later, universal compassion/Bodhisattvas) schools.
Buddhism spread: Ashoka sent missionaries to Sri Lanka, Greece, Egypt; Buddhist councils compiled texts.
Jain texts: Agamas; Buddhist canon: Tripitaka (Vinaya, Sutta, Abhidhamma Pitakas).

9|history_civics|history_4_The_Mauryan_E.overview: The Mauryan Empire (321–185 BCE) was India's first great empire, founded by Chandragupta Maurya and reaching its peak under Ashoka. It unified much of the subcontinent under a centralized administration.

key_concepts.definition: Founder of Mauryan Empire (321 BCE); with Chanakya's guidance defeated the Nanda dynasty; later abdicated and became a Jain monk.
Treatise on statecraft, economy, and military strategy by Chanakya (Kautilya); describes the Mauryan administrative system.
Third Mauryan emperor (268–232 BCE); converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE); spread Dhamma through edicts on rocks and pillars.
Moral code: non-violence, religious tolerance, respect for elders, care for animals and the poor — not Buddhism specifically but ethical principles.
Greek ambassador to Chandragupta's court; wrote 'Indica' — a valuable account of Mauryan India.

key_points: Pataliputra (modern Patna) was the Mauryan capital; described as one of the world's largest cities c. 300 BCE.
Mauryan economy: agriculture taxed, trade flourishing, well-maintained roads (Uttarapatha highway).
Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE): 100,000 killed, 150,000 deported — Ashoka's transformation to Dhamma began.
Ashoka's missions: son Mahinda to Sri Lanka, daughter Sanghamitra with the Bodhi tree.
Decline: after Ashoka, weak successors; last king Brihadratha was killed by general Pushyamitra Shunga (185 BCE).

9|history_civics|history_5_The_Sangam_Ag.overview: The Sangam Age (c. 300 BCE – 300 CE) was a golden period of Tamil culture in South India. Named after the Sangams (literary academies), it is known through a rich corpus of Tamil poetry covering love, war, and society.

key_concepts.definition: Literary academies held under royal patronage in Madurai; three Sangams recorded; produced Sangam literature (Akam — love poetry; Puram — war/heroic poetry).
Chera (Kerala — trade with Rome), Chola (Kaveri delta — Kaveripattinam port), Pandya (Madurai — pearl trade).
Sangam poetry linked themes to five landscapes: Kurinji (mountains–love), Mullai (forests–waiting), Marudam (fields–quarrel), Neydal (seashore–separation), Palai (desert–hardship).
Earliest surviving Tamil grammar and literary treatise; authored by Tolkappiyar.

key_points: Sangam literature — major works: Tirukkural (Thiruvalluvar — ethics, politics, love), Purananuru, Akananuru.
Trade with Rome: Roman gold coins found at Puhar and Arikamedu; exported pepper, muslin, ivory.
Women's role: Sangam poetry mentions women warriors, poets (Avvaiyar), and educated women.
Capital cities: Vanji (Chera), Uraiyur then Kaveripattinam (Chola), Madurai (Pandya).
Society: caste less rigid than north; warriors (Kshatriyas) highly respected; farmers important.

9|history_civics|history_6_The_Age_of_th.overview: The Gupta Empire (c. 320–550 CE) is called the 'Golden Age of India' for achievements in science, mathematics, literature, and art. Chandragupta I founded it; Samudragupta and Chandragupta II expanded and consolidated it.

key_concepts.definition: Founded the Gupta Empire c. 320 CE; took the title 'Maharajadhiraja'; married Lichchhavi princess Kumaradevi.
The 'Napoleon of India'; military genius who expanded the empire; also a poet and musician; described in Allahabad Pillar Inscription by Harisena.
Defeated the Sakas; empire at greatest extent; Fa Hien (Chinese pilgrim) visited during his reign and described prosperity.
Great Buddhist university established in Gupta era; attracted students from China, Central Asia; destroyed by Bakhtiyar Khilji (1193 CE).
Gupta-era mathematician/astronomer: calculated value of π (3.1416), explained solar/lunar eclipses, proposed Earth rotates on its axis, calculated length of year.

key_points: Literary achievements: Kalidasa (Abhijnanashakuntalam, Meghadutam, Kumarsambhavam), Vishakadatta (Mudrarakshasa).
Mathematical contributions: decimal system, zero (as a numeral), negative numbers; Brahmagupta — quadratic equations.
Art: Ajanta cave paintings (Mahayana Buddhist themes); Sarnath, Mathura schools of sculpture.
Decline: Huna (White Hun) invasions; internal weaknesses; last Gupta king — Skandagupta weakened by invasions.
Iron Pillar of Delhi (in Qutub Minar complex): forged during Gupta period; remarkably rust-free for 1,600 years.

9|history_civics|history_7_Medieval_Indi.overview: The Delhi Sultanate (1206–1526 CE) was established after Muhammad of Ghor's conquests and lasted through five dynasties. It introduced Persian-Islamic culture to India and shaped medieval Indian history.

key_concepts.definition: Slave (Mamluk) dynasty → Khilji dynasty → Tughlaq dynasty → Sayyid dynasty → Lodi dynasty (1206–1526 CE).
Third Slave dynasty sultan; consolidated the Delhi Sultanate; received Khalifa's investiture; shifted capital from Lahore to Delhi.
Most powerful Khilji sultan; market reforms (fixed prices, spies to enforce), military campaigns (Malik Kafur to south India), repelled Mongol invasions.
Brilliant but erratic; shifted capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (failed), introduced token currency (failed), raised taxes in Doab.
Land assignment to officials in lieu of salary; assigned territory collected revenue and maintained troops; predecessor of the Mughal mansabdari.

key_points: Battle of Tarain (1192): Muhammad of Ghor defeated Prithviraj Chauhan — opened the way for the Delhi Sultanate.
Razia Sultana: first and only woman sultan of Delhi (Iltutmish's daughter); ruled 1236–1240 CE.
Alai Darwaza and the incomplete Qutb Minar extension are Alauddin Khilji's architectural contributions.
Ibn Battuta visited during Muhammad bin Tughlaq's reign; wrote 'Rihla' — vivid account of the Sultanate.
First Battle of Panipat (1526): Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi with gunpowder — ended the Sultanate.

9|history_civics|history_8_Medieval_Indi.overview: The Mughal Empire (1526–1857) was the dominant power in India for over two centuries. Founded by Babur, it reached its zenith under Akbar and Aurangzeb. Known for administrative efficiency, art, architecture, and religious policy.

key_concepts.definition: Founded Mughal Empire after Battle of Panipat (1526); defeated Rana Sanga at Khanwa (1527); memoirs = Baburnama.
Greatest Mughal emperor; abolished jizya tax; created Din-i-Ilahi (syncretic religion); Mansabdari system; Navratnas (nine gems at court including Birbal, Tansen, Todar Mal).
Akbar's administrative system: nobles (mansabdars) given ranks (mansab) with dual classification (zat — personal rank; sawar — cavalry command).
Built the Taj Mahal (1632–1653) as mausoleum for wife Mumtaz Mahal; Red Fort, Jama Masjid — height of Mughal architecture.
Last great Mughal; re-imposed jizya, destroyed some temples; Deccan campaigns drained treasury; empire began declining after his death (1707).

key_points: Three Battles of Panipat: 1526 (Babur vs Lodi), 1556 (Akbar/Bairam Khan vs Hemu), 1761 (Ahmad Shah Durrani vs Marathas).
Revenue system: Todar Mal introduced Ain-i-Dahsala (ten-year revenue settlement), measurement of land.
Mughal art: miniature painting (Persian influence + Indian themes); Akbarnama by Abul Fazl.
Din-i-Ilahi: Akbar's attempt to create a universal religion — largely rejected; only 18 followers.
Decline after Aurangzeb: Maratha power, Sikh power, internal revolts, Nadir Shah's invasion (1739 — took Peacock Throne).

9|history_civics|history_9_The_Making_of.overview: The Indian Constitution was drafted by the Constituent Assembly (1946–1949) under B.R. Ambedkar's chairmanship. It came into force on 26 January 1950, making India a sovereign democratic republic.

key_concepts.definition: Body of elected representatives that drafted the Constitution; 299 members; met from December 1946; chaired by Dr Rajendra Prasad.
Chaired by Dr B.R. Ambedkar; prepared the actual draft; Ambedkar called 'Father of the Indian Constitution'.
Introduction to the Constitution; declares India as Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic; ensures Justice, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity.
UK: Westminster model, parliamentary system; USA: Fundamental Rights, judicial review; Ireland: DPSP; Canada: federalism; Australia: concurrent list.

key_points: The Constituent Assembly took 2 years, 11 months, 18 days to draft the Constitution.
Original Constitution: 395 articles, 8 schedules; now has 448 articles, 12 schedules (after amendments).
26 November 1949: Constitution adopted; 26 January 1950: Constitution came into force — Republic Day.
Objective Resolution (moved by Nehru, 1946): outlined the goals — basis of the Preamble.
The word 'secular' and 'socialist' were added to the Preamble by the 42nd Amendment (1976).

9|history_civics|history_10_Elections_an.overview: Free and fair elections are the foundation of Indian democracy. The Election Commission of India oversees elections using universal adult franchise. Political parties aggregate interests and compete for power.

key_concepts.definition: Every citizen aged 18+ has the right to vote regardless of caste, religion, gender, or economic status.
Constitutional body (Article 324) responsible for conducting free and fair elections; headed by Chief Election Commissioner; independent of government.
Electoral system used in India for Lok Sabha and State Assembly elections: candidate with most votes wins, even without absolute majority.
Guidelines for parties and candidates during election period: no use of government resources for campaigning, no inflammatory speeches.

key_points: Lok Sabha elections: every 5 years (unless dissolved earlier); 543 elected constituencies.
Rajya Sabha: not directly elected; members elected by State Legislative Assemblies; 1/3 retire every 2 years.
Recognised National Parties must meet ECI criteria: vote share or seat share threshold.
Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs) replaced ballot papers; VVPAT (Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail) added transparency.
Election Commission powers: announce schedule, enforce code of conduct, redraw constituency boundaries (delimitation).

9|history_civics|civics_1_Our_Constituti.overview: The Constitution is the supreme law of India. It is a written, federal, rigid yet flexible document that establishes the structure of government, fundamental rights, and the relationship between the state and citizens.

key_concepts.definition: Two levels of government (Centre + States); written constitution; division of powers (Union, State, Concurrent Lists); independent judiciary.
Strong centre (residual powers with Centre); single citizenship; integrated judiciary; All-India Services; Governor appointed by President.
22 Parts (now); key parts: III (Fundamental Rights), IV (DPSP), IV-A (Fundamental Duties), V-VI (Union/State Governments), XI-XII (Centre-State relations).
Article 368: Simple majority (some provisions), Special majority (2/3 of Parliament + majority of total strength), + State ratification (some provisions).

key_points: The Constitution is the longest written constitution of any sovereign nation.
Fundamental Rights (Part III, Articles 12–35): justiceable (enforceable by courts).
DPSP (Part IV): non-justiceable but fundamental to governance — guide state policy.
Fundamental Duties (Part IVA, Article 51A): added by 42nd Amendment (1976); 11 duties of citizens.
Basic Structure Doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati case, 1973): Parliament cannot amend the 'basic structure' of the Constitution.

9|history_civics|civics_2_Fundamental_Ri.overview: Fundamental Rights (Part III) are justiciable rights guaranteed against state action. Directive Principles (Part IV) are non-justiciable guidelines for the state. Together they form the 'conscience' of the Constitution.

key_concepts.definition: Equality before law; no discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth; abolition of untouchability (Article 17); no titles.
6 freedoms (speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, profession); protection against arbitrary arrest.
Prohibition of trafficking, forced labour (begar); prohibition of child labour in factories/hazardous work.
Dr Ambedkar called it 'heart and soul of Constitution'; right to approach Supreme Court to enforce Fundamental Rights; writs: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto.
Directions to the state for socio-economic justice: equal pay, living wage, free legal aid, uniform civil code, protection of environment, international peace.

key_points: Right to Property: was a Fundamental Right (Article 31); removed by 44th Amendment (1978) — now a legal right (Article 300A).
Right to Education (Article 21A): free and compulsory education for children 6–14 years — added by 86th Amendment (2002).
Habeas Corpus writ: 'produce the body' — commands that an arrested person be brought to court.
Mandamus: 'we command' — orders a public authority to do its duty.
DPSP vs Fundamental Rights: in case of conflict, Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights to implement DPSP (after Golak Nath reversal by 25th Amendment).

9|history_civics|civics_3_Elections_and_.overview: The Election Commission of India is an autonomous constitutional body that ensures free and fair elections. It manages the entire electoral process from voter registration to the announcement of results.

key_concepts.definition: Article 324 establishes the Election Commission; the Chief Election Commissioner can only be removed by the same process as a Supreme Court judge.
List of registered voters; maintained and updated by the ECI; every citizen 18+ eligible (unless disqualified).
Set of guidelines binding on all political parties and candidates from announcement of election to result declaration.
Electronic Voting Machine records votes electronically; VVPAT (Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail) lets voters verify their vote was recorded correctly.

key_points: The ECI is multi-member body: 1 Chief Election Commissioner + 2 Election Commissioners.
ECI powers: schedule elections, recognise parties (national/state), allot symbols, enforce code of conduct, order re-polls.
Voter ID card (EPIC — Electoral Photo Identity Card) is primary proof of registration.
Disqualification: mentally unsound, imprisonment, criminal conviction, holding office of profit.
Proportional Representation (PR): used for President/Vice-President elections and Rajya Sabha — not FPTP.

9|history_civics|civics_4_Local_Self-Gov.overview: Rural local self-government (Panchayati Raj) was constitutionalised by the 73rd Amendment (1992). It is the third tier of government — gram (village) level, block level, and district level — aimed at democratic decentralisation.

key_concepts.definition: Added Part IX and 11th Schedule to the Constitution; gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj institutions; made elections mandatory.
Village-level body (5,000–15,000 population); headed by Sarpanch/Gram Panchayat President; responsible for local development, sanitation, roads.
Middle tier; coordinates Gram Panchayats; implements government schemes; education, health, agriculture at block level.
Apex of three-tier system; oversees the entire district; larger developmental schemes; headed by an elected president.
All voters of a village together form the Gram Sabha — the foundation of Panchayati Raj; elects Gram Panchayat members.

key_points: Reservation: minimum 1/3 seats (now 50% in many states) reserved for women; reservation for SC/ST proportional to population.
State Election Commission (Article 243K): conducts Panchayat elections, separate from ECI.
State Finance Commission: reviews and recommends devolution of funds to Panchayats.
11th Schedule: 29 subjects transferred to Panchayats (agriculture, education, water supply, roads, poverty alleviation etc.).
Balwantrai Mehta Committee (1957) recommended three-tier Panchayati Raj; first implemented in Rajasthan (1959).

9|history_civics|civics_5_Local_Self-Gov.overview: Urban local self-government manages cities and towns. The 74th Constitutional Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) — Nagar Panchayats, Municipal Councils, and Municipal Corporations.

key_concepts.definition: Added Part IX-A and 12th Schedule; constitutionalised urban local bodies; mandated elections every 5 years.
For transitional areas (rural to urban); smallest urban body; handles basic civic functions.
For smaller urban areas; elected councillors; headed by a Chairperson/President; handles sanitation, water, roads, markets.
For large cities (population >1 lakh or as specified by state); Mayor (elected, largely ceremonial) and Municipal Commissioner (IAS officer, executive power).
Sub-unit of Municipal Corporation; makes urban local bodies more responsive at ward level.

key_points: 12th Schedule: 18 subjects including urban planning, regulation of land use, slum improvement, public health, roads, water supply.
Mayor vs Municipal Commissioner: Mayor is politically elected head; Commissioner is the administrative/executive head (IAS).
Property tax is the main source of revenue for Municipal Corporations.
State Finance Commission: recommends sharing of taxes between state and urban local bodies.
Smart Cities Mission: Indian government initiative to develop 100 smart cities with ICT-enabled infrastructure and efficient urban services.

9|geography|1_Earth_as_a_Planet.overview: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only known planet to support life. This chapter covers Earth's basic characteristics: shape, size, motions, and its position in the solar system.

key_concepts.definition: Earth is not a perfect sphere — it's an oblate spheroid: flattened at poles (polar radius: 6,357 km) and bulging at equator (equatorial radius: 6,378 km).
The Sun + 8 planets + dwarf planets + moons + asteroids + comets. Earth is 3rd planet; average distance from Sun = 150 million km (1 AU).
Rotation (spinning on axis — 24 hours) and Revolution (orbiting Sun — 365.25 days).

key_points: Evidence Earth is spherical: horizon, ship disappearing hull-first, circular shadow on Moon during lunar eclipse, circumnavigation.
Earth's axis is tilted at 23.5° to its orbital plane — causes seasons.
Surface: 71% water (oceans), 29% land; 5 oceans (Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern, Arctic).
Mass of Earth: 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg; Circumference: ~40,075 km at equator.
Moon is Earth's natural satellite; average distance ~384,400 km; rotates and revolves in same period (~27.3 days) — same face always visible.

9|geography|2_Geographic_Grid_Latitudes_.overview: The geographic grid of latitudes and longitudes is a coordinate system that locates any point on Earth's surface. Understanding this system is essential for map reading, navigation, and understanding time zones.

key_concepts.definition: Angular distance north or south of the Equator; measured in degrees (0° – 90°N/S). Key parallels: Equator (0°), Tropics of Cancer/Capricorn (23.5°N/S), Arctic/Antarctic Circles (66.5°N/S).
Angular distance east or west of the Prime Meridian (0° at Greenwich); measured in degrees (0°–180°E/W).
0° longitude passing through Greenwich, London; basis for world time zones (Greenwich Mean Time/UTC).
A circle formed by any plane passing through Earth's centre; the Equator is a great circle; used for shortest-route navigation.
All other latitudes (except the Equator) are small circles — they do not pass through Earth's centre.

key_points: 1° of latitude = approx. 111 km; latitude lines are always parallel (same distance apart).
Longitude lines (meridians) converge at poles; are not parallel to each other.
International Date Line: 180° meridian (with diversions); crossing westward gains a day; crossing eastward loses a day.
India: 8°4'N – 37°6'N latitude; 68°7'E – 97°25'E longitude.
Time calculation: for every 1° of longitude, time difference = 4 minutes; 15° = 1 hour.

9|geography|3_Rotation_and_Revolution.overview: Earth's two main motions — rotation (spinning on its axis) and revolution (orbiting the Sun) — are responsible for day and night, the four seasons, and varying lengths of day and night throughout the year.

key_concepts.definition: Earth rotates west to east (anticlockwise from North Pole); completes one rotation in 23 hours 56 minutes 4 seconds (sidereal day) ≈ 24 hours (solar day).
Earth orbits the Sun in 365 days 5 hours 48 minutes — called the tropical year; orbit is slightly elliptical.
Perihelion: Earth closest to Sun (147 million km) — January 3; Aphelion: Earth farthest (152 million km) — July 4.
When the Sun is directly above the Equator; day and night are equal (12 hours each) everywhere. March 21 (Vernal/Spring) and September 23 (Autumnal).
When the Sun is farthest north or south of Equator. June 21 (Summer Solstice): Sun over Tropic of Cancer; longest day in Northern Hemisphere. December 22 (Winter Solstice): shortest day.

laws_and_formulas.name: Speed of Earth's rotation
laws_and_formulas.formula: At equator: ~1,670 km/h; decreases toward poles (cos θ × 1,670 km/h)

key_points: Effects of rotation: day and night; deflection of winds and currents (Coriolis Effect); slight flattening at poles.
Effects of revolution: seasons; change in length of day and night; different constellations visible at different times.
Leap year: every 4 years (366 days) to account for the extra 6 hours/year — except century years not divisible by 400.
Northern Hemisphere summer: Earth is farther from Sun — seasons caused by tilt, not distance.
Antarctica: six months of continuous daylight (October–March) and six months of night — due to tilt.

9|geography|4_Earths_Structure.overview: Earth's interior has a layered structure discovered through seismic wave analysis. From the surface inward: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core. Each layer has distinct composition, density, and temperature.

key_concepts.definition: Outermost layer; continental crust (SiAl — silica + aluminium; 30–50 km thick; less dense); oceanic crust (SiMa — silica + magnesium; 5–10 km; denser, basaltic).
Largest layer (2,900 km thick); solid rock (peridotite); upper mantle has the asthenosphere (plastic/semi-molten) on which plates float.
Outer core: liquid (iron + nickel); generates Earth's magnetic field. Inner core: solid iron-nickel; very high pressure (3.5 million atm) keeps it solid despite extreme heat.
P-waves (primary/compressional): travel through solid and liquid. S-waves (secondary/shear): travel only through solid — their absence in outer core proves it is liquid.

key_points: Temperature and pressure increase with depth; core temperature ~5,000–6,000°C.
Moho (Mohorovicic Discontinuity): boundary between crust and mantle.
Gutenberg Discontinuity: boundary between mantle and outer core (2,900 km depth).
Lehmann Discontinuity: boundary between outer and inner core (5,100 km depth).
Isostasy: the principle that crust 'floats' on the mantle — thicker continental crust stands higher above sea level.

9|geography|5_Landforms_of_the_Earth.overview: Earth's surface features (landforms) include mountains, plateaus, plains, valleys, and coastal features. They are formed by endogenic (internal) forces like tectonic activity and exogenic (external) forces like erosion and deposition.

key_concepts.definition: Formed by compression of sedimentary rocks at convergent plate boundaries: Himalayas (India–Asia collision), Alps, Andes, Rockies.
Formed by faulting: a block is uplifted (horst = block mountain) between two normal faults; adjacent blocks drop (graben = rift valley). E.g., Rhine Valley, Great African Rift Valley.
A flat-topped elevated landform; can be formed by lava flows (Deccan Plateau, India), erosion, or tectonic uplift.
Low, flat landforms formed by deposition: alluvial plains (rivers — North Indian Plain), coastal plains, glacial plains.

key_points: Volcano mountains (Fiji type): formed by accumulation of lava and ash — Kilimanjaro, Mt Fuji.
V-shaped valley: formed by river erosion (upper course); U-shaped valley: glacial erosion.
Escarpment: a steep slope from plateau edge down to lower land.
Delta: triangular deposit where a river meets the sea; formed when sediment deposition rate > erosion.
Atoll: ring-shaped coral island/reef around a lagoon — subsided volcanic island base.

9|geography|6_Rocks_and_Rock_Cycle.overview: Rocks are aggregates of minerals. The three rock types — igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic — are continuously transformed into one another through the rock cycle, driven by heat, pressure, and weathering.

key_concepts.definition: Formed by cooling and solidification of magma/lava. Intrusive (plutonic): cool slowly underground → large crystals (granite, diorite). Extrusive (volcanic): cool quickly at surface → small crystals/glassy (basalt, obsidian).
Formed by deposition and cementation of sediments (rock fragments, minerals, organic material); layered (strata); contain fossils. Examples: sandstone, limestone, shale, coal.
Formed when existing rocks are subjected to high heat and/or pressure: limestone → marble; shale → slate; granite → gneiss; sandstone → quartzite; coal → graphite → diamond.
The continuous process by which rocks are transformed between types: magma → igneous → weathering → sediment → sedimentary → metamorphic → melting → magma.

key_points: Granite: coarse-grained intrusive igneous rock; used in buildings and monuments.
Basalt: fine-grained extrusive igneous rock; makes up ocean floor and Deccan Plateau.
Limestone: sedimentary rock (calcium carbonate); forms by compaction of shell/coral fragments; used in cement.
Coal: sedimentary rock; compressed ancient plant matter; key fossil fuel.
Diamond and graphite: both forms of pure carbon (metamorphic); different arrangement of atoms gives different properties.

9|geography|7_Volcanoes_and_Earthquakes.overview: Volcanoes and earthquakes are endogenic (internal) forces that shape Earth's surface. Both are largely concentrated at tectonic plate boundaries and result from movements in the lithosphere.

key_concepts.definition: Sudden release of energy in Earth's crust creating seismic waves. Focus (hypocenter): where earthquake originates underground. Epicenter: point on surface directly above focus.
Instrument that records seismic waves; used to locate epicenter and measure earthquake magnitude.
Logarithmic scale measuring earthquake magnitude from 1–9+; each step = 10× more energy; below 3.0: usually not felt; above 7.0: major damage.
A vent in the crust through which magma, gases, and ash erupt. Active (currently erupting), Dormant (not erupting but could), Extinct (unlikely to erupt again).
Belt of volcanoes and earthquakes around the Pacific Ocean; 75% of world's volcanoes and 90% of earthquakes occur here.

key_points: Plate boundaries: convergent (subduction — deep earthquakes), divergent (rift — volcanoes), transform (horizontal movement — shallow earthquakes, e.g., San Andreas Fault).
Tsunami: 'harbour wave'; triggered by submarine earthquakes, landslides, or volcanic eruptions; speeds of 700–800 km/h in open ocean.
Lava types: pahoehoe (smooth, basaltic); aa (rough, jagged); pillow lava (underwater).
Caldera: large depression formed when a magma chamber collapses after an eruption.
Composite/Stratovolcano: alternating layers of lava and ash — Mt Fuji, Mt Vesuvius. Shield volcano: gentle slopes, runny lava — Hawaiian volcanoes.

9|geography|8_Weathering_and_Soil_Format.overview: Weathering is the breakdown of rocks in place by physical, chemical, and biological processes. It produces the raw material (regolith) from which soil forms over thousands of years.

key_concepts.definition: Breakdown without chemical change: freeze-thaw (frost action), thermal expansion/contraction (insolation weathering), pressure release (exfoliation), abrasion.
Breakdown with chemical change: hydrolysis (feldspar → clay minerals), oxidation (iron minerals → red iron oxide/rust), carbonation (limestone dissolved by carbonic acid).
Breakdown by living organisms: plant roots splitting rocks, burrowing animals, lichens producing acids.
Soil develops from weathered rock (parent material) + organic matter + climate + organisms + time. Horizon A (topsoil), B (subsoil), C (weathered parent rock).

key_points: Soil Profile: O (organic/humus) → A (topsoil — nutrients, organisms) → B (subsoil — minerals leached from A) → C (weathered parent rock) → R (bedrock).
Factors affecting soil formation: parent rock, climate (rain/temperature), vegetation, organisms, topography, time.
Red soils (tropical): high iron (hematite) — drained, less fertile. Black soils (Deccan, India): moisture-retaining, cotton cultivation.
Soil erosion: caused by water, wind; accelerated by deforestation and poor farming practices.
Humus: decomposed organic matter; improves soil fertility, water retention, and structure.

9|geography|9_Hydrosphere.overview: The hydrosphere encompasses all water on, above, and below Earth's surface. This includes oceans (97.5% of all water), freshwater (2.5% — glaciers, groundwater, rivers, lakes), and the water cycle.

key_concepts.definition: Continuous movement of water: evaporation → condensation → precipitation → runoff → infiltration → evaporation.

Large-scale continuous water movements driven by wind, temperature, salinity differences, and Earth's rotation. Warm currents (Gulf Stream) raise coastal temperatures; cold currents (Labrador) cool coasts.
Rise and fall of sea level caused by gravitational pull of Moon and Sun. Spring tides (new/full moon): highest tides. Neap tides (quarter moons): lowest range.
Sunlit zone (photic, 0–200m): photosynthesis; Twilight zone (200–1000m); Midnight zone (1000–4000m); Abyssal zone (4000–6000m); Hadal zone (trenches >6000m).

key_points: Pacific Ocean: largest and deepest; Mariana Trench (deepest point — 11,034m).
Salinity: average 35 ppt (parts per thousand); higher in Red Sea (~40 ppt), lower in Baltic (<10 ppt).
Groundwater: water stored in aquifers below the water table; tapped by wells and borewells.
Glacier: moving mass of ice formed by compaction of snow; store 69% of all freshwater.
Coriolis Effect: currents deflect to the right in Northern Hemisphere, left in Southern Hemisphere.

9|geography|10_Atmosphere.overview: The atmosphere is the layer of gases surrounding Earth held by gravity. It protects life from UV radiation, meteor impacts, and temperature extremes. Its composition and layers are fundamental to understanding weather and climate.

key_concepts.definition: Nitrogen 78%, Oxygen 21%, Argon 0.93%, CO₂ 0.04%, trace gases (Ozone, water vapour, etc.).
Lowest layer (0–12 km); contains all weather; temperature decreases with altitude (~6.5°C/km = Environmental Lapse Rate).
12–50 km; contains ozone layer (15–35 km) absorbing UV radiation; temperature increases with altitude (UV absorption by ozone).
50–80 km; coldest layer (−90°C at top); meteorites burn up here.
80–600 km; very high temperatures (up to 2000°C) due to absorption of X-rays and UV; aurora phenomena; radio wave reflection.

key_points: Ozone layer (O₃): absorbs harmful UV radiation; thinning (ozone hole) caused by CFCs — banned by Montreal Protocol (1987).
Weather occurs in troposphere; most aircraft cruise in lower stratosphere (stable, above weather).
The Exosphere (above 600 km): gases escape to space; satellites orbit here.
Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, water vapour): trap heat — essential for life but excess causes global warming.
Atmospheric pressure: 1013.25 mb at sea level; decreases with altitude.

9|geography|11_Insolation_and_Atmospheri.overview: Insolation (incoming solar radiation) is the primary source of energy for the atmosphere. The unequal distribution of insolation with latitude drives atmospheric circulation and determines temperature patterns globally.

key_concepts.definition: Solar energy received by Earth's surface; varies with: angle of Sun's rays (more direct = more intense), day length, atmospheric transparency.
Fraction of insolation reflected back to space: fresh snow ~90%, clouds ~50%, ocean ~6%, forests ~15%. Higher albedo = less heating.
Of solar radiation reaching Earth: ~30% reflected (albedo), ~20% absorbed by atmosphere, ~50% absorbed by surface.
Normal: temperature decreases with altitude. Inversion: temperature increases with altitude (cold dense air trapped near surface below warm air) — causes fog, smog.

key_points: Equatorial regions receive most insolation (Sun nearly overhead year-round); poles receive least.
Land heats and cools faster than water (specific heat capacity of water is higher) — causes monsoons and sea breezes.
Isotherms: lines joining places of equal temperature; generally parallel to latitude lines but deflect over continents.
July isotherms: highest temperature over continental interiors (40°N); oceans warm slowly.
January: temperature is highest in Southern Hemisphere (summer there); landmasses cool faster.

9|geography|12_Atmospheric_Pressure_and_.overview: Atmospheric pressure is the weight of air above a given point. Differences in pressure cause winds — air moves from high pressure to low pressure. The Coriolis Effect deflects winds, creating the global wind belts.

key_concepts.definition: Weight of column of air above a unit area; measured in millibars (mb) or pascals (Pa); 1013.25 mb at sea level; decreases with altitude.
Equatorial Low (0°) → Subtropical High (30°N/S) → Sub-polar Low (60°N/S) → Polar High (90°N/S). Driven by temperature differences and Earth's rotation.
Blow from Subtropical Highs toward Equatorial Low; NE trades in Northern Hemisphere; SE trades in Southern Hemisphere; consistent and reliable.
Blow from Subtropical Highs toward Sub-polar Lows; west to east in 40°–60° latitudes; responsible for weather in mid-latitudes.
Deflection of winds/currents due to Earth's rotation — to the right in Northern Hemisphere, left in Southern Hemisphere.

key_points: Isobar: line connecting places of equal atmospheric pressure on a weather map.
Cyclone: low-pressure system; winds spiral inward and upward; brings clouds and rain; anticlockwise in NH, clockwise in SH.
Anticyclone: high-pressure system; winds spiral outward; generally clear, dry weather.
Jet streams: high-altitude (9–16 km) fast-moving winds (>300 km/h) in the upper troposphere; influence weather systems below.
Land breeze vs Sea breeze: daytime — cooler sea → sea breeze (sea → land); night — land cools faster → land breeze (land → sea).

9|geography|13_Humidity_and_Precipitatio.overview: Humidity measures water vapour content in the atmosphere. When air reaches its dew point, water vapour condenses to form clouds. Precipitation occurs when water droplets/ice crystals grow heavy enough to fall as rain, snow, hail, or sleet.

key_concepts.definition: Ratio of actual water vapour present to maximum water vapour air can hold at that temperature, expressed as %; 100% RH = saturated air (dew point reached).
Temperature at which air becomes saturated and condensation begins.

Convectional (heated surface rises → cools → rain — tropics, afternoon); Relief/Orographic (moist air forced over mountains → windward side rain; leeward side dry — rain shadow); Frontal/Cyclonic (warm and cold air masses meet → rain).
High clouds: cirrus (ice crystals); Middle: altostratus, altocumulus; Low: stratus (sheets), cumulus (puffy), cumulonimbus (thunderstorm cloud — tallest).

key_points: The Western Ghats receive heavy orographic rainfall on the western windward side; eastern (leeward) side is drier — rain shadow effect.
Hail: formed in cumulonimbus clouds — ice crystals accumulate layers of ice by updrafts.
Fog: cloud at ground level; forms when air near surface cools to its dew point.
Rain gauge: instrument to measure precipitation in millimetres.
Isohyet: line on a map joining places of equal rainfall.

9|geography|14_Natural_Regions_of_the_Wo.overview: Natural regions are areas with broadly similar climate, vegetation, and soil. They are classified mainly by climate and latitude. Understanding them helps explain the distribution of biomes, agricultural patterns, and human settlement.

key_concepts.definition: 0°–5° N/S; hot (27°C all year), heavy rain (>2000mm), convectional rainfall daily; dense tropical rainforest (Amazon, Congo); biodiversity hotspot.
5°–25° N/S; distinct wet and dry seasons; monsoon winds; South and Southeast Asia; rice cultivation dominant.
20°–30° N/S; very low rainfall (<250mm); extreme temperatures; sparse xerophytic vegetation; Sahara, Arabian, Australian deserts.
30°–45° N/S on west coasts; hot dry summers, mild wet winters; scrub vegetation (maquis/chaparral); citrus fruits, grapes, olives.
Prairies (N. America), Steppes (Russia/Asia), Pampas (S. America), Veld (S. Africa); continental interior; wheat cultivation; low rainfall.

key_points: Tropical rainforest: layered canopy — emergents, canopy, understory, forest floor; lianas, epiphytes.
Tundra: 60°–75°N; permafrost; mosses, lichens, dwarf shrubs; reindeer, musk ox; very short growing season.
Taiga (Boreal Forest): 50°–65°N; coniferous forests (pine, spruce, fir); very cold winters; timber industry.
Temperate Deciduous: 40°–60°N, east coasts; four seasons; oak, beech; dense population (W. Europe, NE USA, China).
Polar Region: <70° latitude; Antarctica/Arctic; ice, snow; penguins in south; polar bears in north.

9|geography|15_Map_Work.overview: Maps are graphic representations of Earth's surface. Topographic maps use contour lines to show relief; different map projections represent the spherical Earth on a flat surface with different distortions.

key_concepts.definition: Ratio of map distance to actual distance on Earth. Representative Fraction (RF): 1:50,000 means 1 cm on map = 50,000 cm (500m) on ground.
Lines joining places of equal elevation on a topographic map; close contours = steep slope; widely spaced = gentle slope; closed circles = hill peak or depression.
Methods of representing the curved Earth surface on a flat map. No projection is perfect — all have some distortion of shape, area, distance, or direction.
Standardised symbols on maps: blue = water, green = forest, brown = contours, red = roads, black = settlements and boundaries.

key_points: Contour interval: the vertical difference between consecutive contour lines; constant throughout a map.
V-shaped contours pointing uphill: indicate a valley/river; V-shaped pointing downhill: indicate a ridge/spur.
Mercator Projection: used for navigation; correct shapes/angles but exaggerates area away from equator (Greenland appears as large as Africa).
Gnomonic Projection: great circles appear as straight lines — used for planning shortest air/ship routes.
Survey of India produces topographic maps; scale 1:25,000 for detailed and 1:250,000 for regional maps.

9|english|lang_1_Composition_Essay_Writi.overview: Essay writing is a core skill in ICSE English. A well-structured essay of 250–300 words has an introduction, body paragraphs, and conclusion. Candidates are tested on clarity of thought, vocabulary, and grammatical accuracy.

key_concepts.definition: Introduction (hook + thesis statement) → Body (3–4 paragraphs, each with a topic sentence + examples) → Conclusion (restate thesis, final thought).
Tells a story from personal experience; first person; chronological order; descriptive language.
Paints a vivid picture using sensory details (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste); appeals to imagination.
Presents both sides of a topic, then gives own opinion; requires logical reasoning and evidence.
The first sentence of each body paragraph — states the main idea of that paragraph.

key_points: Introduction: begin with a question, quote, or striking fact to grab attention; end with a clear thesis.
Each body paragraph = one idea; use connectives: 'Furthermore', 'However', 'In addition', 'On the other hand'.
Vocabulary: avoid repetition; use synonyms; vary sentence length (short for impact, long for explanation).
Conclusion: don't introduce new points; summarise and leave the reader with a thought-provoking closing line.
Plan before writing: spend 3–5 minutes outlining — this prevents going off-topic and improves structure.

9|english|lang_2_Letter_Writing_Formal_a.overview: Letter writing is a compulsory question in ICSE English Language. Formal letters follow a strict format for applications, complaints, and official correspondence; informal letters to friends/relatives are conversational in tone.

key_concepts.definition: Sender's address (top right) → Date → Recipient's address (left) → Subject → Salutation (Dear Sir/Madam) → Body → Closing (Yours faithfully) → Signature.
Sender's address (top right) → Date → Salutation (Dear [Name]) → Body → Closing (Yours lovingly/sincerely) → Signature.
Formal; clearly states the problem, when it occurred, what was promised, and what action you expect.
Formal; for job or school admission — state the position, your qualifications, and request for an interview.

key_points: Formal letter: 'Yours faithfully' if you don't know the person's name; 'Yours sincerely' if you used their name.
Never use contractions (can't, don't) or colloquial language in formal letters.
Informal letter: conversational, warm tone; contractions and idioms are fine.
Subject line (formal only): concise summary of the purpose, e.g., 'Re: Complaint regarding defective product'.
ICSE examiners check: format, relevance, fluency, and length — stay within word limit.

9|english|lang_3_Notice_Writing_and_Emai.overview: Notice writing and email writing are official communication formats tested in ICSE. A notice is a brief public announcement; an email combines formal letter structure with digital communication conventions.

key_concepts.definition: Name of school/organisation → 'NOTICE' (heading) → Date → Title → Body (what, who, when, where, how) → Name and Designation of writer.
Brief, factual, clear; no personal tone; written in third person; uses imperative sentences.
To: (recipient) → Subject: (brief, clear) → Salutation → Body (paragraphs) → Sign-off → Name.
Specific and concise: 'Request for Leave on 15th March' not just 'Leave'.

key_points: A notice is boxed on the page; title is bold and centred; keep within 50–80 words.
Common notice topics: lost and found, meetings, events, competitions, holidays.
Emails can be formal (to a teacher/employer) or semi-formal (to a classmate); match tone to recipient.
Email does NOT need sender's postal address; formal letter structure otherwise applies.
Avoid emoji, abbreviations (LOL, BTW), or informal language in formal emails.

9|english|lang_4_Comprehension_Unseen_Pa.overview: Comprehension tests reading skills — understanding main ideas, inferring meaning, vocabulary in context, and summarising. ICSE passages can be factual, literary, or argumentative.

key_concepts.definition: Direct answers found in the text — the information is explicitly stated.
Reading between the lines — the answer is implied, not stated; requires interpretation.
Understanding a word's meaning from surrounding sentences, not dictionary definition alone.
Condensing the main idea of a passage in your own words, within a given word limit (usually 1/3 of original).

key_points: Read the questions first, then the passage — you'll know what to look for.
Underline key words in questions and find corresponding sentences in the passage.
Answer in your own words — copying verbatim loses marks for vocabulary testing.
For 'give the meaning of' questions: write a synonym or brief explanation, not just the word alone.
Title suggestion questions: choose a title that covers the main idea of the WHOLE passage, not just one paragraph.

9|english|lang_5_Grammar_Tenses_Preposit.overview: Tenses, prepositions, and articles are the pillars of grammatical accuracy. ICSE tests these in fill-in-the-blank, error correction, and rewriting exercises.

key_concepts.definition: 12 tenses in English: Simple (Past/Present/Future) × 4 aspects (Simple, Continuous, Perfect, Perfect Continuous). Each expresses a specific time relationship.
Words showing relationship between nouns/pronouns and other words: at (specific point), in (enclosed), on (surface), for (duration), since (specific time), between (two), among (many).
'a' (indefinite, consonant sound); 'an' (indefinite, vowel sound); 'the' (definite — specific, previously mentioned, or unique).
Present Perfect: action with connection to present ('I have eaten'); Simple Past: completed action at specific time ('I ate yesterday').

key_points: Article rules: 'an hour' (h is silent, vowel sound); 'a university' (u sounds like 'yoo', consonant sound).
'Since' + specific point in time; 'for' + duration: 'since 2020' / 'for 3 years'.
Stative verbs (know, believe, contain, love) — don't use in continuous form.
At: specific time/place (at 5pm, at the door); In: enclosed (in a box, in March); On: surface/day (on the table, on Monday).
No article: plural/uncountable nouns used generally ('Dogs are loyal'); 'the' when both know which one ('Pass the salt').

9|english|lang_6_Grammar_Active_and_Pass.overview: Voice indicates whether the subject performs (active) or receives (passive) the action. Passive voice is common in formal, scientific, and newspaper writing. ICSE tests transformation between voices.

key_concepts.definition: Subject does the action: 'The dog bit the man.' Emphasises the doer.
Subject receives the action: 'The man was bitten by the dog.' Emphasises the receiver; doer may be omitted.
Object of active → subject; verb → 'be' (in right tense) + past participle; subject of active → 'by' phrase (optional).
The 'by + doer' phrase in passive; omitted when doer is obvious or unknown: 'The window was broken.'

laws_and_formulas.name: Active to Passive conversion
laws_and_formulas.formula: Active: S + V + O → Passive: O + be(tense) + V₃ + by S

laws_and_formulas.name: Tense of 'be' in passive
laws_and_formulas.formula: Simple Present: is/are; Simple Past: was/were; Future: will be; Present Perfect: has/have been; Present Continuous: is/are being

key_points: Simple Present active: 'She writes a letter' → passive: 'A letter is written by her'.
Simple Past: 'He broke the window' → 'The window was broken by him'.
Object pronouns in passive: him/her/them/me/us (not he/she/they/I/we).
Modal auxiliaries: 'He can do it' → 'It can be done by him'.
Verbs with no object (intransitive: sleep, die, arrive) cannot be passivised.

9|english|lang_7_Grammar_Direct_and_Indi.overview: Direct speech quotes exact words; indirect (reported) speech conveys meaning without quoting directly. Changing from direct to indirect requires tense shift, pronoun change, and time/place word changes.

key_concepts.definition: Exact words within inverted commas: She said, "I am tired."
Paraphrased without quotes: She said that she was tired.
The verb introducing the speech: said, told, asked, replied, exclaimed, ordered.
When reporting verb is past, the speech tense shifts back: am/is → was; will → would; can → could; have → had.

laws_and_formulas.name: Tense backshift rules
laws_and_formulas.formula: Present Simple → Past Simple; Present Continuous → Past Continuous; Present Perfect → Past Perfect; Past Simple → Past Perfect; will → would; can → could; may → might

laws_and_formulas.name: Time/place word changes
laws_and_formulas.formula: now → then; today → that day; here → there; this → that; tomorrow → the next day; yesterday → the day before; ago → before

key_points: Interrogative: 'Where do you live?' → He asked where I lived. (Yes/No: add 'if/whether')
Imperative: 'Sit down' → He told me to sit down. (Use infinitive)
Exclamatory: 'What a lovely day!' → She exclaimed that it was a very lovely day.
Pronoun changes: I → he/she; we → they; you → I/he/she/they (depends on context).
No tense change if reporting verb is present tense ('says') or if reporting a universal truth.

9|english|lang_8_Grammar_Transformation_.overview: Sentence transformation involves rewriting sentences in different forms (affirmative ↔ negative, simple ↔ compound ↔ complex, exclamatory ↔ assertive) without changing the meaning.

key_concepts.definition: One independent clause, one main verb: 'She sang beautifully.'
Two independent clauses joined by coordinating conjunctions (and, but, or, so, yet, for, nor).
One independent clause + one or more subordinate clauses (because, although, when, if, who, which).
Expresses strong emotion; begins with 'What a/an' or 'How'. 'What a beautiful painting!' = 'It is a very beautiful painting.'

key_points: Affirmative → Negative without meaning change: 'He is the best' → 'No one is better than him'.
Simple → Complex: 'On seeing the police, the thief ran' → 'When the thief saw the police, he ran'.
Compound → Complex: 'She was tired, so she slept' → 'She slept because she was tired'.
Degree transformation: 'He is braver than any other student' = 'No other student is as brave as he' (superlative = positive degree).
Too…to vs So…that: 'She is too young to vote' → 'She is so young that she cannot vote'.

9|english|lang_9_Grammar_Phrases_and_Cla.overview: Phrases and clauses are the building blocks of sentences. Understanding them improves writing quality and helps in error analysis, expansion, and reduction exercises in ICSE.

key_concepts.definition: A group of words without a subject-verb pair; no complete thought. Types: noun phrase, verb phrase, adjective phrase, adverb phrase, prepositional phrase.
A group of words with a subject AND a verb. Independent clause: complete thought; Subordinate clause: incomplete thought, depends on main clause.
Acts as a noun (subject/object): 'What he said was true.' (subject)
Modifies a noun; introduced by who, whom, whose, which, that: 'The boy who won the prize is my friend.'
Modifies a verb/adjective; shows time, reason, condition, contrast: 'She left before the show ended.'

key_points: Phrases: no finite verb. 'Running fast' — no subject, not a clause.
Independent clause stands alone as a sentence; subordinate clause cannot.
Reducing a clause to a phrase: 'When she arrived at the station' → 'On arriving at the station'.
Expanding a phrase to a clause: 'In spite of heavy rain' → 'Although it was raining heavily'.
Restrictive vs non-restrictive clause: 'The book that I lost was expensive' (essential) vs 'My pen, which is red, is expensive' (additional info — use commas).

9|english|lang_10_Grammar_Synonyms_and_A.overview: A rich vocabulary is essential for all sections of ICSE English. Synonyms (words of similar meaning) and antonyms (words of opposite meaning) are tested directly and improve written expression.

key_concepts.definition: A word with the same or similar meaning: brave/courageous, begin/commence, happy/elated.
A word with the opposite meaning: brave ↔ cowardly, ancient ↔ modern, transparent ↔ opaque.
The emotional or cultural meaning of a word beyond its literal meaning: 'slim' (positive) vs 'skinny' (negative) — both mean thin.
The same word can have different synonyms depending on context: 'light' (not heavy → lightweight; not dark → bright; not serious → trivial).

key_points: Common ICSE word pairs — Synonyms: abandon/forsake, anguish/torment, benevolent/charitable, candid/frank, diligent/industrious.
Common ICSE Antonyms: verbose ↔ terse, loquacious ↔ taciturn, indolent ↔ industrious, magnanimous ↔ petty.
Prefix antonyms: happy → unhappy; honest → dishonest; regular → irregular; appropriate → inappropriate.
Suffix changes: hope → hopeless; care → careless (creates negative form, sometimes antonym).
Build vocabulary: read newspapers, novels; note unfamiliar words in context; practise in sentence-building.

10|mathematics|Commercial_Mathematics_Co.overview: Commercial Mathematics at Class 10 ICSE level covers banking (recurring and compound interest), shares and dividends, GST, and linear programming. These topics directly model real-world financial situations.

key_concepts.definition: Fixed monthly deposit for a set period. Maturity value = P × n + Interest, where Interest = P × n(n+1)/(2×12) × r/100.
Face value (FV): printed value of share. Market value (MV): current price. Dividend is a % of FV declared by company. Return% = (Dividend/MV) × 100.
Indirect tax. CGST + SGST = GST (intrastate); IGST = GST (interstate). GST charged on selling price; input tax credit reduces liability.

laws_and_formulas.name: RD Maturity Value
laws_and_formulas.formula: M = P × n + P × n(n+1)/(2×12) × r/100, where P = monthly deposit, n = months, r = annual rate%

laws_and_formulas.name: Annual Income from Shares
laws_and_formulas.formula: Annual Income = (Number of shares) × FV × (dividend rate/100)

key_points: RD interest uses n(n+1)/24 factor — derived from summing 1+2+...+n months.
Shares: if dividend is on FV but you pay MV, return% ≠ dividend% when MV ≠ FV.
GST: Tax at each stage, but only value addition is taxed due to input credit system.
Compute GST: Tax payable = (GST% × selling price)/100. For a chain: each dealer pays GST on their profit margin.
Linear programming (optional enrichment): maximise/minimise objective function subject to constraints — graph the feasible region.

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Linear_Ine.overview: Linear inequations in one variable are solved and represented on a number line. The solution is a set of real or integer values satisfying the inequality, with special attention to the direction of the inequality when dividing/multiplying by negatives.

key_concepts.definition: A statement with <, >, ≤, ≥ instead of = . Solved algebraically like an equation except: multiplying/dividing by a negative reverses the inequality sign.
The set of all values satisfying the inequation. Represented on a number line with open circle (strict inequality) or closed circle (≤ or ≥).
Two simultaneous inequations solved separately; intersection gives the final solution.

laws_and_formulas.name: Reversal Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: If a < b and c < 0, then ac > bc. (Multiplying/dividing by negative reverses inequality.)

key_points: Transpose terms just as in equations — add/subtract the same thing to both sides.
When dividing by a negative number, FLIP the inequality sign.
Number line: open circle for strict (<, >); filled circle for inclusive (≤, ≥).
Replacement set: if integers only, list all integer solutions in the interval.
ICSE format: always state the solution set in set notation: {x : x ≥ 3, x ∈ R}.

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Quadratic_.overview: A quadratic equation has the form ax² + bx + c = 0 (a ≠ 0). Class 10 covers solving by factorisation and by the quadratic formula, and interpreting the nature of roots using the discriminant.

key_concepts.definition: Express ax² + bx + c as product of two linear factors: (px + q)(rx + s) = 0. Each factor = 0 gives a root.
x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / (2a). Always applicable when factorisation is not obvious.
D = b² − 4ac. D > 0: two distinct real roots; D = 0: two equal real roots; D < 0: no real roots (complex roots).

key_points: Always rearrange to standard form ax² + bx + c = 0 before applying any method.
Check: substitute roots back into original equation to verify.
Word problems: form the quadratic from given conditions, solve, reject non-physical roots (e.g., negative length).
Sum/product of roots used to form equation: x² − (α+β)x + αβ = 0.
Completing the square: alternative method; also gives vertex form of the parabola.

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Ratio_and_.overview: Ratio compares two quantities; proportion states two ratios are equal. Class 10 covers componendo-dividendo, properties of proportion, and their application in algebraic problems.

key_concepts.definition: a:b = c:d ⟺ ad = bc (cross multiplication). a and d are extremes; b and c are means. Product of extremes = product of means.
If a/b = c/d, then (a+b)/(a−b) = (c+d)/(c−d). Used to simplify complex rational equations.
If a/b = c/d = e/f = k, then each ratio = k. Useful for proving results like (a+c+e)/(b+d+f) = k.

key_points: Continued proportion: a:b = b:c means b² = ac; b is the geometric mean.
Third proportional: if a:b = b:x, then x = b²/a.
Fourth proportional: if a:b = c:x, then x = bc/a.
Mean proportional between a and b: √(ab).
Apply componendo-dividendo to equations of form (x+a)/(x-a) = (y+b)/(y-b) to eliminate fractions quickly.

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Factorisat.overview: Class 10 factorisation covers the factor theorem, remainder theorem, and factorising cubic polynomials by finding rational roots, then dividing out the linear factor.

key_concepts.definition: When p(x) is divided by (x − a), the remainder = p(a). No long division needed.
If p(a) = 0, then (x − a) is a factor of p(x). Converse: if (x − a) is a factor, then p(a) = 0.
Used to divide polynomial by a linear factor once a root is found; gives the remaining quadratic.

laws_and_formulas.name: Factor Theorem
laws_and_formulas.formula: p(a) = 0 ⟺ (x − a) is a factor of p(x)

laws_and_formulas.name: HCF of polynomials
laws_and_formulas.formula: Fully factorise each polynomial; HCF = product of common factors with lowest powers.

key_points: To find rational roots of p(x), try factors of the constant term ÷ factors of leading coefficient.
After finding one root (x = a), divide p(x) by (x − a) to get a quadratic, then factorise that.
Algebraic identities used in factorisation: a³ ± b³, (a±b)², (a+b)(a−b).
HCF and LCM of polynomials: HCF × LCM = product of the two polynomials.
ICSE format: show the substitution that proves (x − a) is a factor before dividing.

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Matrices.overview: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers. Class 10 covers addition, subtraction, multiplication of 2×2 matrices, and properties. Matrix equations are solved to find unknown elements.

key_concepts.definition: A matrix with m rows and n columns has order m × n. Element at row i, column j is aᵢⱼ.
Possible only if matrices have the same order. Add corresponding elements: (A+B)ᵢⱼ = aᵢⱼ + bᵢⱼ.
A (m×n) × B (n×p) = C (m×p). cᵢⱼ = sum of products of row i of A with column j of B. Not commutative in general.

laws_and_formulas.name: 2×2 Matrix Multiplication
laws_and_formulas.formula: [a b; c d] × [e f; g h] = [ae+bg af+bh; ce+dg cf+dh]

laws_and_formulas.name: Null (Zero) Matrix
laws_and_formulas.formula: All elements = 0. A + O = A for any matrix A.

laws_and_formulas.name: Identity Matrix
laws_and_formulas.formula: I = [1 0; 0 1]. AI = IA = A for any 2×2 matrix A.

key_points: Matrix multiplication is defined only if columns of first = rows of second: (m×n)(n×p)=(m×p).
AB ≠ BA in general (not commutative), but A(BC) = (AB)C (associative).
Transpose: (Aᵀ)ᵢⱼ = aⱼᵢ. Reflect elements across the main diagonal.
ICSE: matrix equations like 2A + B = C — solve element by element.
Scalar multiplication: k × [a b; c d] = [ka kb; kc kd].

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_1_Arithmetic.overview: An Arithmetic Progression (AP) is a sequence where each term differs from the previous by a constant (common difference d). Class 10 covers the nth term formula, sum of n terms, and problem-solving using AP properties.

key_concepts.definition: d = a₂ − a₁ = a₃ − a₂ = ... constant for all consecutive terms. Can be positive, negative, or zero.
Tₙ = a + (n−1)d, where a = first term, n = position. Used to find any term or the number of terms.
Sₙ = n/2 × (2a + (n−1)d) = n/2 × (first term + last term).

laws_and_formulas.name: Sum of n Terms
laws_and_formulas.formula: Sₙ = n/2 [2a + (n−1)d] = n/2 (a + l), where l = last term

key_points: Three terms in AP: take them as (a−d), a, (a+d) to simplify sum conditions.
Four terms in AP: use (a−3d), (a−d), (a+d), (a+3d).
Sum of natural numbers: 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2 — a special AP.
If Sₙ is known, the AP's nth term = Sₙ − Sₙ₋₁.
ICSE: always state values of a and d clearly before applying formulas.

10|mathematics|Geometry_Similarity.overview: Two figures are similar if they have the same shape but not necessarily the same size — corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are proportional. Class 10 covers similarity criteria for triangles and the Basic Proportionality Theorem.

key_concepts.definition: Triangles with equal corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides. Symbol: △ABC ~ △DEF.
AA (Angle-Angle): two pairs of equal angles. SAS (Side-Angle-Side): one angle equal, including sides proportional. SSS: all three sides proportional.
If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle, it divides the other two sides in the same ratio.

laws_and_formulas.name: BPT (Thales' Theorem)
laws_and_formulas.formula: If DE ∥ BC in △ABC, then AD/DB = AE/EC

key_points: Similarity ≠ Congruence: similar triangles have equal angles but sides only proportional (not necessarily equal).
Area ratio = square of the linear scale factor.
In right triangles: altitude to hypotenuse creates two triangles each similar to the original — a classic proof setup.
Converse of BPT: if AD/DB = AE/EC, then DE ∥ BC.
ICSE: always state the similarity criterion used and write the correspondence of vertices in order.

10|mathematics|Algebra_Part_2_Coordinate.overview: Coordinate geometry links algebra and geometry. Class 10 covers the distance formula, section formula, midpoint, area of a triangle, slope, and equations of lines including various standard forms.

key_concepts.definition: Distance between (x₁,y₁) and (x₂,y₂) = √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²].
Point dividing line segment joining (x₁,y₁) and (x₂,y₂) in ratio m:n is ((mx₂+nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂+ny₁)/(m+n)).
m = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁) = tan θ where θ is angle with positive x-axis. Parallel lines: equal slopes. Perpendicular lines: m₁ × m₂ = −1.

laws_and_formulas.name: Equation of Line
laws_and_formulas.formula: Slope-intercept: y = mx + c. Point-slope: y−y₁ = m(x−x₁). Two-point: (y−y₁)/(y₂−y₁) = (x−x₁)/(x₂−x₁)

key_points: Collinear points: area of triangle = 0.
x-intercept of a line: set y = 0 and solve. y-intercept: set x = 0 and solve.
Centroid of triangle: ((x₁+x₂+x₃)/3, (y₁+y₂+y₃)/3).
Slope of horizontal line = 0; slope of vertical line = undefined.
ICSE: for section formula, always identify which point is P and which is Q before applying ratio m:n.

10|mathematics|Geometry_Part_2_Circles.overview: Class 10 circle theorems cover tangents, chords, angles subtended at centre and circumference, cyclic quadrilaterals, and the intersecting chords/secant-tangent relationships.

key_concepts.definition: A line touching the circle at exactly one point. Tangent ⊥ radius at point of contact. Two tangents from an external point are equal in length.
Angle in a semicircle = 90°. (Angle subtended by diameter at any point on the circle is a right angle.)
A quadrilateral with all four vertices on a circle. Opposite angles are supplementary: ∠A + ∠C = ∠B + ∠D = 180°.

laws_and_formulas.name: Angle at Centre
laws_and_formulas.formula: Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference subtended by same arc.

laws_and_formulas.name: Tangent-Chord Angle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Angle between tangent and chord = angle in alternate segment.

laws_and_formulas.name: Intersecting Chords
laws_and_formulas.formula: PA × PB = PC × PD (chords AB and CD intersecting at P inside the circle)

key_points: Angles in same segment (subtended by same chord) are equal.
Tangent-secant from external point: PA × PB = PT² (PA, PB on secant; PT = tangent length).
Tangent from external point: draw radius to point of contact; triangle formed is right-angled at contact point.
Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre and at the circumference.
ICSE: always state the theorem name used in each step of a proof.

10|mathematics|Mensuration_Complete_Unit.overview: Class 10 Mensuration deals with surface areas and volumes of 3D shapes — cylinder, cone, sphere, and hemisphere — and combinations of these solids. Conversion between shapes (e.g., melting and recasting) is a key problem type.

key_concepts.definition: Curved Surface Area = 2πrh. Total Surface Area = 2πr(h+r). Volume = πr²h.
Slant height l = √(r² + h²). CSA = πrl. TSA = πr(l+r). Volume = (1/3)πr²h.
Sphere: SA = 4πr². V = (4/3)πr³. Hemisphere: CSA = 2πr². TSA = 3πr². V = (2/3)πr³.

key_points: Volume is conserved when a solid is melted and recast — equate volumes.
Frustum (truncated cone): CSA = π(R+r)l where l = √(h²+(R−r)²); Volume = (πh/3)(R²+r²+Rr).
Number of small solids from large = Volume of large / Volume of one small.
Total surface area of combined solid: CSA of each part — omit the base(s) where parts join.
Take π = 22/7 unless stated otherwise in ICSE problems.

10|mathematics|Trigonometry_Complete_Uni.overview: Class 10 Trigonometry builds on Class 9 ratios by introducing trigonometric identities and their application in proving equalities. Heights and distances problems require angle of elevation/depression.

key_concepts.definition: sin θ, cos θ, tan θ, cosec θ, sec θ, cot θ and their reciprocal relationships; defined for acute angles in right triangles.
sin θ = cos(90°−θ); tan θ = cot(90°−θ); sec θ = cosec(90°−θ). Pairs add to 90°.
Angle of elevation: looking up to an object. Angle of depression: looking down. Draw a right triangle from the given scenario.

laws_and_formulas.name: Pythagorean Identities
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1; 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ; 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ

laws_and_formulas.name: Standard Values
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin30°=½, sin45°=1/√2, sin60°=√3/2; cos is reverse; tan30°=1/√3, tan45°=1, tan60°=√3

key_points: Identity proofs: start with the more complex side; aim to reach the simpler side.
Convert everything to sin and cos when stuck in an identity proof.
Heights & distances: ICSE often has two positions of observer — set up simultaneous equations.
Angle of depression from a height h = angle of elevation from the ground (alternate angles).
ICSE: show all intermediate steps clearly; leave answer in simplified surd form unless told to approximate.

10|mathematics|Statistics_Complete_Unit.overview: Statistics at Class 10 covers finding mean, median, and mode from grouped data, and drawing cumulative frequency curves (ogives) to estimate median and quartiles graphically.

key_concepts.definition: Mean = Σfx / Σf, where x = class midpoint. Or use assumed mean: Mean = A + Σfd/Σf, where d = x − A.
Locate the median class (where cumulative frequency ≥ N/2 first). Use formula: M = L + [(N/2 − cf)/f] × h.
Plot upper class boundaries against cumulative frequencies. Median = x-value at N/2 on the y-axis. Quartiles at N/4 and 3N/4.

laws_and_formulas.name: Median Formula
laws_and_formulas.formula: M = L + [(N/2 − cf) / f] × h, where L = lower boundary of median class, cf = cumulative freq before median class, f = freq of median class, h = class width

key_points: Mode from grouped data: modal class has the highest frequency; use the formula M = L + [f₁−f₀/(2f₁−f₀−f₂)] × h.
Ogive: always plot points at the UPPER class boundary, not midpoint.
Less-than ogive and more-than ogive intersect at the median.
Quartile deviation = (Q₃ − Q₁)/2 — measure of dispersion.
ICSE: draw ogive neatly with uniform scale; label axes correctly including units.

10|mathematics|Probability_Complete_Unit.overview: Probability is the measure of likelihood of an event occurring, ranging from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Class 10 covers experimental and theoretical probability, sample space, and complementary events.

key_concepts.definition: The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. For a coin: {H, T}. For a die: {1,2,3,4,5,6}.
P(E) = Number of favourable outcomes / Total number of equally likely outcomes.
P(E) + P(E') = 1. If P(getting a six) = 1/6, then P(not getting a six) = 5/6.

laws_and_formulas.name: Probability Formula
laws_and_formulas.formula: P(E) = n(E)/n(S), where n(E) = favourable outcomes, n(S) = total outcomes

laws_and_formulas.name: Addition Rule (mutually exclusive)
laws_and_formulas.formula: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B), if A and B are mutually exclusive

key_points: 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1 always. P(impossible event) = 0; P(certain event) = 1.
Cards: 52 cards, 4 suits (13 each: A, 2–10, J, Q, K), 2 colours (26 red, 26 black).
Dice: 6 faces; two dice give 36 ordered pairs. List them systematically.
Mutually exclusive events cannot happen simultaneously; exhaustive events cover all outcomes.
Experimental probability: based on actual trials. Theoretical: based on ideal conditions. They converge as trials → ∞.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_U.overview: An object moving in a circle at constant speed undergoes uniform circular motion. Despite constant speed, velocity changes direction — so acceleration (centripetal) always points toward the centre.

key_concepts.definition: Acceleration directed toward the centre of the circle: a = v²/r = ω²r, where v = speed, ω = angular velocity, r = radius.
Net force providing centripetal acceleration: F = mv²/r = mω²r. Not a separate force — it's the role played by tension, gravity, friction, etc.
ω = 2π/T = 2πf, where T = period, f = frequency. Relates to linear velocity: v = ωr.
