key_points: Centripetal force is always perpendicular to velocity — it changes direction but not speed.
No centripetal force → object moves in straight line (Newton's 1st Law).
Circular motion examples: planets orbiting, car rounding a bend, washing machine drum.
Centrifugal 'force' is a pseudo-force felt in the rotating frame — not real in inertial frame.
Period T: time for one complete revolution; frequency f = 1/T (Hz).

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_T.overview: A turning effect about a pivot is called the moment of a force or torque. It depends on both the magnitude of the force and the perpendicular distance (moment arm) from the pivot. Equilibrium requires balanced moments.

key_concepts.definition: Moment = Force × perpendicular distance from pivot. Unit: N·m. Clockwise moments are balanced by anticlockwise moments in equilibrium.
For a body in equilibrium: sum of clockwise moments = sum of anticlockwise moments about any point.
Two equal, opposite, and parallel forces not acting along the same line. Their moment = one force × perpendicular distance between them.

laws_and_formulas.name: Moment of Force
laws_and_formulas.formula: τ = F × d (F = force in N, d = perpendicular distance in m; τ in N·m)

key_points: Always use the perpendicular (shortest) distance from the pivot to the line of action of force.
Moment is a vector — sign convention: anticlockwise positive, clockwise negative (or vice versa consistently).
Centre of gravity: point where entire weight acts. For uniform objects, it is the geometric centre.
Conditions for equilibrium: (1) Net force = 0; (2) Net moment = 0.
Examples: seesaw, spanner, door hinge, steering wheel.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_G.overview: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

key_concepts.definition: F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg². Acts along the line joining the centres of masses.
g = GM/R² at Earth's surface. g ≈ 9.8 m/s². Varies with altitude and latitude (less at equator, more at poles).
Mass: amount of matter, constant everywhere (kg). Weight: gravitational force W = mg; varies with g. Weight is zero in free fall (weightlessness).

key_points: g decreases as altitude increases: g' = g(R/(R+h))².
g decreases slightly below Earth's surface too (less mass pulls you).
Moon's g ≈ 1/6 of Earth's — same mass on Moon weighs 1/6 as much.
Escape velocity: v = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s for Earth.
Gravitational force is always attractive — unlike electrostatic which can repel.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_W.overview: Work is done when force causes displacement in its direction. Energy is the capacity to do work; power is the rate of doing work. The work-energy theorem links these concepts.

key_concepts.definition: W = F × d × cos θ, where θ is angle between force and displacement. SI unit: Joule (J). Work is zero if force ⊥ displacement.
Energy of motion: KE = ½mv². Unit: J. Work-Energy Theorem: Net work done = change in KE.
Gravitational PE = mgh. Elastic PE = ½kx². Stored energy due to position or deformation.

laws_and_formulas.name: Power
laws_and_formulas.formula: P = W/t = Fv (instantaneous power). Unit: Watt (W) = J/s

laws_and_formulas.name: Conservation of Energy
laws_and_formulas.formula: KE + PE = constant (for conservative forces, no friction)

key_points: Negative work: force opposes motion (friction does negative work).
1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J (unit used in electricity billing).
Efficiency = (useful output work / input work) × 100%. Always < 100% due to friction.
Horsepower: 1 hp = 746 W.
Free fall: KE gained = PE lost at any point — total mechanical energy conserved.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_M.overview: Simple machines multiply force or change its direction. Key concepts: Mechanical Advantage (MA), Velocity Ratio (VR), and Efficiency. Class 10 covers levers, pulleys, and inclined planes.

key_concepts.definition: MA = Load / Effort. How many times the machine multiplies the effort force.
VR = Distance moved by effort / Distance moved by load. Depends on machine design, not friction.
η = (MA/VR) × 100% = (useful work output / work input) × 100%. Always ≤ 100%.

laws_and_formulas.name: Ideal Machine
laws_and_formulas.formula: For ideal machine: MA = VR (efficiency = 100%). Effort × effort distance = Load × load distance

laws_and_formulas.name: Lever Principle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Load × load arm = Effort × effort arm (MA = effort arm / load arm)

key_points: Levers — Class 1: fulcrum between load and effort (scissors, seesaw); Class 2: load between (wheelbarrow); Class 3: effort between (tweezers, hand).
Block and tackle: VR = number of rope segments supporting the movable block.
Inclined plane: VR = length of plane / height = 1/sin θ.
MA < VR always in real machines due to friction.
Ideal (perfect) machine has no friction and no deformation — used for theoretical calculations.

10|physics|Force_Work_Power_and_Energy_C.overview: The Law of Conservation of Energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed — only converted from one form to another. Total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

key_concepts.definition: Mechanical (KE + PE), heat, light, sound, electrical, chemical, nuclear. Conversions occur constantly in nature and technology.
In an isolated system: Total energy (initial) = Total energy (final). For free fall: KE + PE = constant at every point.
While total energy is conserved, useful energy decreases due to conversion to heat (friction, resistance). This is addressed by the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

key_points: A ball dropped from height h: just before hitting ground, all PE converted to KE. v = √(2gh).
Spring: elastic PE = ½kx² converted to KE when released.
Hydroelectric: gravitational PE → KE of water → electrical energy.
No machine is 100% efficient — some energy always lost as heat due to friction.
Conservation of energy is one of the most fundamental laws of physics — no exception has ever been found.

10|physics|Light_Refraction_of_Light.overview: Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one medium to another due to a change in speed. Snell's Law quantifies the relationship between angles of incidence and refraction and the refractive indices.

key_concepts.definition: n = speed of light in vacuum / speed of light in medium = c/v. Also n = sin i / sin r (Snell's Law).
n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r. At interface of two media, the ratio of sines equals inverse ratio of refractive indices.
A medium with higher n is optically denser. Light bends toward normal when entering denser medium, away from normal when entering rarer medium.

laws_and_formulas.name: Snell's Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: n₁ sin i = n₂ sin r; also n = sin i / sin r when entering from air (n₁=1)

key_points: Normal: perpendicular to the surface at the point of incidence. All angles measured from the normal.
Causes of refraction: change in speed of light. Frequency remains the same; wavelength changes.
Real depth vs apparent depth: n = real depth / apparent depth.
Atmospheric refraction: causes sunrise/sunset to appear before actual event; twinkling of stars.
Mirages: due to total internal reflection in atmosphere from hot air layers near ground.

10|physics|Light_Refraction_through_Glas.overview: When light passes through a glass slab, it emerges parallel to the original ray (lateral displacement). A prism disperses white light into its constituent colours (spectrum) due to different refractive indices for different wavelengths.

key_concepts.definition: Perpendicular shift of emerging ray from incident ray when passing through a parallel-sided glass slab. Depends on slab thickness, angle of incidence, and refractive index.
White light splits into VIBGYOR (violet, indigo, blue, green, yellow, orange, red) on passing through a prism. Violet bends most (highest n); red bends least (lowest n).
Angle between the original incident ray and the emergent ray from a prism. Minimum deviation occurs at a specific angle of incidence.

laws_and_formulas.name: Lateral Displacement
laws_and_formulas.formula: d = t × sin(i−r)/cos r, where t = slab thickness

laws_and_formulas.name: Deviation by Prism (minimum deviation)
laws_and_formulas.formula: n = sin[(A+D)/2] / sin(A/2), where A = prism angle, D = angle of minimum deviation

key_points: Glass slab: incident and emergent rays are parallel; ray is shifted laterally.
Prism: incident and emergent rays are not parallel; net deviation = sum of deviations at two surfaces.
Rainbow: formed by refraction, internal reflection, and dispersion of sunlight in water droplets.
Recombination: two prisms arranged base-to-base recombine the spectrum back to white light.
Scattering: blue sky — blue light scattered most by atmosphere (Rayleigh scattering).

10|physics|Light_Total_Internal_Reflecti.overview: Total Internal Reflection (TIR) occurs when light in a denser medium hits the interface at an angle greater than the critical angle — all light reflects back into the denser medium with no refraction.

key_concepts.definition: The angle of incidence in the denser medium at which the angle of refraction = 90°. For angles > C, TIR occurs. sin C = 1/n (for air-medium interface).
Light must travel from denser to rarer medium. Angle of incidence must exceed critical angle. All light is reflected internally.
Thin glass fibre where light travels by successive TIR along the core. Used in telecommunications and medical endoscopes (fibre optics).

laws_and_formulas.name: Critical Angle
laws_and_formulas.formula: sin C = 1/n (from denser medium to air); n = 1/sin C

laws_and_formulas.name: Conditions for TIR
laws_and_formulas.formula: (1) Light in optically denser medium; (2) Angle of incidence > Critical angle

key_points: Glass (n≈1.5): C ≈ 42°. Diamond (n≈2.4): C ≈ 24° — why diamonds sparkle so brilliantly.
Periscope and binoculars use TIR in 45°-45°-90° prisms instead of mirrors (no light loss).
Mirages and looming: atmospheric TIR effects.
TIR gives 100% reflection — better than even the best mirrors.
Medical: endoscope uses optical fibres to view inside the body without surgery.

10|physics|Light_Lenses_Convex_and_Conca.overview: Lenses refract light using two curved surfaces. A convex lens converges light and forms real or virtual images; a concave lens always diverges light and forms virtual, erect, diminished images. The lens formula and magnification apply to both.

key_concepts.definition: Convex lens: f is positive; principal focus is where parallel rays converge. Concave lens: f is negative; parallel rays appear to diverge from the focus.
1/v − 1/u = 1/f, where v = image distance, u = object distance, f = focal length (all measured from optical centre with sign convention).
P = 1/f (in metres). Unit: dioptre (D). Convex lens: P > 0. Concave lens: P < 0. Combined lenses: P_total = P₁ + P₂.

laws_and_formulas.name: Magnification
laws_and_formulas.formula: m = v/u = height of image / height of object

key_points: Sign convention: distances measured from optical centre; +ve in direction of incident light, -ve against.
Convex lens images: real and inverted when object is beyond F; virtual and erect when object is within F.
Eye defects: myopia (short sight) corrected by concave lens; hypermetropia (long sight) by convex lens.
Lens maker's equation not in ICSE syllabus — focus on ray diagrams and formula.
Magnifying glass: convex lens with object within focal length; always virtual, erect, magnified.

10|physics|Light_The_Electromagnetic_Spe.overview: The electromagnetic (EM) spectrum is the complete range of EM waves ordered by frequency/wavelength, from radio waves (lowest frequency) to gamma rays (highest). All EM waves travel at c = 3×10⁸ m/s in vacuum.

key_concepts.definition: Transverse waves; travel at c in vacuum; no medium needed. Carry energy; obey reflection, refraction, diffraction laws. Not deflected by electric/magnetic fields.
Increasing frequency (decreasing wavelength): Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible → Ultraviolet → X-ray → Gamma ray.
Wavelength: 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red). Narrow band within the full EM spectrum.

laws_and_formulas.name: Wave Equation
laws_and_formulas.formula: c = fλ (c = 3×10⁸ m/s, f = frequency in Hz, λ = wavelength in m)

key_points: Radio waves: longest wavelength; TV/radio broadcasting.
Microwaves: radar, mobile communications, microwave ovens.
Infrared: heat radiation; remote controls; night-vision cameras.
Ultraviolet: causes sunburn; used in sterilisation; detected by fluorescent substances.
X-rays: penetrate soft tissue; used in medical imaging; high-energy photons.
Gamma rays: highest energy; from nuclear decay; most penetrating; used in cancer treatment.

10|physics|Sound_Reflection_of_Sound_Wav.overview: Sound reflects off hard surfaces following the same laws as light reflection. An echo is a reflected sound heard distinctly after the original. Class 10 covers conditions for echoes and applications of sound reflection.

key_concepts.definition: A distinct repetition of sound due to reflection. For a distinct echo, the reflecting surface must be at least 17 m away (sound travels 34 m in 0.1 s, the minimum time for the ear to distinguish).
Persistence of sound after the source stops, due to multiple reflections in an enclosed space. Excessive reverberation makes speech unclear.
Sound Navigation and Ranging. Uses ultrasound to detect underwater objects. Distance = (speed × time) / 2.

laws_and_formulas.name: SONAR Distance
laws_and_formulas.formula: d = v×t/2, where t = time for echo to return, v = speed of sound in water (~1500 m/s)

key_points: Sound obeys same laws of reflection as light: angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
Acoustics of buildings: absorbing materials (curtains, carpets) reduce reverberation.
Speed of sound in air at 0°C = 332 m/s; at 20°C ≈ 343 m/s. Increases with temperature.
Megaphones, curved walls in whispering galleries, stethoscopes use sound reflection.
SONAR used in submarines, to map ocean floor, and to locate fish shoals.

10|physics|Sound_Natural_Damped_and_Forc.overview: Vibrations can be classified by their driving conditions. Natural vibrations occur at a body's own frequency; damped vibrations lose energy over time; forced vibrations occur at an external driving frequency.

key_concepts.definition: Vibrations at the body's own natural frequency when displaced and released. Amplitude is constant (ideal — no energy loss).
Vibrations where amplitude decreases due to energy loss (friction, air resistance). Eventually come to rest.
When an external periodic force is applied at any frequency — the body vibrates at the driving frequency, not its own natural frequency.

key_points: Natural frequency depends on physical properties: mass, stiffness of spring, length of string.
Damping can be light (slow decay), critical (returns to rest fastest), or overdamped (too slow).
Examples of forced vibrations: tuning fork placed on a box — box vibrates at fork's frequency.
Amplitude of forced vibration depends on driving frequency — maximum when driving frequency = natural frequency.
Reducing vibrations: shock absorbers in cars use damping; buildings in earthquake zones have dampers.

10|physics|Sound_Resonance.overview: Resonance occurs when the frequency of an applied force matches the natural frequency of the system — the system vibrates with maximum amplitude. It is a special case of forced vibrations.

key_concepts.definition: Driving frequency = Natural frequency of the body. At resonance, amplitude of vibration is maximum.
Strings vibrate at frequencies f = n/(2L) × √(T/μ), where n = harmonic number, L = length, T = tension, μ = linear mass density.
Air column in a tube resonates when its length is odd multiples of λ/4 (closed pipe) or multiples of λ/2 (open pipe).

key_points: Tuning forks: two forks of same frequency — strike one, the other vibrates by resonance.
Resonance can be destructive: Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed due to resonance with wind.
Soldiers break step while crossing a bridge to avoid resonance.
MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging): nuclear magnetic resonance in medicine.
Radio/TV tuning: adjusting circuit's natural frequency to match the broadcast frequency.

10|physics|Sound_Loudness_Pitch_and_Qual.overview: Three key characteristics distinguish sounds: loudness (related to amplitude/intensity), pitch (related to frequency), and quality or timbre (related to the number and intensity of overtones present).

key_concepts.definition: Subjective measure of sound intensity. Measured in decibels (dB). Intensity I = P/A (W/m²). Loudness ∝ amplitude².
How high or low a sound feels — determined by frequency. Higher frequency = higher pitch. e.g., woman's voice (higher pitch) vs man's voice.
The characteristic that distinguishes two sounds of same loudness and pitch from different sources. Determined by the number and intensity of overtones (harmonics).

laws_and_formulas.name: Intensity
laws_and_formulas.formula: I = P/A (W/m²); Loudness (dB) = 10 log(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m²

laws_and_formulas.name: Frequency and Pitch
laws_and_formulas.formula: Pitch increases with frequency. Human hearing range: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.

key_points: Intensity follows inverse square law: I ∝ 1/r² (doubles of distance → quarter of intensity).
Ultrasound: > 20,000 Hz. Infrasound: < 20 Hz. Audible: 20 Hz – 20 kHz.
Noise pollution: prolonged exposure >85 dB causes hearing damage.
Same note on violin vs flute: same pitch and loudness, different quality due to different harmonics.
Waveform: pure tone = sine wave; musical instruments produce complex waves with multiple harmonics.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Ohm.overview: Ohm's Law states the current through a conductor is proportional to the voltage across it (at constant temperature). Resistance depends on material, length, cross-section, and temperature.

key_concepts.definition: V = IR (V in volts, I in amperes, R in ohms). Valid for ohmic conductors at constant temperature.
Material property: R = ρL/A, where L = length, A = cross-sectional area, ρ = resistivity (Ω·m). Resistivity increases with temperature for metals.
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + ... (same current, voltages add). Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + ... (same voltage, currents add).

key_points: Doubling length → doubles resistance. Doubling cross-section area → halves resistance.
In series: same current everywhere; total voltage = sum of individual voltages.
In parallel: same voltage across each; total current = sum of individual currents.
Ammeters in series; voltmeters in parallel.
Non-ohmic devices: diodes, thermistors, filament bulbs — resistance changes with current.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Ele.overview: Electrical power is the rate of energy transfer. Electrical energy is measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh) for billing. Heating effect of current (Joule heating) is used in appliances like heaters and electric irons.

key_concepts.definition: P = VI = I²R = V²/R. Unit: Watt (W). Measures how fast electrical energy is converted to other forms.
Heat produced H = I²Rt (joules). More current, more resistance, more time → more heat.
E = Pt = VIt. Commercial unit: 1 kWh = 1 unit = 3.6 × 10⁶ J. Cost = (kWh) × rate per unit.

laws_and_formulas.name: Energy in kWh
laws_and_formulas.formula: Energy (kWh) = Power (kW) × Time (h); Cost = kWh × tariff rate

key_points: High resistance in series with same current → more heat (e.g., fuse blows by Joule heating).
Household appliances connected in parallel: all get same 230V; total current adds up.
Reading electricity meter: difference in kWh readings × tariff rate = bill.
Fuse: thin wire of high resistance; melts when current exceeds rated value, breaking the circuit.
A 60W bulb connected for 5 hours uses 60×5 = 300 Wh = 0.3 kWh.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Hou.overview: Household electrical circuits use alternating current (AC) at 230V/50Hz in India. Safety devices include fuses, circuit breakers, and earthing. The three-pin plug provides live, neutral, and earth connections.

key_concepts.definition: Live wire (L): carries current at 230V (brown). Neutral (N): return path at ~0V (blue). Earth (E): safety connection to ground (green/yellow).
Fuse: melts when current exceeds rated value. MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker): trips electromagnetically; can be reset.
Metal casings of appliances connected to earth. If live wire touches casing, current flows to earth (not user) — prevents electric shock.

key_points: Main switch, meter, and fuse box connected in series with the supply.
Lighting and power circuits are separate — different fuse ratings.
Three-pin plug: top pin = earth (largest, makes contact first for safety). Bottom left = neutral, bottom right = live.
Short circuit: live touches neutral directly (zero resistance path) → huge current → fuse blows.
Overloading: too many appliances on one circuit → excess current → fuse blows. Always use correct ampere fuse.

10|physics|Electricity_and_Magnetism_Mag.overview: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it. This principle is used in electromagnets, electric motors, and generators. Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction describes the reverse effect.

key_concepts.definition: Electric current produces a magnetic field. A compass needle near a current-carrying wire deflects. Direction given by Right-Hand Thumb Rule.
Coil of wire (solenoid) with iron core — current magnetises the core. Strength increases with current, number of turns, or soft iron core.
Changing magnetic flux through a coil induces an EMF (Faraday's Law). Used in generators: mechanical energy → electrical energy.

laws_and_formulas.name: Right-Hand Thumb Rule
laws_and_formulas.formula: Curl fingers in direction of current; thumb points in direction of magnetic field lines around straight wire.

laws_and_formulas.name: Faraday's Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: Induced EMF = −dΦ/dt (rate of change of magnetic flux); Lenz's Law: induced current opposes the change causing it.

key_points: Electric motor: current-carrying coil in magnetic field rotates (electrical → mechanical energy).
Generator (dynamo): coil rotated in magnetic field → induced current (mechanical → electrical).
Transformer: changes AC voltage using mutual induction. V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p.
Hard vs soft iron: soft iron: easy to magnetise/demagnetise (electromagnets). Hard steel: permanent magnets.
Right-hand grip rule for solenoid: fingers in direction of current → thumb points to North pole.

10|physics|Heat_Latent_Heat.overview: Latent heat is the heat absorbed or released during a change of state at constant temperature and pressure. No temperature change occurs during a phase change — all energy goes into breaking (or forming) intermolecular bonds.

key_concepts.definition: Heat needed to melt 1 kg of solid at its melting point. For ice: Lf = 3.36 × 10⁵ J/kg. At 0°C for ice-water.
Heat needed to vaporise 1 kg of liquid at its boiling point. For water: Lv = 2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg at 100°C.
Evaporation takes latent heat from surroundings — feels cool. Rate of evaporation increases with temperature, decreased humidity, increased surface area, wind.

laws_and_formulas.name: Heat for Phase Change
laws_and_formulas.formula: Q = mL (no temperature change); Q = mcΔT (temperature change, no phase change)

key_points: Steam burns more severely than boiling water: steam carries additional 2260 J/g of latent heat.
Ice in drinks: cools by absorbing latent heat of fusion at 0°C — more effective than cold water.
Sweating: perspiration evaporates, absorbing latent heat from skin — cooling effect.
Cooking in pressure cooker: raises boiling point → food cooks faster.
Refrigerator: refrigerant evaporates in freezer compartment (absorbs latent heat) and condenses outside (releases heat).

10|physics|Heat_Specific_Heat_Capacity_a.overview: Specific heat capacity (c) is the heat needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C (or 1K). Calorimetry applies the principle of conservation of energy: heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body in an insulated system.

key_concepts.definition: c = Q/(mΔT). Unit: J/(kg·°C) or J/(kg·K). Water has high c (4200 J/kg·K) — acts as heat reservoir; metals have low c.
C = mc. Heat capacity of an object = mass × specific heat. Unit: J/K or J/°C.
Heat lost = Heat gained (no heat exchange with surroundings). m₁c₁(T₁−T_f) = m₂c₂(T_f−T₂).

laws_and_formulas.name: Calorimetry Principle
laws_and_formulas.formula: Heat lost by hot body = Heat gained by cold body: m₁c₁ΔT₁ = m₂c₂ΔT₂

key_points: Water has the highest specific heat of common substances — why coastal areas have mild climates.
Calorimeter: double-walled insulated container to minimise heat loss to surroundings.
Finding specific heat: mix known masses at known temperatures; measure final temperature; apply formula.
Thermal equilibrium: final temperature is between initial temperatures of the two bodies.
Latent heat problems combine Q = mL and Q = mcΔT in the same calculation (e.g., ice added to hot water).

10|physics|Heat_Loss_and_Gain_of_Heat.overview: Heat transfer occurs by conduction (through contact), convection (fluid circulation), and radiation (electromagnetic waves). Good conductors and insulators, greenhouse effect, and thermos flask design are key applications.

key_concepts.definition: Heat transfer through direct contact without bulk movement of matter. Metals: good conductors (many free electrons). Non-metals, gases: poor conductors (insulators).
Heat transfer through movement of fluid (liquid or gas). Hot fluid rises (less dense), cool fluid sinks → convection current. Land/sea breeze, boiling water.
Heat transfer as EM waves (infrared); no medium needed. All bodies absorb and emit radiation. Black bodies: best absorbers and emitters. Shiny surfaces: best reflectors.

key_points: Thermos flask: double glass walls with vacuum (no conduction/convection); silvered walls (minimise radiation).
Greenhouse effect: atmosphere transparent to visible light, absorbs infrared from Earth's surface → warming.
Newton's Law of Cooling: rate of heat loss ∝ temperature difference with surroundings (for small differences).
Woollen clothes: trap air (poor conductor) — keep body warm by reducing conduction and convection.
Solar cooker: uses radiation from Sun; black surface absorbs maximally; glass cover traps heat.

10|physics|Modern_Physics_Radioactivity_.overview: Radioactivity is the spontaneous disintegration of unstable nuclei, emitting alpha, beta, or gamma radiation. These radiations differ in nature, penetrating power, and ionising ability.

key_concepts.definition: Helium nucleus (2p + 2n, charge +2, mass 4). Least penetrating (stopped by paper); most ionising; slow speed (~1/10 c). Parent nucleus loses 2 protons, 2 neutrons.
High-speed electron (charge −1, negligible mass). Moderate penetrating power (stopped by few mm aluminium). Neutron → proton + electron emitted.
High-energy electromagnetic waves (no charge, no mass). Most penetrating (several cm lead needed). Least ionising. Emitted along with α or β to release excess energy.

key_points: Half-life T½: time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay. Constant for a given isotope.
After n half-lives: N = N₀/2ⁿ. Mass remaining = original/2ⁿ.
Ionising power: α > β > γ. Penetrating power: γ > β > α. (Inverse relationship.)
Uses: α — smoke detectors. β — thickness gauging. γ — cancer treatment, sterilisation, PET scans.
Background radiation: from cosmic rays, rocks (radon gas), food, medical X-rays — always present.

10|physics|Modern_Physics_Nuclear_Energy.overview: Nuclear reactions release enormous energy by E = mc². Fission splits heavy nuclei (uranium-235); fusion combines light nuclei (hydrogen isotopes). Both convert mass to energy; fusion is the energy source of the Sun.

key_concepts.definition: A heavy nucleus (²³⁵U) splits into two medium nuclei when struck by a slow neutron, releasing 3 neutrons and ~200 MeV of energy. Chain reaction possible.
Light nuclei (deuterium + tritium) combine at very high temperatures to form a heavier nucleus, releasing energy. Powers the Sun and hydrogen bombs.
E = mc². Mass defect (Δm) in nuclear reaction is converted to energy: E = Δm × c².

laws_and_formulas.name: Chain Reaction
laws_and_formulas.formula: ²³⁵U + n → fission products + 3n + energy; 3 new neutrons can cause 3 more fissions

key_points: Critical mass: minimum mass for self-sustaining chain reaction. Nuclear bomb: supercritical mass.
Nuclear reactor: controlled chain reaction; moderator (heavy water/graphite) slows neutrons; control rods (cadmium) absorb neutrons.
Fusion requires temperatures of ~10⁷–10⁸ K — plasma state. Difficult to achieve on Earth (tokamak reactor research).
1 kg of matter: E = 9 × 10¹⁶ J — enormous energy from tiny mass.
Nuclear waste: radioactive for thousands of years — a major environmental challenge.

10|physics|Modern_Physics_Photocells_and.overview: Photocells (photoelectric cells) use the photoelectric effect — light ejects electrons from a metal surface — to convert light into electrical signals. Modern applications span solar panels, burglar alarms, and exposure meters.

key_concepts.definition: When light of sufficient frequency falls on a metal, electrons are ejected. Threshold frequency (f₀): minimum frequency for emission. Above f₀, KE of electrons ∝ (f − f₀).
Device using photoelectric effect: light hits photoemissive cathode → electrons flow → electric current. Used as a transducer (light → electricity).
Semiconductor device (silicon p-n junction) that converts light directly to DC electricity by the photovoltaic effect (different from photoelectric effect).

laws_and_formulas.name: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation
laws_and_formulas.formula: KE_max = hf − φ, where φ = hf₀ = work function (minimum energy to emit electron)

key_points: Photocell applications: burglar alarms (beam breaks → alarm), automatic door openers, street light switches, camera exposure meters.
Solar panels: arrays of solar cells; most common renewable energy source currently being scaled.
Photoelectric effect cannot be explained by wave theory of light — requires photon (particle) model.
Work function: characteristic of the metal — different for each material.
LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes): reverse of photocells — electricity → light (optoelectronics).

10|chemistry|1_Periodic_Properties_and_V.overview: The Modern Periodic Table arranges elements in order of increasing atomic number, with chemical and physical properties repeating periodically. Class 10 covers trends in atomic radius, ionisation energy, electron affinity, and electronegativity across periods and down groups.

key_concepts.definition: Properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers (Moseley's Modern Periodic Law). Mendeleev's original law used atomic masses.
Decreases across a period (more protons pull electrons closer); increases down a group (new shells added).
Energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom. Increases across a period; decreases down a group.
Tendency to attract bonding electrons. Increases across a period; decreases down a group. Fluorine has highest electronegativity (4.0).

laws_and_formulas.name: Periodic Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: Properties repeat after every 2, 8, 8, 18, 18... elements (corresponding to filled shells)

key_points: Across a period: atomic radius ↓, IE ↑, electronegativity ↑, metallic character ↓.
Down a group: atomic radius ↑, IE ↓, electronegativity ↓, metallic character ↑.
Noble gases: completely filled outer shells → very high IE → chemically inert.
Diagonal relationship: Li resembles Mg; Be resembles Al — due to similar charge/size ratio.
Electron affinity: energy released when an atom gains an electron. Generally increases across a period.

10|chemistry|2_Chemical_Bonding.overview: Chemical bonds hold atoms together in compounds. Ionic bonds form by electron transfer; covalent bonds by electron sharing. The type of bond determines physical properties like melting point, solubility, and electrical conductivity.

key_concepts.definition: Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer. Metal loses electrons (forms cation); non-metal gains electrons (forms anion). Example: NaCl.
Formed by sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms. Single bond: 1 shared pair. Double bond: 2 shared pairs. Triple bond: 3 shared pairs.
A covalent bond where both electrons are donated by one atom (the donor/Lewis base) to another (the acceptor/Lewis acid). Example: NH₄⁺ formation.

key_points: Ionic compounds: high MP/BP, conduct electricity when dissolved or melted, usually soluble in water.
Covalent compounds: generally low MP/BP, non-conductors (molecular covalent), soluble in organic solvents.
Lewis dot structures: represent valence electrons; octets completed in ionic/covalent bonding.
Electronegativity difference: > 1.7 → ionic; < 1.7 → covalent (approximate rule).
Metallic bond: 'sea of electrons' model; explains conductivity, malleability, ductility of metals.

10|chemistry|3_Study_of_Acids_Bases_and_.overview: Acids release H⁺ ions in water; bases release OH⁻ ions. Salts are formed by neutralisation reactions. Class 10 covers properties, pH, indicators, and types of salts with preparation methods.

key_concepts.definition: Acid: produces H⁺ in water. Base: produces OH⁻ in water. Limitation: applies only to aqueous solutions.
pH = −log[H⁺]. pH < 7: acidic. pH = 7: neutral. pH > 7: basic. pH range 0–14.
Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Strong acid + Strong base: pH of salt = 7. Strong acid + Weak base: pH < 7. Weak acid + Strong base: pH > 7.

key_points: Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃): fully ionise. Weak acids (CH₃COOH, H₂CO₃): partially ionise.
Indicators: litmus (red in acid, blue in base), phenolphthalein (colourless in acid, pink in base), methyl orange.
Types of salts: normal (NaCl), acidic (NaHSO₄), basic (Mg(OH)Cl), double (Alum), complex.
Salt hydrolysis explains why some salt solutions are acidic or basic despite being 'neutral'.
Preparation of salts: from acid + metal, acid + carbonate, acid + oxide, direct combination, precipitation.

10|chemistry|4_Analytical_Chemistry.overview: Analytical chemistry uses specific tests to identify the ions present in an unknown salt solution. Class 10 covers flame tests, blowpipe tests, and confirmatory tests for common anions and cations.

key_concepts.definition: Flame test identifies group I and II metals by characteristic flame colour: Na+ (yellow), K+ (violet), Ca2+ (brick red), Cu2+ (blue-green). Ammonium detected by NH3 gas with NaOH.
Cl⁻: white ppt with AgNO₃ (soluble in NH₃). SO₄²⁻: white ppt with BaCl₂ (insoluble in HCl). CO₃²⁻: effervescence with HCl, gas turns limewater milky. NO₃⁻: brown ring test.
Each identified ion is confirmed with a specific reagent that gives an unmistakable result (precipitate colour, gas, or colour change).

key_points: Systematic analysis: test for ammonium and group 2 cations first (volatile NH3 would be lost on heating).
Dilute H₂SO₄ preliminary test: effervescence with CO₃²⁻/HCO₃⁻; pungent smell with SO₃²⁻; rotten egg with S²⁻.
AgNO₃ test: Cl⁻ white ppt soluble in NH₃; Br⁻ pale yellow ppt slightly soluble; I⁻ yellow ppt insoluble.
BaCl₂ test for SO₄²⁻: white precipitate insoluble in dilute HCl — distinguishes from SO₃²⁻ (which is soluble).
ICSE: always state reagent added, observation, and conclusion for each test.

10|chemistry|5_Mole_Concept_and_Stoichio.overview: The mole is the SI unit for amount of substance — 1 mole = 6.022×10²³ entities (Avogadro's number). Stoichiometry uses balanced equations to calculate masses, volumes, and moles of reactants and products.

key_concepts.definition: 1 mole of any substance contains 6.022×10²³ (Avogadro's number, Nₐ) particles. Molar mass = mass of 1 mole in grams = relative atomic/molecular mass in grams.
At STP (0°C, 1 atm): 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 L. At RTP (room temperature): ~24 L/mol.
Using balanced equation molar ratios to find: moles of product from given moles of reactant; then convert to mass or volume.

laws_and_formulas.name: Mole Calculations
laws_and_formulas.formula: Moles = mass/molar mass = volume(L)/22.4 (at STP) = number of particles/6.022×10²³

laws_and_formulas.name: Percentage Composition
laws_and_formulas.formula: % of element = (mass of element in formula / molar mass of compound) × 100

key_points: Empirical formula: simplest whole number ratio of atoms. Molecular formula: actual number.
Limiting reagent: the reactant that runs out first; determines maximum product.
% yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) × 100%.
Avogadro's number applies to atoms, molecules, ions, electrons — any specified entity.
ICSE: balance equations first; then use mole ratios from balanced equation for all calculations.

10|chemistry|6_Electrolysis.overview: Electrolysis is the decomposition of an electrolyte by passing an electric current through it. Cations migrate to the cathode (reduced); anions migrate to the anode (oxidised). Faraday's laws quantify the products.

key_concepts.definition: A substance that conducts electricity when dissolved or molten by means of ions. Strong electrolytes: fully ionise (HCl, NaOH). Weak electrolytes: partially ionise (CH₃COOH).
Mass of substance deposited/liberated ∝ quantity of electricity passed (Q = It, in coulombs).
Mass deposited by same charge from different electrolytes ∝ their equivalent weights (molar mass / valency).

laws_and_formulas.name: Faraday's First Law
laws_and_formulas.formula: m = ZIt = ZQ, where Z = electrochemical equivalent (g/C)

laws_and_formulas.name: Quantity of Charge
laws_and_formulas.formula: Q = It (I in amperes, t in seconds, Q in coulombs); 1 Faraday = 96500 C = charge of 1 mole of electrons

key_points: At cathode: cation gains electrons → reduced (metals deposited, H₂ at cathode for dilute acids).
At anode: anion loses electrons → oxidised (Cl₂ from concentrated HCl; O₂ from dilute solutions).
Electroplating: uses electrolysis to deposit a metal coating. Anode = metal to be deposited; cathode = object to be plated.
Electrolysis of water: cathode gives H₂ (2 volumes), anode gives O₂ (1 volume).
Applications: extraction of aluminium (Hall-Héroult process), refining of copper, chlor-alkali process.

10|chemistry|7_Metallurgy.overview: Metallurgy covers the extraction of metals from their ores. The process depends on the reactivity of the metal: very reactive metals use electrolysis; moderately reactive use reduction with carbon/CO; less reactive use displacement or chemical reduction.

key_concepts.definition: Mineral: naturally occurring compound of a metal. Ore: mineral from which metal can be extracted profitably.
Metal oxides reduced to metals by carbon, CO, hydrogen, or aluminium (thermite) or electrolysis. Example: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂.
Metals above carbon in activity series extracted by electrolysis (Na, Mg, Al). Metals below carbon extracted by carbon reduction (Fe, Cu at the bottom extracted easily).

key_points: Aluminium: extracted by electrolysis of bauxite (Al₂O₃) dissolved in molten cryolite — Hall-Héroult process.
Iron: extracted in blast furnace from haematite (Fe₂O₃). CaO removes silica impurities as slag.
Copper: low in reactivity series; can be extracted by roasting copper sulphide ore: 2CuS + 3O₂ → 2CuO + 2SO₂, then reduced by carbon.
Refining of metals: electrolytic refining gives very pure metal (impure metal as anode, pure metal as cathode).
Alloys: mixtures of metals. Steel (Fe+C), brass (Cu+Zn), bronze (Cu+Sn), solder (Pb+Sn) — properties differ from pure metals.

10|chemistry|8_Study_of_Compounds.overview: This chapter covers the detailed study of specific industrial and laboratory compounds: sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄), nitric acid (HNO₃), and ammonia (NH₃) — their preparation, properties, and uses.

key_concepts.definition: Prepared industrially by Contact Process: S→SO₂→SO₃→H₂SO₄. Oleum (H₂SO₄·SO₃) formed; diluted to give concentrated H₂SO₄. A strong, dibasic acid.
Prepared by Haber Process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst). Lab: heating ammonium salt with alkali.
Prepared by Ostwald's Process: NH₃ oxidised with O₂ on Pt/Rh catalyst → NO → NO₂ → HNO₃ with water. A strong, monobasic, oxidising acid.

key_points: H₂SO₄ concentrated: dehydrating agent (removes water from organic compounds); oxidising agent; sulphonating agent.
Dilute H₂SO₄: reacts with metals above H in series to produce H₂; reacts with carbonates to produce CO₂.
NH₃: basic; reacts with acids to form ammonium salts; used in manufacture of fertilisers (urea, ammonium nitrate), HNO₃.
HNO₃ dilute: reacts with metals (no H₂ gas); dilute HNO₃ + copper → NO gas.
Concentrated HNO₃ + copper → NO₂ (brown fumes). Aqua regia: 3 parts conc. HCl + 1 part conc. HNO₃ — dissolves gold.

10|chemistry|9_Organic_Chemistry.overview: Organic chemistry is the study of carbon compounds. Class 10 covers hydrocarbons (alkanes, alkenes, alkynes), functional groups, IUPAC nomenclature, and important reactions of alcohols and carboxylic acids.

key_concepts.definition: A series of organic compounds with the same functional group, differing by -CH₂- units, and following the same general formula. E.g., Alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
An atom or group of atoms responsible for the characteristic reactions of the compound. E.g., −OH (alcohol), −COOH (carboxylic acid), −CHO (aldehyde), >C=O (ketone), −NH₂ (amine).
1-Identify longest carbon chain (parent name). 2-Number from end nearest substituent. 3-Name substituents with locant. 4-Add suffix for functional group. Example: CH₃CH₂OH = Ethanol.

laws_and_formulas.name: General Formulas
laws_and_formulas.formula: Alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Alkenes: CₙH₂ₙ. Alkynes: CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. Benzene: C₆H₆.

key_points: Alkanes: saturated (only C-C single bonds); undergo substitution reactions with halogens (light-catalysed).
Alkenes: one C=C double bond; undergo addition reactions: H₂ (hydrogenation), X₂ (halogenation), H₂O (hydration).
Ethanol (C₂H₅OH): reacts with Na (H₂ gas); oxidised to ethanoic acid; esterification with carboxylic acids.
Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH): weak acid; forms esters (esterification with alcohol + conc. H₂SO₄); reacts with carbonates (CO₂).
Isomers: same molecular formula, different structural formula. Example: n-butane and isobutane (C₄H₁₀).

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Chromosomes_Str.overview: Chromosomes are thread-like structures of DNA and protein found in the nucleus, carrying genetic information. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Structure and morphology vary based on centromere position.

key_concepts.definition: Composed of DNA wound around histone proteins (nucleosomes → chromatin). Visible as distinct X-shaped structures during cell division.
Metacentric (centromere central, arms equal). Submetacentric (centromere slightly off-centre). Acrocentric (centromere near end). Telocentric (centromere at tip).
Autosomes (22 pairs in humans): determine body traits. Sex chromosomes (1 pair): XX = female; XY = male.

key_points: Diploid (2n): two copies of each chromosome. Haploid (n): one copy — in gametes.
Chromatids: two identical copies of a chromosome joined at centromere — visible after DNA replication.
Karyotype: photographic display of all chromosomes of a cell, arranged in pairs by size and shape.
Chromosomal abnormalities: Down syndrome (trisomy 21 — extra chromosome 21).
Chromatin: loosely packed form during interphase; condenses into visible chromosomes during division.

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Genetics_Mendel.overview: Gregor Mendel's experiments on pea plants established the principles of heredity. His two laws — Segregation and Independent Assortment — explain how traits are passed from parents to offspring.

key_concepts.definition: Each organism carries two alleles for each trait; these segregate during gamete formation so each gamete carries one allele. Also called Law of Purity of Gametes.
Alleles of different genes assort independently during gamete formation. Applies when genes are on different chromosomes (not linked).
Dominant allele (T): expressed in both TT and Tt. Recessive allele (t): expressed only in tt. Phenotype: observable trait. Genotype: genetic constitution.

laws_and_formulas.name: Monohybrid Cross Ratio
laws_and_formulas.formula: F₂: 3 Dominant : 1 Recessive (phenotype). Genotype ratio: 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt

laws_and_formulas.name: Dihybrid Cross Ratio
laws_and_formulas.formula: F₂: 9 : 3 : 3 : 1 (phenotype ratio for two independent traits)

key_points: Punnett square: grid to predict offspring genotype ratios from parental crosses.
Test cross: cross an unknown genotype with a homozygous recessive (tt). If any offspring is tt, the unknown was Tt.
Incomplete dominance: neither allele is fully dominant — F₁ is intermediate (red × white → pink in snapdragons).
Co-dominance: both alleles expressed equally (e.g., AB blood type).
ABO blood group: multiple alleles (IA, IB, i). A and B are co-dominant; O (i) is recessive.

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Cell_Division_M.overview: Mitosis is cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells from one parent cell. It is responsible for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction in eukaryotes.

key_concepts.definition: Not division — preparation phase. G1 (cell growth), S (DNA replication — chromosome number remains 2n but DNA is doubled), G2 (further growth).

PMAT: Prophase (chromosomes condense, spindle forms, nuclear envelope breaks), Metaphase (chromosomes align at equator), Anaphase (sister chromatids separate to poles), Telophase (nuclear envelopes reform, chromosomes decondense).
Division of cytoplasm after nuclear division. Animal cells: cleavage furrow. Plant cells: cell plate forms (because of rigid cell wall).

key_points: Mitosis maintains chromosome number: 2n → 2n.
Significance: growth of multicellular organisms, repair of tissues, asexual reproduction (budding, fragmentation).
Spindle fibres (made of tubulin protein): attach to centromeres at kinetochores; pull chromatids apart.
Plant vs Animal: animal cells have centrioles; plant cells do not. Cell plate forms instead of cleavage furrow.
Abnormal mitosis: uncontrolled cell division = cancer (cells escape normal cell cycle control).

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Cell_Division_M.overview: Meiosis is a two-stage cell division that produces four haploid cells (gametes) from one diploid cell. It is essential for sexual reproduction and introduces genetic variation through crossing over and random assortment.

key_concepts.definition: Separates homologous chromosomes: 2n → n. Prophase I: synapsis and crossing over (chiasmata). Metaphase I: bivalents at equator. Anaphase I: homologs separate.
Similar to mitosis: separates sister chromatids: n → n. Each cell from Meiosis I divides again to give 4 haploid cells.
Exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during Prophase I. Creates recombinant chromosomes — source of new genetic combinations.

key_points: Meiosis: 2n → 4 haploid cells (n). Maintains chromosome number across generations when gametes fuse.
Significance of crossing over: produces new allele combinations → increases genetic diversity.
Tetrad (bivalent): pair of homologous chromosomes joined at chiasmata during Prophase I.
Spermatogenesis: one cell → 4 sperm (all functional). Oogenesis: one cell → 1 egg + 3 polar bodies.
Meiosis I is called reductional (chromosome number halved); Meiosis II is equational (like mitosis).

10|biology|Basic_Biology_Cell_Cycle.overview: The cell cycle is the ordered sequence of events from cell formation to cell division. It comprises interphase (G1, S, G2) and M phase (mitosis + cytokinesis). Cell cycle checkpoints prevent errors.

key_concepts.definition: Cell grows in size; synthesises proteins and organelles. Decision point: if conditions are favourable, cell commits to division (G1 checkpoint).
DNA replication occurs — genome is duplicated. Each chromosome now consists of two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere.
Further cell growth; synthesises proteins needed for cell division (e.g., tubulin for spindle). G2 checkpoint ensures DNA replication is complete.

key_points: Interphase (G1 + S + G2) takes ~90% of the cell cycle time. M phase is relatively brief.
Cell cycle checkpoints: G1, G2, and spindle assembly checkpoints ensure fidelity.
Tumour suppressor genes (e.g., p53) halt the cell cycle if DNA is damaged — mutations cause cancer.
Go phase: cells that exit the cycle and remain non-dividing (neurons, muscle cells).
Growth factors: external signals that stimulate cells to enter or progress through the cell cycle.

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Absorption_b.overview: Water and mineral absorption by plant roots involves diffusion, osmosis, and active transport. Root hairs increase surface area for absorption. Water moves via the osmotic gradient from soil to xylem.

key_concepts.definition: Movement of water from a region of higher water potential (dilute solution) to lower water potential (concentrated solution) through a semi-permeable membrane.
Movement of substances from high to low concentration (down a concentration gradient). No energy required (passive process).
Movement of ions against concentration gradient, requiring energy (ATP) and carrier proteins. Used for mineral ion absorption by root hair cells.

laws_and_formulas.name: Osmotic Pressure
laws_and_formulas.formula: Turgor pressure: water potential (ψ) = osmotic potential (ψs) + pressure potential (ψp). Water moves from high ψ to low ψ.

key_points: Root hair cells: long extensions of epidermal cells; increase surface area; thin cell wall; high surface-to-volume ratio.
Path of water: root hair → cortex → endodermis (Casparian strip) → xylem (apoplast and symplast pathways).
Plasmolysis: plant cell in concentrated (hypertonic) solution loses water by osmosis; protoplasm shrinks away from cell wall.
Deplasmolysis: plasmolysed cell placed in hypotonic solution regains turgor.
Mineral absorption: mostly by active transport — requires metabolic energy (respiration); selective for specific ions.

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Photosynthes.overview: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy (glucose) using CO₂ and H₂O in chloroplasts. It occurs in two stages: the light-dependent reactions (thylakoids) and the Calvin cycle (stroma).

key_concepts.definition: Occur in thylakoid membranes. Chlorophyll absorbs light; water is split (photolysis) → O₂ is released; ATP and NADPH produced.
Occur in stroma. CO₂ fixed using ATP and NADPH; glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) produced. Does not directly require light.
Green pigment in chloroplasts that absorbs red and blue-violet light; reflects green. Chlorophyll a is the primary pigment; b, carotenoids are accessory pigments.

key_points: Leaf adaptations: broad, flat (max surface area); thin (CO₂ diffuses easily); stomata (gas exchange); chloroplasts mainly in mesophyll cells.
Factors affecting rate: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature, water availability.
Compensation point: light intensity where photosynthesis rate = respiration rate (no net gas exchange).
C₃ vs C₄ plants: C₄ plants (sugarcane, maize) are more efficient in hot, dry conditions.
Oxygen released in photosynthesis comes from water (proved by isotope labelling with ¹⁸O).

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Transpiratio.overview: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from the aerial parts of plants, mainly through stomata. It creates a transpiration pull that draws water up the xylem from roots to leaves.

key_concepts.definition: 90–95% of water loss occurs through stomata (pores in leaf epidermis). Stomata open during the day (light → guard cells become turgid) and close at night.
Cohesion-tension mechanism: water molecules are cohesive (stick together) and adhesive (to xylem walls). Evaporation at leaves creates tension, pulling water up as a continuous column.
Temperature (↑ T → ↑ evaporation → ↑ transpiration), humidity (↑ humidity → ↓ transpiration), light (opens stomata), wind (removes water vapour → ↑ rate), leaf surface area.

key_points: Wilting: excessive transpiration without enough root absorption → loss of turgor pressure.
Potometer: apparatus used to measure rate of transpiration (by measuring water uptake).
Advantages of transpiration: mineral transport (xylem sap), cooling of leaf surface, maintains water flow.
Guard cells: bean-shaped cells flanking stomata. When turgid → stomata open; when flaccid → stomata close.
Xerophytes: desert plants adapted to reduce transpiration — thick cuticle, sunken stomata, rolled leaves, few stomata.

10|biology|Plant_Physiology_Chemical_Coo.overview: Plant hormones (phytohormones) coordinate growth and responses without a nervous system. Key hormones: auxins (growth), gibberellins (elongation), cytokinins (division), abscisic acid (dormancy), ethylene (ripening).

key_concepts.definition: Produced in shoot tips; promote cell elongation. Responsible for phototropism and geotropism. High concentrations inhibit root growth but promote shoot growth.
Promote stem elongation, seed germination, breaking dormancy, and fruit development (parthenocarpy).
Stress hormone. Promotes stomatal closure during drought; causes dormancy of seeds and buds; inhibits growth. 'Anti-gibberellin' in many respects.

key_points: Phototropism: shoot bends toward light due to auxin accumulation on shaded side → greater elongation there.
Geotropism: roots grow toward gravity (positive geotropism); shoots grow away (negative geotropism).
Cytokinins: promote cell division; delay leaf senescence (aging) — used in tissue culture.
Ethylene: gaseous hormone; promotes fruit ripening and leaf abscission; used commercially to ripen bananas.
Apical dominance: high auxin from shoot tip suppresses lateral bud growth. Removing tip → bushier plant.

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The human circulatory system comprises the heart, blood vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries), and blood. It transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste products. The heart maintains a double circulation (pulmonary and systemic).

key_concepts.definition: Blood passes through the heart twice per circuit. Pulmonary: right side pumps deoxygenated blood to lungs. Systemic: left side pumps oxygenated blood to body.
4 chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle. Valves: tricuspid (right), bicuspid/mitral (left), pulmonary and aortic semilunar valves prevent backflow.
Plasma (55%): liquid. Red blood cells (RBCs/erythrocytes): carry O₂ via haemoglobin. White blood cells (WBCs/leucocytes): immunity. Platelets (thrombocytes): clotting.

laws_and_formulas.name: Cardiac Output
laws_and_formulas.formula: Cardiac output = Heart rate × Stroke volume. Normal: ~5 L/min at rest.

key_points: Arteries: carry blood away from heart; thick muscular walls; high pressure. Veins: carry blood to heart; valves prevent backflow; low pressure. Capillaries: one cell thick; exchange site.
Cardiac cycle: systole (contraction), diastole (relaxation). Heart rate: ~72 beats/min.
Blood pressure: systolic (120 mmHg)/diastolic (80 mmHg). Hypertension: > 140/90.
Haemoglobin: protein in RBCs; carries O₂ (oxyhaemoglobin) and CO₂ (carbaminohaemoglobin).
Lymphatic system: drains excess tissue fluid; contains lymph nodes (filter bacteria); lymphocytes mature here.

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The excretory system removes metabolic waste from the body. The kidneys are the main excretory organs, filtering blood to produce urine. Nephrons are the functional units of the kidney.

key_concepts.definition: Bean-shaped organs. Cortex (outer, contains glomeruli and Bowman's capsules). Medulla (inner, contains loops of Henle and collecting ducts). Pelvis collects urine → ureter → bladder.

Glomerular filtration: blood pressure forces small molecules into Bowman's capsule (filtrate). Selective reabsorption: glucose, amino acids, water reabsorbed in tubules. Tubular secretion: extra H⁺, toxins secreted into filtrate.
Regulation of water and salt balance. ADH (antidiuretic hormone) from pituitary: increases water reabsorption → concentrated urine when dehydrated.

laws_and_formulas.name: Urine Composition
laws_and_formulas.formula: ~95% water, ~2% urea, ~1% creatinine, ~2% salts (NaCl), trace others. Normal urine output: 1–1.5 L/day.

key_points: Excretory products: urea (protein metabolism), uric acid (nucleic acid), CO₂ (respiration), water, salts.
Dialysis (kidney failure): blood passed through a dialysis machine acting as an artificial kidney.
Kidney stones: crystallised salts (calcium oxalate); can block ureter causing severe pain.
Liver's role: converts ammonia (toxic) to urea (less toxic) — urea synthesis (ornithine cycle).
Skin (sweat): excretes water, salts, traces of urea. Lungs: excrete CO₂ and water vapour.

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The nervous system coordinates and regulates body functions through electrical signals (nerve impulses). It comprises the Central Nervous System (CNS) and Peripheral Nervous System (PNS), with specialised receptor-effector pathways.

key_concepts.definition: Structural and functional unit. Parts: cell body (cyton), dendrites (receive signals), axon (transmit signals), myelin sheath (insulation, speeds conduction), synaptic terminals.
Junction between two neurons. Signal crosses via neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine) released into the synaptic cleft — only allows one-way transmission.
Receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector. A reflex is an automatic, rapid, involuntary response. Reflex arc bypasses brain.

key_points: CNS: Brain (cerebrum, cerebellum, medulla oblongata) + Spinal cord. PNS: all nerves outside CNS.
Cerebrum: intelligence, memory, conscious thought, voluntary movement, sensory perception.
Cerebellum: balance and coordination. Medulla oblongata: controls involuntary actions (breathing, heartbeat).
Autonomic NS: controls involuntary organs. Sympathetic ('fight-or-flight'): increases heart rate, dilates pupils. Parasympathetic ('rest-and-digest'): slows heart rate.
Types of neurons: sensory (afferent), motor (efferent), relay/interneurons.

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: The endocrine system coordinates body functions through chemical messengers called hormones, secreted directly into the bloodstream by ductless glands. Unlike the nervous system, endocrine responses are slower but longer lasting.

key_concepts.definition: A chemical messenger produced by an endocrine gland, transported in blood, and acting on target organs/cells. Examples: insulin, adrenaline, thyroxin, oestrogen, testosterone.

Pituitary: master gland; GH, TSH, ADH, oxytocin. Thyroid: thyroxin (regulates metabolism). Pancreas: insulin (lowers blood glucose), glucagon (raises it). Adrenal: adrenaline (emergency hormone). Gonads: sex hormones.
Mechanism that keeps hormone levels balanced. High hormone level → inhibits further secretion. E.g., high blood glucose → more insulin released → blood glucose falls → insulin secretion reduces.

key_points: Diabetes mellitus: insufficient insulin (Type 1) or ineffective insulin (Type 2) → high blood glucose → managed with insulin injection or diet.
Dwarfism: deficiency of GH in childhood. Gigantism: excess GH in childhood. Acromegaly: excess GH in adulthood.
Goitre: iodine deficiency → thyroid enlarges (cannot make thyroxin).
Adrenaline: 'fight-or-flight' hormone — increases heart rate, dilates bronchi, raises blood glucose, diverts blood to muscles.
Hypothalamus: interface between nervous and endocrine systems — controls pituitary gland.

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: Human reproduction is sexual — involving the fusion of male (sperm) and female (egg) gametes. The male reproductive system produces and delivers sperm; the female system produces eggs, houses and nourishes the developing foetus.

key_concepts.definition: Testes: produce sperm (spermatogenesis) and testosterone. Epididymis: sperm maturation and storage. Vas deferens → urethra: sperm transport. Seminal vesicles, prostate: provide seminal fluid.
Ovaries: produce eggs (oogenesis) and sex hormones. Fallopian tubes: site of fertilisation. Uterus: implantation and development of embryo. Cervix and vagina: birth canal.
~28-day cycle regulated by FSH, LH, oestrogen, progesterone. Menstruation (days 1–5) → follicular phase → ovulation (day 14) → luteal phase → menstruation again if no fertilisation.

key_points: Fertilisation: occurs in fallopian tube → zygote formed → divides to form blastocyst → implants in uterus wall.
Placenta: exchange organ between mother and foetus — O₂ and nutrients pass to foetus; CO₂ and wastes pass to mother.
Contraception methods: barrier (condoms), hormonal (pill), intrauterine device (IUD), surgical (vasectomy/tubectomy).
STIs (Sexually Transmitted Infections): HIV/AIDS, gonorrhoea, syphilis — spread through unprotected sex.
Puberty: hormonal changes trigger secondary sexual characteristics — boys: testosterone; girls: oestrogen.

10|biology|Human_Anatomy_and_Physiology_.overview: Human population has grown exponentially, especially since the industrial revolution, with significant implications for resources, environment, and quality of life. India is the world's most populous country.

key_concepts.definition: Birth rate: number of live births per 1000 people per year. Death rate: number of deaths per 1000 per year. Natural increase = Birth rate − Death rate.
Shift from high birth and death rates (Stage 1) through improving health/declining death rate (Stage 2) to low birth and death rates (Stage 4) as countries develop.
Maximum population size an environment can sustainably support given available resources. Exceeded → environmental degradation.

key_points: World population: ~8 billion (2024); India: ~1.44 billion (most populous). Historical doubling times are shrinking.
Consequences of overpopulation: resource depletion (water, food, fuel), deforestation, pollution, unemployment, poverty.
Family planning: educating communities about birth control, spacing of children, small family norms.
Infant mortality rate (IMR): number of infant deaths per 1000 live births. Lower IMR is indicator of development.
Ageing population: developed countries face inverted age pyramids — more elderly, fewer working age — economic burden.

10|biology|Physical_Health_and_Hygiene.overview: Good health requires maintaining physical, mental, and social well-being. Class 10 covers communicable diseases, their transmission and prevention, and the role of vaccination and personal hygiene.

key_concepts.definition: Diseases spread from person to person or via a vector. Modes: direct contact, droplet infection, contaminated food/water, vector (mosquito, housefly).
Injecting weakened/dead pathogens or antigens to stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies, creating memory cells for future protection.
Cannot spread between individuals. Examples: diabetes, hypertension, heart disease, cancer — linked to lifestyle factors.

key_points: Malaria: Plasmodium (protozoan) transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito. Symptoms: cyclic fever. Prevention: mosquito nets, insecticides, avoiding stagnant water.
Typhoid: Salmonella typhi via contaminated food/water. Prevention: clean water, sanitation, typhoid vaccine.
HIV/AIDS: attacks helper T-cells (CD4+); transmitted through blood, unprotected sex, contaminated needles, mother to child. No cure; managed with antiretroviral therapy.
Personal hygiene: regular bathing, hand washing, dental care, clean food/water — first line of defence against communicable diseases.
Vector control: eliminating breeding sites of mosquitoes and flies reduces malaria, dengue, typhoid.

10|biology|Pollution.overview: Pollution is the introduction of harmful substances into the natural environment, causing adverse changes. Types: air, water, soil, and noise pollution. Each has specific causes, effects, and control measures.

key_concepts.definition: Pollutants: CO, CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, particulates, ozone (ground level). Sources: vehicle emissions, industries, burning fossil fuels. Effects: smog, acid rain, global warming, respiratory diseases.
Pollutants: sewage, industrial effluents, agricultural chemicals (nitrates, pesticides), heavy metals. Effects: eutrophication (algal bloom → O₂ depletion), waterborne diseases.
Pollutants: pesticides, fertilisers, industrial waste, plastic. Effects: reduced soil fertility, bioaccumulation in food chains, contamination of groundwater.

key_points: Greenhouse effect: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, water vapour trap heat → global warming → climate change.
Ozone depletion: CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) break down ozone (O₃) in stratosphere → increased UV reaching Earth → skin cancer.
Acid rain: SO₂ + NOₓ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ + HNO₃ → pH < 5.6. Damages crops, corrodes buildings, acidifies water bodies.
Noise pollution: > 85 dB prolonged exposure causes hearing damage. Sources: traffic, aircraft, industry.
Control measures: catalytic converters, scrubbers, effluent treatment plants, composting, reduce-reuse-recycle, renewable energy.

10|history_civics|history_1_The_First_Wa.overview: The Revolt of 1857 was the first major armed uprising against British rule in India. Beginning as a sepoy mutiny, it quickly spread to include princes, zamindars, and civilians across northern and central India.

key_concepts.definition: Political (Doctrine of Lapse — Dalhousie annexing states), economic (drain of wealth, ruin of Indian industries), social (interference with customs, caste), military (discrimination, low pay, greased cartridges) and religious (fear of conversion).
Enfield rifle cartridges rumoured to be greased with cow and pig fat — offending both Hindu and Muslim sepoys; immediate trigger of the revolt.
Mangal Pandey (Barrackpore), Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi, Nana Sahib (Kanpur), Tantia Tope, Begum Hazrat Mahal (Lucknow), Bahadur Shah Zafar (Delhi — nominal leader).

key_points: Started at Meerut on 10 May 1857; spread to Delhi, Kanpur, Lucknow, Jhansi, Bareilly.
Suppressed by 1858; last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar exiled to Rangoon.
Result: British Crown assumed direct control of India from the East India Company (Government of India Act, 1858).
Significance: first expression of organised resistance to British rule; symbol of unity for later nationalists.
Limitations: limited to northern/central India; no unified leadership; no common goal beyond immediate grievances.

10|history_civics|history_2_Growth_of_Na.overview: Indian nationalism emerged in the 19th century as educated Indians became aware of their common identity and subjugation. Western education, the press, and social reform movements planted the seeds of national consciousness.

key_concepts.definition: Western education (exposed Indians to democratic ideals), press and literature (spread ideas), racial discrimination (unified Indians), economic exploitation (drain theory — Dadabhai Naoroji), social reforms, and memories of 1857.
Dadabhai Naoroji's concept that British rule caused a systematic 'drain' of India's wealth to Britain — export surplus not matched by financial returns.
Newspapers (Amrit Bazar Patrika, Kesari, Hindu) spread nationalist ideas and exposed British policies. Vernacular Press Act (1878) tried to suppress it but backfired.

key_points: Indian National Congress founded in 1885 by A.O. Hume with Lord Dufferin's approval — initially moderate.
Social reformers (Ram Mohan Roy, Vivekananda, Dayanand Saraswati) instilled pride in Indian culture.
Vernacular literature: poets and writers used their language to awaken national consciousness.
Ilbert Bill Controversy (1883): Europeans agitated against equal treatment under law — exposed racial discrimination.
Transportation (railways) and telegraph unified India geographically, facilitating nationalist communication.

10|history_civics|history_3_First_Phase_.overview: The first phase (1885–1905) was dominated by moderate leaders who believed in constitutional agitation, petitions, and prayers to achieve self-governance within the British Empire. The Indian National Congress was the main platform.

key_concepts.definition: Petitions, memorials, resolutions, speeches in legislature, press campaigns. Moderates believed in the goodness of British rule and sought reforms through persuasion.
Dadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Surendranath Banerjea, Pherozeshah Mehta, Badruddin Tyabji.
Indianisation of civil services, reduction of military expenditure, abolition of India Council, representative government, economic relief.

key_points: INC annual sessions passed resolutions and petitions — 'three P's': Petition, Prayer, Protest.
Moderates had faith in British justice; later nationalists criticised this as 'mendicancy'.
Economic criticism: B.G. Tilak, R.C. Dutt criticised drain of wealth.
1892 Indian Councils Act: minor concession — additional members, some elected indirectly.
Limitations: narrow social base (educated elites), ignored masses, no mass mobilisation.

10|history_civics|history_4_Second_Phase.overview: The second phase saw the rise of extremist/assertive nationalists who rejected moderate methods and demanded Swaraj (self-rule). The Partition of Bengal (1905) triggered the Swadeshi movement — a turning point in Indian nationalism.

key_concepts.definition: Curzon divided Bengal into Bengal (Hindu majority) and East Bengal (Muslim majority) — administrative pretext but divisive intent. United Indians in opposition; triggered Swadeshi movement.
Use of Indian-made goods; boycott of British goods; promotion of Indian education, enterprise. First mass movement involving ordinary people, not just elites.
Bal Gangadhar Tilak (Maharashtra), Bipin Chandra Pal (Bengal), Lala Lajpat Rai (Punjab). Demanded Swaraj as a birthright; used mass agitation; revived Hindu symbols.

key_points: Tilak's rallying cry: 'Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it!'
Partition of Bengal annulled in 1911 — first nationalist victory forcing British reversal.
Home Rule Movement (1916): Tilak and Annie Besant launched leagues demanding self-government.
Surat Split (1907): INC divided into Moderates and Extremists at Surat session.
Muslim League founded in 1906 — claimed to represent Muslim interests separately from INC.

10|history_civics|history_5_The_Muslim_L.overview: The All India Muslim League was founded in 1906 to protect Muslim political interests. Initially loyal to the British, it gradually moved toward demanding a separate homeland for Muslims, culminating in the Lahore Resolution of 1940.

key_concepts.definition: Founded at Dhaka by Nawab Salimullah and the Aga Khan. Initial aim: protect Muslim interests and maintain loyalty to the British Crown.
Indian Councils Act; introduced separate electorates for Muslims — a British strategy to divide Hindus and Muslims ('divide and rule'). League had lobbied for this.
Agreement between INC (Tilak) and Muslim League (Jinnah) — cooperation for self-government; Congress accepted separate electorates for Muslims.

key_points: Jinnah: initially secular nationalist in INC; gradually moved to League and two-nation theory.
Two-Nation Theory: Hindus and Muslims are two distinct nations — cannot coexist in one state. Used to justify Pakistan.
Lahore Resolution (1940): League demanded independent Muslim state(s) — seed of Pakistan demand.
Communal riots and British policy: League consistently used communal tension to strengthen its position.
1946 elections: League won most Muslim seats — further strengthened case for partition.

10|history_civics|history_6_Mahatma_Gand.overview: Gandhi transformed the INC into a mass movement using non-violent civil disobedience (Satyagraha). His methods — non-cooperation, civil disobedience, salt march — mobilised millions across caste and class lines.

key_concepts.definition: 'Truth-force' or 'soul-force' — non-violent resistance to injustice. Participants willingly suffer punishment without retaliating. First used in South Africa against racial discrimination.
Surrender of titles, boycott of legislatures/courts/schools, no-tax campaigns, swadeshi. Withdrawn by Gandhi after Chauri Chaura violence (1922).
Began with Dandi March (salt satyagraha) — defying British salt monopoly. Mass civil disobedience nationwide; Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931) temporarily suspended it.

key_points: Rowlatt Act (1919): allowed detention without trial — sparked widespread protests, Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
Jallianwala Bagh (13 April 1919): General Dyer ordered firing on peaceful crowd — ~1000 killed; turning point in nationalism.
Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931): government released political prisoners, conceded salt production; Gandhi attended Round Table Conference.
Constructive programme: khadi, Hindu-Muslim unity, untouchability removal, women's empowerment.
Role of women: massive participation in CDM — picketing liquor shops, joining salt marches.

10|history_civics|history_7_Quit_India_M.overview: The Quit India Movement (1942) was Gandhi's most radical call — 'Do or Die'. India's independence (1947) came alongside painful Partition, dividing the subcontinent into India and Pakistan amid communal violence.

key_concepts.definition: 'Do or Die' — Gandhi called for immediate British withdrawal. All INC leaders arrested; underground movement took over. Mass protests, strikes, sabotage of communications.
Proposed Dominion status after the war; rejected by INC as 'a post-dated cheque on a crashing bank' (Gandhi). Failure precipitated Quit India Movement.
Mountbatten Plan (June 1947): India and Pakistan to be two separate dominions. Punjab and Bengal divided by the Radcliffe Line. Massive communal violence and refugee crisis (10–15 million displaced).

key_points: Indian National Army (INA): Subhas Chandra Bose led Indian soldiers against British; 'Jai Hind'.
Naval Mutiny (1946): ratings of Royal Indian Navy revolted — showed British could no longer rely on armed forces.
Independence: 15 August 1947 — India independent; Pakistan created; Jawaharlal Nehru first PM.
Integration of princely states: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (Iron Man) persuaded/pressured 562 princely states to join India.
Consequences: communal violence, millions displaced, Kashmir dispute (ongoing), formation of two nations.

10|history_civics|history_8_The_First_Wo.overview: World War I (1914–18) was triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and grew from a web of alliances, militarism, imperialism, and nationalism into a devastating global conflict costing ~20 million lives.

key_concepts.definition: Militarism (arms race), Alliance system (Triple Alliance vs Triple Entente), Imperialism (competition for colonies), Nationalism (especially in Balkans), Assassination (Franz Ferdinand, Sarajevo, June 1914).
Triple Alliance: Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy. Triple Entente: France, Russia, Britain. Meant a local conflict escalated into a world war.
Trench warfare, poison gas, tanks, aeroplanes, submarines (U-boats). Stalemate on Western Front for most of the war.

key_points: Eastern Front: Germany vs Russia; Russia withdrew after 1917 revolution — Treaty of Brest-Litovsk.
USA joined (1917): unrestricted German submarine warfare and Zimmermann Telegram turned USA against Germany.
Armistice: 11 November 1918 — Germany surrendered. Kaiser abdicated.
Treaty of Versailles (1919): Germany blamed (War Guilt Clause), heavy reparations, loss of territory, limited army — humiliating terms that sowed seeds of WW2.
League of Nations: established to prevent future wars — US Senate refused to join; weakened it fatally.

10|history_civics|history_9_Rise_of_Dict.overview: Post-WWI economic depression and political instability allowed Fascism (Mussolini in Italy) and Nazism (Hitler in Germany) to rise. Both were totalitarian, ultranationalist, and militarist ideologies that directly led to WWII.

key_concepts.definition: Mussolini came to power in 1922 (March on Rome). Features: totalitarianism, nationalism, corporate state, suppression of opposition, expansionism. Slogan: 'Everything within the state, nothing outside the state.'

Hitler's National Socialism. Features: racial superiority (Aryan master race), antisemitism, Lebensraum (living space), totalitarian rule, Hitler as Führer. Holocaust: systematic murder of 6 million Jews.
Humiliation of Versailles (Germany), Great Depression (unemployment, poverty), weak democratic governments, communist fear, effective propaganda, charismatic leaders.

key_points: Mussolini created first fascist state; ally of Hitler (Rome-Berlin-Tokyo Axis formed 1936).
Hitler appointed Chancellor 1933; became Führer 1934. Systematically dismantled democracy (Enabling Act).
Nuremberg Laws (1935): stripped Jews of citizenship — precursor to Holocaust.
Appeasement: Britain and France allowed Hitler's early territorial demands (Munich Agreement, 1938) hoping to avoid war.
Anschluss (1938): Germany annexed Austria; Munich: Sudetenland taken; Poland invaded 1939 → WW2 began.

10|history_civics|history_10_The_Second_.overview: World War II (1939–45) was the deadliest conflict in history (~70–85 million deaths). Fought between the Allied Powers (Britain, France, USSR, USA, China) and the Axis Powers (Germany, Italy, Japan) across Europe, Asia, and Africa.

key_concepts.definition: Germany invaded Poland (1 Sep 1939); Britain and France declared war on Germany. Fall of France (1940); Battle of Britain (RAF vs Luftwaffe). Germany invaded USSR (Operation Barbarossa, 1941).

Japan attacked Pearl Harbor (7 Dec 1941) → USA entered war. Japan swept Southeast Asia; turned at Battle of Midway (1942). USA dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima (6 Aug) and Nagasaki (9 Aug) 1945 → Japan surrendered.
Nazi Germany's systematic murder of 6 million Jews and ~5 million others (Roma, disabled, political opponents, LGBTQ+) using gas chambers and death camps.

key_points: Turning points: Battle of Stalingrad (1942–43, German defeat in USSR), D-Day (6 June 1944, Allied invasion of Normandy).
Germany surrendered 8 May 1945 (VE Day). Japan 15 Aug 1945 (VJ Day).
Yalta Conference (1945): Churchill, Roosevelt, Stalin — planned post-war Europe; agreed on UN; USSR to join Pacific war.
Consequences: ~70 million dead; massive destruction; Cold War began; UN formed; decolonisation accelerated; nuclear age began.
Nuremberg Trials (1945–46): Nazi leaders tried for war crimes and crimes against humanity — established international law precedent.

10|history_civics|history_11_United_Nati.overview: The United Nations was established in 1945 to prevent future wars, promote peace, human rights, and international cooperation. It replaced the failed League of Nations and now has 193 member states.

key_concepts.definition: UN Charter signed 26 June 1945 (San Francisco); came into force 24 October 1945 (UN Day). Originally 51 members. India is a founding member.
Maintain international peace and security; develop friendly relations among nations; achieve international cooperation on economic, social, cultural, humanitarian problems; promote human rights.
General Assembly, Security Council, Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC), Trusteeship Council (largely inactive), International Court of Justice (ICJ), Secretariat.

key_points: General Assembly: all 193 members; one country one vote; advisory resolutions; meets annually.
Security Council: 15 members (5 permanent: USA, UK, France, Russia, China + 10 rotating elected). Primary responsibility for peace and security.
Veto power: any of the 5 permanent members can block a resolution. A significant structural issue.
