Secretary-General: chief administrative officer (currently António Guterres, Portugal).
UN headquarters: New York City; other major offices in Geneva, Vienna, Nairobi.

10|history_civics|history_12_Major_Agenc.overview: The UN system includes specialised agencies that address specific global issues: health, education, children, refugees, food, labour, and more. These agencies work independently but coordinate with the UN.

key_concepts.definition: Coordinates global health responses; eradicated smallpox (1980); leads responses to pandemics (COVID-19); HQ Geneva.
Provides humanitarian aid to children and mothers; programmes in health, nutrition, education, child protection; HQ New York.

key_concepts.term: UNESCO (UN Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization)
key_concepts.definition: Promotes education, science, culture, press freedom; World Heritage Sites designation; HQ Paris.

key_points: FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization): combats hunger; promotes agricultural development; HQ Rome.
UNHCR (UN Refugee Agency): protects refugees and stateless persons; provides shelter, food, legal assistance.
ILO (International Labour Organization): sets labour standards; promotes fair employment worldwide; founded 1919 (pre-UN); HQ Geneva.
IMF and World Bank: financial institutions linked to UN; help developing countries with economic stability and development loans.
IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency): promotes peaceful use of nuclear energy; inspects nuclear facilities to prevent weapons proliferation.

10|history_civics|history_13_Non-Aligned.overview: The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) was formed by developing nations refusing to align with either the USA-led Western Bloc or the USSR-led Eastern Bloc during the Cold War. India's Nehru was a founding leader.

key_concepts.definition: After WW2: USA and USSR in ideological conflict (capitalism vs communism) without direct war. World polarised into two blocs. Developing nations felt pressured to choose sides.
First NAM Summit: Belgrade (1961). Founders: Nehru (India), Tito (Yugoslavia), Nasser (Egypt), Sukarno (Indonesia), Nkrumah (Ghana). Bandung Conference (1955) was a precursor.
Five principles of peaceful coexistence: mutual respect for sovereignty, non-aggression, non-interference, equality, peaceful coexistence. Signed between India and China (1954).

key_points: NAM aimed for political independence from superpower influence and focus on decolonisation and economic development.
India's foreign policy: 'Non-alignment' — maintain independence of judgment in foreign affairs.
Currently 120 member states — largest intergovernmental organisation after UN.
Relevance post-Cold War: shifted focus to economic development, south-south cooperation, global reform.
Criticism: some members were not truly non-aligned and tilted toward one superpower or the other.

10|history_civics|civics_1_The_Union_Par.overview: The Union Parliament is the supreme legislative body of India, comprising the President, Lok Sabha (House of the People), and Rajya Sabha (Council of States). It makes laws on subjects in the Union and Concurrent Lists.

key_concepts.definition: Lower house; 543 elected members + 2 nominated (Anglo-Indians, now abolished). Members elected by universal adult suffrage for 5-year terms. More powerful house for money bills and no-confidence motions.
Upper house; 250 members (238 elected by state legislatures, 12 nominated by President for expertise). Permanent house — cannot be dissolved; 1/3 retire every 2 years.

Bill introduced in either house (except Money Bills — Lok Sabha only), passed by both houses, sent to President for assent → becomes law. Deadlock resolved by joint sitting (Lok Sabha dominates due to size).

key_points: Speaker: presides over Lok Sabha; elected by members. Vice-President is ex-officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha.
Parliament's functions: legislation, control over executive (question hour, no-confidence), budget approval, constitutional amendments.
Money Bill: can only be introduced in Lok Sabha; Rajya Sabha can suggest amendments (not binding); 14-day limit.
Constitutional amendments: Article 368; most require 2/3 majority + ratification by half the states for federal provisions.
Sessions: Budget session (Feb–May), Monsoon session (Jul–Aug), Winter session (Nov–Dec). President summons and prorogues.

10|history_civics|civics_2_The_Union_Exe.overview: The President of India is the constitutional head of state. Executive power vests in the President but is exercised on the advice of the Council of Ministers. The Vice-President serves as ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

key_concepts.definition: Indirect election by an electoral college comprising elected members of both houses of Parliament and elected members of all state legislative assemblies. Single transferable vote with proportional representation.

Executive (appoints PM, ministers, governors), Legislative (summons/prorogues Parliament, gives assent to bills, addresses Parliament), Judicial (pardons, reprieves), Emergency (national, state, financial emergency).
President acts on advice of Council of Ministers (44th Amendment — binding advice). Real executive power is with the PM and Cabinet.

key_points: Qualifications: Indian citizen, ≥35 years, eligible for Lok Sabha membership, not holding profit-making government post.
Term: 5 years. Can be re-elected. Removed by impeachment (by Parliament, 2/3 majority of both houses).
Vice-President: elected by both houses of Parliament. Qualification same as Rajya Sabha membership.
Emergency powers: Article 352 (national emergency), Article 356 (President's Rule in states), Article 360 (financial emergency).
Veto power: can return a bill (except Money Bill) for reconsideration — but must give assent if returned by Parliament.

10|history_civics|civics_3_The_Prime_Min.overview: The Prime Minister is the real head of the executive in India's parliamentary democracy. The Council of Ministers (headed by PM) is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha and is the key decision-making body.

key_concepts.definition: Leader of the party/coalition with majority in Lok Sabha appointed as PM by the President. PM appoints other ministers (President formally appoints on PM's advice).
Three tiers: Cabinet Ministers (most senior, head important departments), Ministers of State, Deputy Ministers. Cabinet is the inner circle that actually governs.
All ministers publicly support cabinet decisions. If a vote of no-confidence is passed against the government, all ministers must resign.

key_points: PM's functions: chairs cabinet, coordinates ministries, advises President, represents India internationally, announces major policies.
PM must be a member of Parliament (if not at appointment, must become one within 6 months).
Cabinet system: confidentiality of cabinet proceedings; ministers resign if they cannot support cabinet policy.
Anti-defection law (10th Schedule): MPs who vote against party whip can lose their seat.
Coalition government: when no single party has majority, parties form coalition; PM from dominant coalition partner.

10|history_civics|civics_4_The_Supreme_C.overview: The Supreme Court of India is the highest court and final interpreter of the Constitution. It protects fundamental rights and settles disputes between the Centre and states. The Constitution guarantees its independence.

key_concepts.definition: Chief Justice of India + up to 33 other judges. Appointed by President on advice of a collegium (senior judges). Retirement age: 65 years.
Disputes between the Union and states, or between states (Article 131). Also fundamental rights (Article 32 — 'heart and soul' of Constitution, per Ambedkar).
Final court of appeal in civil, criminal, and constitutional matters. Certifies cases for appeal from High Courts.

key_points: Constitutional interpretation: SC's interpretation of the Constitution is final and binding on all courts.
Judicial review: SC can declare laws and executive actions unconstitutional (ultra vires).
Public Interest Litigation (PIL): anyone can file a PIL for public interest — powerful tool for enforcing rights.
Independence: judges can only be removed by Parliament (motion passed by 2/3 majority of both houses).
Advisory jurisdiction: President can seek SC's opinion on constitutional questions (Article 143) — but opinion is not binding.

10|history_civics|civics_5_The_High_Cour.overview: Each state has a High Court as the highest court in the state. Below the High Courts are subordinate civil and criminal courts. High Courts exercise original, appellate, and supervisory jurisdiction within their state.

key_concepts.definition: Chief Justice + judges. Appointed by President in consultation with CJI and State Governor. Retirement age: 62 years.

Original (Calcutta, Bombay, Madras have original civil jurisdiction). Appellate (from subordinate courts). Supervisory (over all subordinate courts in state). Writ jurisdiction (Article 226 — broader than SC's Article 32).
Civil courts: District Court, Civil Judge's Court. Criminal courts: Sessions Court, Magistrate's Court. Each headed by officers of the state judicial service.

key_points: High Court is superior to all courts in the state (except SC). SC is the apex.
Transfer of judges: President can transfer HC judges between High Courts.
Writ jurisdiction: HCs can issue writs (habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari, quo warranto) — wider territorial jurisdiction than SC.
Fast-track courts: established for speedy disposal of specific cases (POCSO, rape, corruption).
Subordinate court structure varies by state; general pattern: civil and criminal parallel hierarchies.

10|history_civics|civics_6_Subordinate_C.overview: Lok Adalats (People's Courts) are alternative dispute resolution forums established under the Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987. They provide quick, informal, and free justice, especially for cases that can be settled by compromise.

key_concepts.definition: Forum where disputes are resolved by conciliation and compromise. Verdict is final and binding; no appeal. No court fees; settled amounts not taxable. Cases: motor accident claims, matrimonial, labour, public utility disputes.
Constituted under 1987 Act; provides free legal services to weaker sections (SC/ST, women, children, disabled, victims of disaster). Organises Lok Adalats.
For pre-litigation settlement of disputes related to public utility services. Can give an award even if one party refuses compromise — binding on both.

key_points: Advantage over regular courts: speedy (one-day settlement often), no court fees, final award prevents appeals.
Eligible cases for Lok Adalat: pending in courts or at pre-litigation stage. Both parties must agree to participate.
Motor accident claims: most common type; insurance company settles directly without prolonged trial.
District Legal Services Authority (DLSA): at district level; coordinates legal aid and organises monthly Lok Adalats.
Right to Legal Aid: Article 39A — state must ensure equal justice; free legal aid to poor is a fundamental duty of state.

10|geography|1_Map_Work_Interpretation_o.overview: Topographical maps (topos) are detailed large-scale maps showing natural and man-made features using contours, conventional signs, and colours. Interpreting them is a core Class 10 ICSE skill.

key_concepts.definition: Lines joining points of equal altitude. Closely spaced = steep slope; widely spaced = gentle slope; V-shaped pointing uphill = valley; V-shaped pointing downhill = ridge/spur.
Blue: water bodies. Green: vegetation/forests. Brown: contours/terrain. Black: roads, buildings, railways. Red: major roads, urban areas. Yellow: cultivated land.
Representative Fraction (RF): ratio of map distance to actual distance. Grid references: 4-figure (gives 1km²), 6-figure (gives 100m²). Always read eastings (horizontal) then northings (vertical).

key_points: Spot heights: exact altitude at a specific point printed on the map.
Triangulated heights (benchmarks): precisely surveyed altitude points, shown as triangles.
Identifying landforms from contours: hill (concentric closed circles), plateau (flat top with steep sides), valley (V or U-shape), cliff (contours merge or very close).
Direction: N is towards the top of standard topos. Use the compass rose if present.
ICSE topo questions: identify features, give grid reference, calculate distances, draw cross-sections.

10|geography|2_Location_Extent_and_Physi.overview: India is the seventh-largest country by area (3.29 million km²), located in South Asia between 8°4'N–37°6'N latitude and 68°7'E–97°25'E longitude. Its physical features include the Himalayas, Northern Plains, Peninsular Plateau, Coastal Plains, and Islands.

key_concepts.definition: Young fold mountains along India's northern frontier. Three parallel ranges: Greater Himalayas (Himadri — highest, includes Everest), Middle Himalayas (Himachal — hill stations), Outer Himalayas (Shivaliks — foothills). Origin: collision of Eurasian and Indo-Australian plates.
Alluvial plains of the Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra river systems. Most fertile and densely populated region. Formed by deposition of silt from rivers.
Ancient stable landmass (part of Gondwanaland). Comprises Deccan Plateau, Malwa Plateau, Chotanagpur Plateau. Rich in minerals. Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats bound the coasts.

key_points: Standard Meridian: 82°30'E (IST = UTC+5:30). Located through Allahabad (Mirzapur).
Coastline: ~7517 km. Eastern coast: straight (Coromandel). Western coast: broken (Konkan, Malabar, Gujarat).
Islands: Andaman and Nicobar (Bay of Bengal — volcanic and coral); Lakshadweep (Arabian Sea — coral atolls).
Neighbours: Pakistan (NW), China, Nepal, Bhutan (N), Bangladesh, Myanmar (NE), Sri Lanka (S — separated by Palk Strait).
Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N) passes through: Gujarat, Rajasthan, MP, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, Mizoram.

10|geography|3_Climate.overview: India has a monsoon-influenced climate. The dominant feature is the reversal of winds: the South-West Monsoon (June–September) brings 80% of India's annual rainfall. Climate varies from tropical in the south to alpine in the Himalayas.

key_concepts.definition: Moisture-laden winds from the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal strike India in June (Kerala coast first). Arabian Sea branch: west coast, Western Ghats; Bay of Bengal branch: northeast India, then reverses westward.
October–December: winds reverse (blow from NE to SW). Brings rainfall to Tamil Nadu and SE coast (Bay of Bengal moisture). Rest of India has dry weather.
Latitude (tropical vs temperate), Altitude (decreasing temp with height), Distance from sea (continental vs maritime), Monsoon winds, Relief (windward vs leeward — orographic rainfall).

key_points: Western Ghats: windward side gets very heavy rainfall (Cherrapunji/Mawsynram — world's wettest). Leeward side: rain shadow (Deccan).
El Niño: warming of Pacific Ocean → suppresses Indian monsoon → drought conditions in India.
Seasons: Cold Weather (Dec–Feb), Hot Weather (Mar–May), Southwest Monsoon (Jun–Sep), Retreating Monsoon (Oct–Nov).
Mawsynram (Meghalaya): receives highest average annual rainfall in the world (~11,872 mm).
Rajasthan: lowest rainfall — far from sea, Aravalli doesn't obstruct monsoon, hot dry desert conditions.

10|geography|4_Soil_Resources.overview: India has diverse soil types, each suited to specific crops. The major types are alluvial, black (regur), red, laterite, desert, and mountain soils. Soil erosion is a major concern addressed by conservation measures.

key_concepts.definition: Most extensive and fertile. Found in the Indo-Gangetic Plains. Rich in potash, phosphorous, lime. Two types: Khadar (new, riverine, very fertile) and Bhangar (old, on terraces, slightly less fertile). Best for wheat, rice, sugarcane.
Deccan Plateau (Maharashtra, MP, Gujarat). Rich in iron, lime, calcium, magnesium. Retains moisture; cracks in dry season. Best for cotton — called 'black cotton soil.'
Eastern and southern India (Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Tamil Nadu). Red due to iron oxides. Porous, low fertility without irrigation/fertilisers. Best for millets, groundnuts.

key_points: Laterite soil: high rainfall areas (Western Ghats, eastern coast). High iron, low humus. Tea, coffee, cashew.
Desert soil: Rajasthan. Sandy, low moisture retention, saline. Bajra and coarse grains with irrigation.
Soil erosion: wind erosion (Rajasthan), sheet/rill/gully erosion (rainfall). Deforestation, overgrazing main causes.
Conservation: contour ploughing, terracing (step farming), afforestation, check dams, strip cropping.
Mountain soils: steep, shallow, acidic. Organic matter from forest litter. Crops: tea, fruits (apple), saffron in Kashmir.

10|geography|5_Natural_Vegetation.overview: India's natural vegetation is determined by rainfall, temperature, and altitude. Major types: tropical evergreen, tropical deciduous (monsoon), thorny scrub, montane, and mangroves. Forest cover is critical for ecology and climate.

key_concepts.definition: Rainfall > 200 cm. Western Ghats, northeast India, Andaman & Nicobar. Dense, multi-layered canopy; trees don't shed leaves simultaneously. Valuable hardwoods: mahogany, ebony, rosewood.
Most widespread. 100–200 cm rainfall. Shed leaves in dry season. Moist deciduous (teak, sal — more rainfall); Dry deciduous (100–150 cm — more open). Best timber forests of India.
Rainfall < 75 cm. Rajasthan, semi-arid areas. Acacias, cacti, thorny bushes adapted to drought (deep roots, small leaves, stored water).

key_points: Mangroves: tidal forests along coasts (Sundarbans — world's largest mangrove; W Bengal/Bangladesh). Dense, salt-tolerant, stilted roots. Protects against cyclones and erosion.
Montane forests: change with altitude — tropical → subtropical → temperate → alpine → tundra.
Forest policy: National Forest Policy (1988) — aim of 33% forest cover; protected areas (national parks, wildlife sanctuaries).
Deforestation causes: agricultural expansion, timber, urbanisation, mining. Consequences: floods, drought, soil erosion, biodiversity loss.
Social forestry: community participation in tree planting along roads, railways, degraded lands — participatory conservation.

10|geography|6_Water_Resources.overview: India has abundant water resources but their distribution is uneven. Rivers, groundwater, and rain provide water, but demand exceeds supply in many regions. Conservation, dams, and rainwater harvesting are critical strategies.

key_concepts.definition: Perennial (flow all year — fed by snow and monsoon): Ganga, Yamuna, Brahmaputra, Indus and tributaries. Have high erosion capacity and large basins.
Seasonal (fed mostly by monsoon — dry in summer): Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Kaveri, Narmada, Tapi. Older rivers — less erosion, slower flow.
Purposes: irrigation, hydroelectricity, flood control, drinking water, navigation, recreation. Examples: Bhakra-Nangal (Sutlej), Hirakud (Mahanadi), Damodar Valley.

key_points: Groundwater: ~85% of rural drinking water from groundwater. Over-extraction → falling water table.
Rainwater harvesting: traditional (bawdis, johads, tanks) and modern systems collect and store rainwater — mandatory in many cities.
Interlinking of rivers: proposed to transfer water from surplus to deficit basins — environmental concerns.
Water pollution: industrial effluents, sewage, agricultural runoff (pesticides, fertilisers) pollute rivers.
Ganga Action Plan (GAP): launched to clean the Ganga — largely unsuccessful; Namami Gange (2014) — current mission.

10|geography|7_Mineral_and_Energy_Resour.overview: India is richly endowed with minerals concentrated in the Deccan Plateau and northeastern India. Energy resources include coal, petroleum, natural gas, and growing renewable energy (solar, wind, nuclear).

key_concepts.definition: India has large reserves (4th largest globally). Main deposits: Odisha (Mayurbhanj), Jharkhand (Singhbhum), Chhattisgarh (Bastar), Karnataka (Bellary). Types: magnetite (highest quality), haematite.
India's primary energy resource. Main coalfields: Jharia, Raniganj, Bokaro, Talcher. Two types: Gondwana (most), Tertiary. Used in thermal power, iron/steel making, railways.
Mumbai High (offshore — largest), Digboi (Assam — oldest), Ankleshwar (Gujarat), Krishna-Godavari basin. Used for fuels, lubricants, petrochemicals.

key_points: Mica: India leads global production; Jharkhand, Rajasthan. Used in electrical insulation.
Manganese: Odisha, Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh. Used in steel-making.
Bauxite: Odisha, Gujarat, Jharkhand. Raw material for aluminium.
Renewable energy: India ranks high in solar (Gujarat, Rajasthan) and wind (Tamil Nadu, Gujarat) energy. National Solar Mission targets 500 GW by 2030.
Thermal power: coal-based, still dominant (>50% of electricity). Nuclear: Tarapur, Rawatbhata, Kalpakkam, Kudankulam.

10|geography|8_Agriculture.overview: Agriculture is the backbone of India's economy, employing ~50% of the workforce. India produces rice, wheat, cotton, jute, tea, and many other crops. The Green Revolution transformed productivity but created environmental challenges.

key_concepts.definition: Kharif (sown June–July, harvested Sep–Oct): rice, cotton, jute, maize, groundnut. Rabi (sown Oct–Nov, harvested Mar–Apr): wheat, barley, mustard, peas. Zaid: short summer crops between rabi and kharif.
Staple food; requires high temperature (>25°C), heavy rainfall (>150 cm) or irrigation. Major producers: West Bengal, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu.

1960s–70s: high-yielding variety (HYV) seeds, fertilisers, irrigation, mechanisation. Dramatically increased wheat and rice production. Benefits concentrated in Punjab, Haryana, western UP. Environmental issues: soil degradation, waterlogging, groundwater depletion.

key_points: Wheat: Punjab, Haryana, UP, Rajasthan. Cool winters, hot dry summers, loamy soil, irrigation.
Cotton: Maharashtra, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh. Black (regur) soil, high temperature, moderate rainfall. 'White Gold.'
Tea: Assam, West Bengal (Darjeeling), Tamil Nadu, Kerala. Acidic loamy soil, heavy rainfall, cool nights.
Jute: West Bengal, Bihar, Assam. Hot and humid climate, waterlogged land. Used for packaging.
Agriculture challenges: fragmented landholdings, dependence on monsoon, low mechanisation, inadequate credit, crop loss to pests.

10|geography|9_Manufacturing_Industries.overview: India has a diversified industrial sector. Key industries: iron and steel, textiles (cotton and jute), sugar, cement, petrochemical, and IT. Industrialisation drives economic growth and employment.

key_concepts.definition: Concentrated near coal and iron ore deposits (Chotanagpur). Major centres: Jamshedpur (Tata — private), Bhilai (Chhattisgarh — largest), Rourkela (Odisha), Durgapur (West Bengal), Bokaro. Inputs: iron ore, coking coal, limestone, manganese.
Oldest major industry. Mumbai (originally — 'Manchester of India'). Now spread to Ahmedabad, Coimbatore, Kanpur, Ludhiana. Requires raw cotton, water, humid climate, market.

India's fastest growing sector. Hubs: Bengaluru ('Silicon Valley of India'), Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune, NCR. Software exports, BPO, IT services. Advantages: English-speaking, skilled manpower, time zone.

key_points: Sugar industry: UP and Maharashtra are largest producers. Near sugarcane fields (raw material is bulky and perishable).
Cement industry: MP, Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh (near limestone deposits). Inputs: limestone, coal, gypsum.
Petrochemical industry: Mumbai-Vadodara belt. Inputs from petroleum refineries.
Jute industry: concentrated in Hooghly valley (West Bengal). Proximity to jute-growing areas, water, cheap labour.
Industrial regions: Mumbai-Pune, Bengaluru-Chennai, Chotanagpur, Hooghly, Ahmedabad-Vadodara.

10|geography|10_Transport.overview: India's transport network — railways, roads, waterways, airways — connects its vast territory. Railways are the backbone of freight and passenger transport; road networks have expanded massively under PMGSY and NHAI.

key_concepts.definition: Indian Railways: one of the largest networks in the world (~68,000 km). Gauges: Broad (most common), Metre, Narrow. Connects remote areas; key for freight. Zones: 18 zones. HQ: New Delhi.
National Highways (NH): maintained by NHAI. Golden Quadrilateral: 5846 km highway connecting Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata. State Highways, District Roads, Village Roads.
Inland waterways (rivers, canals): NW1 (Ganga: Allahabad–Haldia), NW2 (Brahmaputra), NW3 (Kerala backwaters). Coastal shipping: major ports — Mumbai, JNPT, Chennai, Kolkata.

key_points: Major ports: Mumbai (largest natural harbour), JNPT (India's busiest container port), Kolkata (Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port).
Airways: Air India (national carrier), IndiGo, SpiceJet dominate domestic. International airports: Delhi (IGI), Mumbai (CSIA), Chennai, Bengaluru.
Pipeline transport: crude oil (Salaya–Mathura), petroleum products, natural gas (HBJ pipeline — Hazira–Bijapur–Jagdishpur).
PMGSY (Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana): all-weather roads to rural areas.
Problems: poor road conditions in rural areas, gauge conversion issues in railways, waterway under-utilisation.

10|geography|11_Waste_Management.overview: India generates enormous amounts of solid, liquid, and hazardous waste. Proper waste management — reduce, reuse, recycle — is essential for environmental sustainability. The Swachh Bharat Mission has prioritised sanitation and waste collection.

key_concepts.definition: Biodegradable (organic — food, paper, garden): decomposes. Non-biodegradable (plastic, glass, metals): persists. Hazardous (medical, industrial chemicals, e-waste): needs special disposal.
Reduce: consume less; generate less waste. Reuse: use items multiple times. Recycle: convert waste into usable raw material (paper, glass, metal recycling).
Discarded electronic equipment (mobiles, computers). Contains toxic metals (lead, mercury, cadmium). India generates ~3 million tonnes/year. Formal recycling essential.

key_points: Landfills: most common disposal; should be sanitary landfills (lined, leachate collected). Unscientific dumps → groundwater contamination.
Composting: biodegradable waste → rich compost (organic fertiliser). Kitchen waste + garden waste.
Biogas: anaerobic decomposition of organic waste → methane (cooking fuel), slurry (fertiliser).
Swachh Bharat Mission (2014): open defecation free India, solid waste management, clean cities.
Medical waste: needs special treatment (incineration, autoclaving) — must not mix with regular waste.

10|english|lang_1_Composition_Essay_Writ.overview: The Class 10 ICSE composition paper requires essays of 300–350 words. Essays may be argumentative, descriptive, narrative, or discursive. Organisation, vocabulary, and grammar are the main assessment criteria.

key_concepts.definition: Introduction (hook + thesis statement) → Body paragraphs (each with one main point, supporting examples, connectives) → Conclusion (summary + final thought/recommendation).
Argumentative: argue for a position with evidence. Descriptive: create a vivid picture. Narrative: tell a story. Discursive: present multiple viewpoints on a topic.
Topic sentence → supporting detail → example → linking sentence. Each paragraph should cover exactly one idea.

key_points: Plan before writing: spend 5 minutes on a brief outline — saves time and improves structure.
Introduction: avoid starting with 'In this essay I will...' — use a statistic, quote, rhetorical question, or strong statement.
Connectives: firstly, furthermore, however, on the contrary, in conclusion — vary them to show sophisticated use.
Vocabulary: use precise words, avoid repetition; use synonyms. Avoid vague words like 'nice', 'good', 'thing'.
Word limit: 300–350 words strictly. Excess or shortage both indicate poor time/content management.

10|english|lang_2_Letter_Writing_Formal_.overview: Letter writing at Class 10 ICSE level tests both formal (to authorities, editors, institutions) and informal (to friends, relatives) letter formats. Both follow specific conventions for layout, salutation, and sign-off.

key_concepts.definition: Sender's address (top right) → Date → Receiver's address (left) → Subject → Salutation (Dear Sir/Madam) → Body (purpose, details, request) → Complimentary close (Yours faithfully) → Signature.
Letter to editor (opinion/complaint), letter to authority (application, complaint), official inquiry, resignation — each has slight format variations.
Sender's address → Date → Dear [Name] → Body (chatty, personal tone) → Yours lovingly/Yours sincerely → Signature (first name). No receiver's address.

key_points: Letter to editor: address to 'The Editor, [Newspaper Name]'; subject line essential; state the issue, give evidence, make a request or recommendation.
'Yours faithfully' — salutation 'Sir/Madam'. 'Yours sincerely' — salutation with name.
Paragraph 1: reason for writing. Para 2–3: supporting details/examples. Para 4: request/conclusion.
Informal letter: no formal subject line needed; conversational language allowed; contractions acceptable.
ICSE gives specific contexts — read carefully: complaint vs request vs enquiry each needs different tone.

10|english|lang_3_Notice_Writing_and_Ema.overview: Notices are brief formal announcements. Emails are digital communication formats. Both are tested in ICSE Class 10 and require clear, concise language in specific formats.

key_concepts.definition: Name of organisation → NOTICE (bold, centred) → Date → Title → Body (who, what, when, where, how) → Name + Designation of issuer. Box the notice for formal presentation.
From: (optional) → To: (recipient's email) → Subject: → Salutation → Body → Closing (Regards/Sincerely) → Name. Subject must be specific and concise.
Third person or impersonal; imperative/passive constructions; factual and concise; no personal pronouns ('I'); present tense for ongoing; future tense for events.

key_points: Notice word limit: ~80 words. Email: ~120 words. Do not exceed — marks deducted.
Notice title: informative and specific: 'ANNUAL SPORTS DAY' not 'SPORTS EVENT'.
Email subject line: specific: 'Request for Leave on 20 June' not 'Leave'.
Email body: formal for official communication; semi-formal for school communications; informal for friend.
Both require a closing — for notice: name and designation; for email: name and designation/role.

10|english|lang_4_Comprehension_Unseen_P.overview: The comprehension passage at Class 10 ICSE level is more complex — longer passages, more inferential questions, and vocabulary-in-context tasks. Careful reading and precise answering are key.

key_concepts.definition: Answers directly stated in the text. Locate the relevant sentence, paraphrase, and write in complete sentences.
Answers implied but not stated. Use context clues and reasoning — 'what does the author suggest/imply?'
Give meaning of a word as used in the passage — use surrounding sentences as clues. Do not give dictionary definition if context implies a different meaning.

key_points: Read questions first, then the passage — targeted reading saves time.
Answer in own words unless asked to quote. Copying whole sentences directly = poor marks.
Attitude/tone questions: look for adjectives, strong verbs, emotive language in the passage.
Title question: covers the WHOLE passage — not just one part. One or two words is rarely enough.
Write in complete sentences; don't use bullet points in answers unless specifically asked.

10|english|lang_5_Grammar_Tenses_Preposi.overview: Grammar at Class 10 ICSE requires accurate use of tenses, prepositions, articles, and conjunctions in gap-filling and sentence correction exercises. Understanding the 'why' prevents guessing.

key_concepts.definition: Look for time signals (yesterday, since, just, for, when, while, soon) to identify the correct tense. Ensure sequence of tenses in complex sentences.
Idiomatic prepositions must be memorised: in time, on time; good at, fond of, worried about, responsible for, interested in. Confusables: beside vs besides; since vs for.
Articles: context determines 'a/an' (first mention) vs 'the' (specific/second mention) vs zero article (general statements). Conjunctions: choose based on the logical relationship between clauses.

key_points: Never put two conjunctions in one sentence: 'Although he tried, but he failed' is WRONG.
Stative verbs (know, love, believe, smell, contain) — never use in -ing form.
Since (point in time); for (duration): 'I have lived here for 5 years / since 2020.'
Use 'an' before vowel sounds: an hour, an honest man, an MBA — depends on pronunciation, not spelling.
Conditional sentences: tense pattern must be consistent — Type 1: present + will; Type 2: past + would; Type 3: had + would have.

10|english|lang_6_Grammar_Active_and_Pas.overview: Voice and speech conversion are critical grammar topics at ICSE Class 10. Both require systematic application of rules — tense shifts, pronoun changes, and structural transformations.

key_concepts.definition: Object becomes subject. Subject becomes 'by' phrase (with object pronoun). Verb: 'be' in correct tense + past participle. All tense forms have a passive equivalent.
Remove quotation marks. Add reporting verb + 'that' (for statements). Shift tenses back (backshift). Change pronouns, time/place expressions.
Modal + be + V₃: 'must do it' → 'it must be done'. All common modals follow this pattern.

laws_and_formulas.name: Passive Formula
laws_and_formulas.formula: Object (of active) + am/is/are/was/were/will be/has been/had been + V₃ + by + subject (object pronoun form)

laws_and_formulas.name: Speech Tense Shift
laws_and_formulas.formula: is/are → was/were; has/have → had; will → would; can → could; may → might; must → had to

key_points: Intransitive verbs have no passive (no object): 'She slept' → no passive form.
When reporting verb is present tense, no tense backshift needed in indirect speech.
Interrogative in indirect: ask + if/whether (yes/no) or question word (wh-) + statement order (not question order).
Imperative → indirect: told/ordered/requested/advised + object + to/not to + infinitive.
Exclamatory → indirect: exclaimed (with joy/sorrow) + that + positive/superlative statement.

10|english|lang_7_Grammar_Transformation.overview: Transformation rewrites sentences in different grammatical forms without changing meaning. At Class 10 level, this includes degree conversion, simple/compound/complex conversions, and voice/speech transformations.

key_concepts.definition: Positive → Comparative → Superlative: 'No other mountain is as high as Everest' = 'Everest is higher than any other mountain' = 'Everest is the highest mountain.'
Participle phrases → finite clauses. 'Having finished work, he left.' (Simple) → 'When he had finished work, he left.' (Complex)
Two simple sentences joined with appropriate conjunction/relative pronoun to form compound or complex. Choose conjunction that accurately represents the logical relationship.

key_points: Too...to → so...that...not: 'She is too tired to work' → 'She is so tired that she cannot work.'
Enough...to → so...that: 'He is strong enough to lift it' → 'He is so strong that he can lift it.'
Inversion of conditionals (no 'if'): 'Were he here, I would be happy.' 'Had I known, I would have helped.'
Comparative → superlative: Always replace 'any other' with 'the' + superlative: 'Gold is more precious than any other metal' → 'Gold is the most precious metal.'
Compound → complex: coordinating conjunction → subordinating conjunction of same sense: 'but' → 'although/though/even though'.

10|english|lang_8_Grammar_Synonyms_Anton.overview: Vocabulary at Class 10 level includes identifying synonyms (same meaning), antonyms (opposite meaning), and homophones (same sound, different spelling/meaning). Contextual vocabulary usage is also tested.

key_concepts.definition: Words with the same or similar meaning: brave/courageous, intelligent/clever. Context matters — 'slim' and 'thin' are synonyms but 'slim' is complimentary while 'thin' can be critical.
Words of opposite meaning. Formed with prefixes (un-, in-, dis-, im-, ir-: unhappy, incomplete, disorder, impossible) or different words (hot/cold, love/hate).
Homophones: bare/bear, stationary/stationery, principle/principal. Confusables: affect/effect, accept/except, complement/compliment, council/counsel.

key_points: Context determines which synonym is appropriate: 'She gave a short speech' — 'brief' is better than 'little' as a synonym for 'short'.
Prefix rules: 'im' before m, b, p (impossible, immoral). 'ir' before r (irregular). 'il' before l (illegal). 'un' and 'in' for most others.
Effect (noun/result) vs Affect (verb/to influence): 'The rain affected the game' / 'The effect of rain was felt.'
Complement (completes) vs Compliment (praise): 'The tie complements the suit.' 'She paid me a compliment.'
Word families: know the noun, verb, adjective, adverb forms of key vocabulary — ICSE may test any form.
